I_kwDOEYAvFc5KNNLH.Title: Deprecate `--dev` mode.
I_kwDOEYAvFc5KNNLH.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5KNNLH.Body:
It's better to simply use environment variables (like `ENV=dev`)

The only difference with `--dev` mode is filter out the pages with `draft=true`, and this is something that can be easily done with (pre)processors.

I_kwDOEYAvFc6jt7F8.Title: Skipped page (file content is empty) warning is not useful for type-only TypeScript files with esbuild
I_kwDOEYAvFc6jt7F8.Author: domenic
I_kwDOEYAvFc6jt7F8.Body:
### Version

2.4.3

### Platform

Windows

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

I have a TypeScript file which just centralizes some type definitions.

Whenever I build my Lume site, I get an unhelpful warning telling me that the corresponding `.js` file is empty and will be skipped. But this should not be a warning; this is expected.

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Full repro:

**deno.json**

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "lume/": "https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.4.3/"
  },
  "tasks": {
    "lume": "echo \"import 'lume/cli.ts'\" | deno run -A -",
    "build": "deno task lume",
    "serve": "deno task lume -s"
  },
  "compilerOptions": {
    "types": [
      "lume/types.ts"
    ]
  }
}
```

**_config.ts**

```ts
import lume from "lume/mod.ts";
import esbuild from "lume/plugins/esbuild.ts";

const site = lume({
  src: "./src",
});

site.copy([".html"]);

site.use(esbuild({
  extensions: [".ts"],
  options: {
    bundle: false,
    minify: false,
    treeShaking: false,
    keepNames: false,
  },
}));

export default site;
```

**src/types.ts**

```ts
export type StringAlias = string;
```

**src/test.ts**

```ts
import { StringAlias } from "./types.ts";

const string: StringAlias = "Hello, world!";

document.body.innerHTML = string;
```

**src/index.html**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<meta charset="utf-8">
<title>Test</title>

<script type="module" src="test.js"></script>
```

### What is the expected behavior?

When running `deno run build`, I'd get something like

```
$ deno run build
Task build deno task lume
Task lume echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run -A -
Loading config file file:///C:/Users/d/OneDrive%20-%20domenic.me/Code/WIP/lume-test/_config.ts
🔥 /test.js <- /test.ts
🔥 /index.html <- /index.html
🍾 Site built into ./_site
  3 files generated in 0.03 seconds
```

### What do you see instead?

I get

```
$ deno run build
Task build deno task lume
Task lume echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run -A -
Loading config file file:///C:/Users/d/OneDrive%20-%20domenic.me/Code/WIP/lume-test/_config.ts
WARN [Lume] Skipped page /types.js (file content is empty)
🔥 /test.js <- /test.ts
🔥 /index.html <- /index.html
🍾 Site built into ./_site
  3 files generated in 0.03 seconds
```

### Additional information

- It'd be ideal if this situation could be detected and handled without me having to manually annotate the files in `_config.ts`.

- It's a little strange the message says "Skipped page" because a JavaScript file is not a page.

- /cc @bryophyta who seems to have originally implemented this warning in https://github.com/lumeland/lume/pull/221.

Comments.Body:
That's not a bug. Lume pages are not only html pages. [Learn about html pages and asset pages](https://lume.land/docs/core/concepts/#page-files). It's just the way to tell you that Lume won't save a file with no content.

I understand the issue but I don't know if it's easy to fix. It's like if you have a javascript file with all code commented (and removed by a minifier). The best way to do this is ignoring the file renaming it to `_types.ts` or in the _config.ts file adding the `site.ignore("types.ts")`.

Maybe, due it's only a file with types declarations, you can rename it to `types.d.ts`. I'm not sure if Lume ignored the `.d.ts` files but definitely it should. I can make Lume to ignore this extension by default. What do you think?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I'd never used personally-written `.d.ts` files before, and [the documentation](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/templates/module-d-ts.html) doesn't make it seem like it would work. But I just tested renaming my file to use that extension, and it seems to work well! So I think this is a pretty good solution.
Comments.Author: domenic

I_kwDOEYAvFc5hH4LT.Title: How to write client side (javascript) code ?
I_kwDOEYAvFc5hH4LT.Author: max-l
I_kwDOEYAvFc5hH4LT.Body:
From reading about components in the doc: https://lume.land/docs/core/components/

it appears that writting client side javascript is done with components.

I wrote a component in ./_components/MyComponent.jsx  (as suggested by the doc),

and it works fine as a component, i.e. it gets rendered correctly.

However, if I add a client side handler, it doesn't get fired : 

```
<button onClick={() => {
      console.log("!!!")
  }}>MyButton...</button>
```

I figured I have to tell Lume that the component uses client side react.

I tried adding this import the header : 
`
import React from "react";
`
Hopping that Lume would "discover" the use of client side react.

It did not help, client side click handler doesn't work.

Perhaps there is a config to let Lume know of the react client side dependency ?

I also added this to deno.json : 

```
  "imports": {
    "lume/": "https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.3/",
    "react/jsx-runtime": "https://esm.sh/react@18.2.0/jsx-runtime",
    "react":  "https://esm.sh/react@18.2.0"
  }

```

I also tried tu make use of useEffect:


``` 
import React,{useEffect} from "react";

export default ({}) => {
    useEffect(() => {
        console.log(res)
    },[])    
  return <button onClick={() => {
      console.log("!!!")
  }}>MyButton...</button>
}

```

And something is definitely wrong with my setup, because I get this:

```
Error: Error rendering the layout of this page
- page: /index.jsx
    at (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.3/core/renderer.ts:162:19)

Caused by TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'useEffect')
    at n.useEffect (https://esm.sh/stable/react@18.2.0/deno/react.js:2:6284)
    at default (file:///home/maxou/dev/site-altorf/altorf/python-lib/openprot_pipeline/site/_components/button.jsx:6:5)
    | 6 |     useEffect(() => {
    | ~ | ~~~~^
    | 7 |         console.log("...")

    at JsxEngine.renderSync (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.3/plugins/jsx.ts:76:9)
    at (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.3/core/component_loader.ts:111:39)
    at Object.render (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.3/core/component_loader.ts:110:32)
    at (https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.15.3/core/source.ts:542:60)
    at renderWithHooks (file:///home/maxou/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/react-dom/18.2.0/cjs/react-dom-server-legacy.browser.development.js:5661:16)

```

MDU6SXNzdWU3NzYzODkzMzE=.Title: new filters
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzYzODkzMzE=.Author: oscarotero
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzYzODkzMzE=.Body:
There are some filters that I'd like to add to lume:

- [ ] `inline`: to inline content in the html (css, javascript, svg, etc). Example:

```njk
<style>
    {{ "/css/my-styles.css" | inline | safe }}
</style>
```
This will return the content of the css file.


- [ ] `css`: The css plugin could register the css filter, so we can not only inline css but also transform it:

```njk
<style>
    {{ "/css/my-styles.css" | inline | css | safe }}
</style>
```

- [ ] `bundle`: We can do the same with the `bundler` plugin, to transform javascript/typescript.
- [ ] `svg`: We can do the same with the `svg` plugin, to transform svg.

Comments.Body: by 'bundler plugin', do you mean having a plugin to do `deno bundle`? swc, on which Deno is built, is working on implementing a port for Terser to add to this. I'm using denopack, which uses the browser version of Rollup/Terser, for bundling/minifying. Their [roadmap](https://github.com/denofn/denopack/issues/8) mentions adding 'static generation', so perhaps there's scope for working together?
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
The idea is that existing processors like css (which uses postcss) or svg (which uses svgo) can be used also as template filters, to transform inline sources.

The `bundle` processor uses the built-in `Deno.bundle()` (see here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/bundler.js).

Any other bundler like denopack can be used (it's really easy to create more plugins for Lume). I'll take a look to Denopack.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
ok. Maybe an example in the docs of how to create a plugin? There's a couple of issues with Denopack, but basically it works well. I'll look into creating a Terser plugin, which would be useful for minifying js without bundling.

I'd suggest standardising the docs to use the same terms throughout. For example, the home page talks of "transformers", but I don't think this word is used anywhere else. Does this mean both plugins and filters? https://lumeland.github.io/creating-pages/filters/ says, under `md`, "It's installed by the markdown plugin", which is correct, but at the top it says "New plugins must be registered" which I think should be 'filters'.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Yes, right. "New plugins must be registered" is a typo, it should say "New filters". I'm fixing it.
I have to complete the docs with info about extending Lume with new plugins, transformers, filters, etc.
I just added docs for loaders (https://lumeland.github.io/advanced/loaders/).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I just added some documentation for processors, engines and plugins.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: looks like deno_swc uses Terser (via esm.sh and Wasm) for minification. Seems rather a roundabout way of using Terser :-). https://github.com/swc-project/swc/pull/1302 is swc's current work on adding a Terser port. https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/6900 is Deno's issue for incorporating this in Deno bundle. denopack's using the browser version of Terser looks like the best way forward to me. A Lume plugin could easily do something like the shell script in https://github.com/terser/terser/issues/544#issuecomment-724663556.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: Have you tried https://github.com/timreichen/Bundler?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: looks interesting - something else for me to investigate. :-) Also uses Terser from esm.sh.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: AFAICS, Bundler does no tree-shaking. So I'm sceptical how much use it is for browsers. Might be ok for Deno though, where bundle size is not so important.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: Instead creating filters for every processor (css, bundle, svg etc), I finally created the `inline` processor, to insert automatically in the html the code of any resouce with the `inline` attribute. For example: ```html ``` Is converted to: ```html ```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc58GluT.Title: Lume components don't render in JSX pages as expected
I_kwDOEYAvFc58GluT.Author: cafour
I_kwDOEYAvFc58GluT.Body:
### Version 2.0.3 ### Platform Windows ### What steps will reproduce the bug? By following the button sample at https://lume.land/docs/core/components, I end up with `index.jsx` and `_components/button.vto` (see [lume-components.zip](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/files/13939605/lume-components.zip)). ### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition? Always. ### What is the expected behavior? I expect `index.jsx` to produce this `index.html`: ```html

Welcome to my site.

Comments.Body:
You're right and the documentation should clarify this. Components work well if you're using JSX components inside a text-based template engine (like JSX button inside a Vento page) or JSX components in JSX templates.

What don't work well is text-based components inside JSX template engines (like in your example, a Vento component inside a JSX page), because strings are automatically escaped in JSX.

I didn't found a good way to fix that and probably the `div` wrapper is be best solution ATM.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5RyEyX.Title: filterPages plugin
I_kwDOEYAvFc5RyEyX.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5RyEyX.Body:
A simple plugin to filter pages. It allows to ignore some pages, for example ignore `draft` pages in "prod" environment:

```ts
site.use(filterPages(
  (page) => !page.data.draft || Deno.env.get("ENV") !== "prod"
));
```

This is useful once the `dev` mode is deprecated and removed (#244).

Other option is include this functionality built-in in the Site instance, without need to import and use a plugin, example:

```ts
site.filterPages(
  (page) => !page.data.draft || Deno.env.get("ENV") !== "prod"
);
```
But not sure if this can be confused with the `site.ignore` function, very similar but it works at path level (before the page is loaded, so it can't filter by page data).

TypeError: Module not found "https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.0.2/core.ts".
    at https://deno.land/x/lume_langdata@v1.0.9/mod.ts:10:22
    at async createSite (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.0.2/cli/run.ts:50:17)
    at async build (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.0.2/cli/build.ts:31:16)
    at async Command.execute (https://deno.land/x/cliffy@v0.25.7/command/command.ts:1794:7)
    at async Command.parseCommand (https://deno.land/x/cliffy@v0.25.7/command/command.ts:1639:14)
    at async https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.0.2/cli.ts:121:3 {
  code: "ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND"
}
```


### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

This is a deterministic bug that happens each time I try upgrading from lume v1.19.4 to lume v2.0.x.

### What is the expected behavior?

`deno task serve` and  `deno task build`  must not crash.

### What do you see instead?

`deno task serve` is crashing (and so does `deno task build`):

```
Task serve deno task lume -s
Task lume echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run --unstable -A - "-s"
Loading config file file:///Users/[path to project]/_config.ts
TypeError: Module not found "https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.0.2/core.ts".
    at https://deno.land/x/lume_langdata@v1.0.9/mod.ts:10:22
    at async createSite (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.0.2/cli/run.ts:50:17)
    at async build (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.0.2/cli/build.ts:31:16)
    at async Command.execute (https://deno.land/x/cliffy@v0.25.7/command/command.ts:1794:7)
    at async Command.parseCommand (https://deno.land/x/cliffy@v0.25.7/command/command.ts:1639:14)
    at async https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.0.2/cli.ts:121:3 {
  code: "ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND"
}
```

### Additional information

Reverting back to lume v1.19.4 in `import_map.json` fixes the buggy behaviour.

Comments.Body:
In Lume 2, there's no `/core.ts` file. The types are imported from the `deno.json` file and accessible from the global `Lume` namespace (See more info here: https://lume.land/blog/posts/lume-2/#typescript-improvements).

Seems that the `lume_langdata` plugin is trying to import `https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.0.2/core.ts` ([here](https://deno.land/x/lume_langdata@v1.0.9/mod.ts?source#L10)), which is what's failing.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Thanks, that fixed it. 

Lume2 is looking fine from where I am standing, most notably Vento.

I've updated my _Lume_ plugins, hopefully other plugin developers are aware of this breaking Lume update:

- [https://deno.land/x/lume_navbardata](https://deno.land/x/lume_navbardata)
- [https://deno.land/x/lume_langdata](https://deno.land/x/lume_langdata)
- [https://deno.land/x/lume_cross_language_content](https://deno.land/x/lume_cross_language_content)
Comments.Author: doga

I_kwDOEYAvFc5OG4NF.Title: Question on renderOrder, to access data assigned from plugins
I_kwDOEYAvFc5OG4NF.Author: jrson83
I_kwDOEYAvFc5OG4NF.Body:
I'm using the [remark plugin](https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins/tree/main/remark), which now adds `vFile.data` to the markdown `pages.data`. It is working great, so I wrote another rehype plugin, to exctract an `excerpt` (the first paragraph, with max length of 140 characters), from every blog post. The `vfile.data.excerpt` gets appended to the `page.data.excerpt`, like using the `rehype-extract-toc` plugin. Now using the `.md` files layout file, I have access to the `page.data` which includes: ```js url: "/posts/blog-post-02/", excerpt: "Lorem markdownum exclamant. Saevit iussa, in parentem praenovimus gerit sceleratior ipse ima quam\nal...", toc: [ { depth: 2, value: "Praevisos ductae et poenae esse pereunt enituntur" }, { depth: 2, value: "Illis Hyadasque dederat" }, { depth: 2, value: "Adversi firmat nigra et traiectus quos" }, { depth: 2, value: "Dignior lucis multa comitavit tenet" } ], ``` In my `index.tsx` page file, for which the `excerpt` data was supposed to be, I loop through the posts: ```js export default ({ search }: SiteData) => { const posts = search.pages("type=post", "date=desc", 3); return (

Recent blog posts

{posts.map(({ data }) => (

{data.title as string}

{data.excerpt}
))}
); }; ``` Here I don't have access to the data, since like I read in the docs [render order](https://lume.land/docs/core/render-order/), the pages didn't exist before the `index` page was rendered. I tried changing the `renderOrder` from all posts inside `./posts/_data.ts`: `export const renderOrder = -1;` But after build I can see no navbar items at all, (they are defined through `menu` inside the pages), the blog posts getting displayed, but the `page.data` still not contains `excerpt` & `toc`. When I use `export const renderOrder = 1;` inside the index page, the navbar does not contain the index page, the blog posts getting displayed, but the `page.data` still not contains `excerpt` & `toc`. Here is my `_config.ts` order: ```js site .copy("assets", ".") .use(readingTime()) .use(date()) .use(slugifyUrls()) .use(remark({ remarkPlugins, rehypePlugins, })) .use(preactjsx()) .use(postcss()) ``` If you can give any advice, I would be pleased.

Comments.Body:
This is not related with `renderOrder`. The reason is the data inserted by remark plugin is not persistent. It's only available while the page is being rendered (the page file and the layouts used by the page), allowing to insert the `toc` inside the page layout.

I have to think in a way to make this data persistent, in order to be used by other pages. Meanwhile, a simple way to extract an excerpt is this https://github.com/lumeland/theme-simple-blog/blob/main/mod.ts#L24-L26, using a `<!-- more -->` comment in the post content.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> I have to think in a way to make this data persistent

That would be cool. For my readingTime plugin I figured out I had to use `site.preprocess` to make the data persistent.

> Meanwhile, a simple way to extract an excerpt is this https://github.com/lumeland/theme-simple-blog/blob/main/mod.ts#L24-L26, using a <!-- more --> comment in the post content.

Thanks I will try it out.
Comments.Author: jrson83
jrson83

Comments.Body:
What @oscarotero has suggested is a workaround and not a solution. We should have a discussion around how Lume can have an API for any data that is accessible to `search` and `paginate` that can be safely enriched by plugins.

I was thinking about introducing some sort of global store that can be referred by `search` but this is something that needs deliberation and community feedback.
Comments.Author: naiyerasif

Comments.Body:
The data object passed to the renderers is a copy of the original page data object (here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/renderer.ts#L240) to prevent issues caused by modify this object later (like here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/renderer.ts#L271-L272), specially after rendering the page again after an update.

This is the reason why data stored in this object is accessible only when the page is being rendered, but is not really stored in the `page.data` object, hence, is not present when retrieving this page with `search`.

One simple solution could be include the page in the data object, like:

```ts
let data = { ...page.data, page };
```

So plugins can store data in the `data` object, like they do now or in the `data.page.data` object (so it's persistent).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc48jNmS.Title: cli.utils.runWatch timeout is too long?
I_kwDOEYAvFc48jNmS.Author: fserb
I_kwDOEYAvFc48jNmS.Body:
Right now, there's a 500ms debounce timer on `runWatch()` that makes the rebuild happen 500ms after the last event has been seen.

I understand that the intention is to try to wait a bit before doing an unnecessary rebuild if multiple files are being saved. But there are two issues:

1. 500ms is a small eternity for fast save-rebuild-reload feedback (and there's also the actual build time).
2. `FSWatcher ASyncIterator` will do the right thing if multiple files are being detected at the same time. I.e., if we do *any* `setTimeout` it will happen **after** all the FSWatchers are done. The FSWatcher async iterator will always happen before (if it actually detects a file change).

I've tested this on my machine by editing multiple files and saving them all (with "save all" on my editor).
With a `setTimeout(callback, 0)` I still get a single reload that detects all the changes before (the 2 events for each file, etc).

No matter what the debounce time is, it probably won't be fast enough for multiple manual saves, so we are only really ready for automatic saves (i.e., save all). I understand that theoretically there may be machines that take XXms to save files, but that's probably not common (or at least not very relevant for save-rebuild?).

That said, I'd suggest moving this to 0, but I'd be happy with either a much much smaller number (less than 10ms?) or with a way to set this with a config. WDYT?

Comments.Body:
Hi, @fserb 
Yes, perhaps 500ms is too much. Maybe the native `Deno.watchFS` works better now, but previously in my computer, it returned multiple iterations with different files or the same file multiple times. So after make a change (or even without making a change but just opening a file), Lume built the site twice or more times. Perhaps it depends on the OS (in my case I have a iMac with SSD).

Anyway, the debounce can be much more smaller (100 or 200ms is enough, I guess) because we are using two watchers: one for the src folder to build the site and other for the dest folder to reload the browser so, in the worst case, the time to wait to see a change in the browser after saving is the double (for 500ms of debounce, you should wait 1000ms).

I'll change that (or if you want to work on a PR, feel free). But I won't release a new version this week because I'll be out the next 4 days.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: New version released, including this change. The debounce waits only 100ms instead 500ms.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Hi @geetsww 
This is a known limitation of Liquid. It seems that Liquid doesn't use `()` to call functions, and I'm not sure if it's even possible to pass arguments.

I'm not very familiarized with Liquid, but If you know a way to use the `search` helpers in Liquid templates, please let me know (because I was thinking of deprecate Liquid templates at some point due this).

I_kwDOEYAvFc5be0A2.Title: Pagefind plugin exited with error code 127
I_kwDOEYAvFc5be0A2.Author: shishkin

Comments.Body:
Someone reported the same issue some days ago. We think it's a bug of the last version of pagefind in the Apple M1 processors.
Try again configuring the pagefind version to the previous version: `v0.10.5`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Try again configuring the pagefind version to the previous version: v0.10.5.

Thanks @oscarotero. Setting the version to v0.10.5 worked indeed.
Comments.Author: shishkin

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero I think this is an issue on the Lume side — either in dbin and untar, from a skim? Though I'm not quite sure how it would be happening.

I'm seeing the same error as reported in this issue on my M1 machine. Running Pagefind through its `npx` wrapper is working fine for me, and downloading the release url specified in the Lume log (`https://github.com/CloudCannon/pagefind/releases/download/v0.10.6/pagefind-v0.10.6-aarch64-apple-darwin.tar.gz`) extracts fine, and the binary runs correctly.

My `_bin/pagefind` file created by Lume looks identical to the one referenced above:
```
30 mtime=1671398107.623792028
22 GNU.sparse.major=1
22 GNU.sparse.minor=0
28 GNU.sparse.name=pagefind
32 GNU.sparse.realsize=10945478
```

This looks like the tar headers of the file, in some way? I'm not super familiar with tar headers, but the `mtime` lines up with when that archive was created in the GitHub Action, and the `realsize` works out to the correct size of the decompressed Pagefind binary. So it looks like everything is downloading correctly, but the tar headers are being copied over instead of the tar contents.

I have no explanation for why a previous version of Pagefind is working, however. Nothing in the release flow changed between those two versions, and looking at the logs they both ran on the same OS version/build image, so they should have been archived the same. 

Let me know if there's anything I can help debug using my machine
Comments.Author: bglw

Comments.Body:
Okay, what I think is the case is that the Deno standard library archive functions don't support sparse tar files — and `v0.10.6` happened to archive as sparse, as that can happen by default on the system tar used in macOS.

I don't know how common it is for tar libraries to handle sparse tars, so I don't know if it's worth opening any issue against Deno for this. It might be one to look further into, though, as any GitHub action building for macOS that uses the system tar may, at any point, create a sparse archive that fails in Deno/Lume. Whether it does so seems to depend on the underlying blocks of the filesystem, so it's a tossup whether or not it will happen.

In any case, I've updated Pagefind's release flow to force GNU tar instead of bsdtar on macOS, which defaults the other way on sparse files, so they will no longer be created. That means version `v0.10.7` and onwards of Pagefind will be working again in Lume.
Comments.Author: bglw

Comments.Body:
Many many thanks @bglw for your time digging into this issue and releasing a new patch for Deno/Lume.
I'm not familiarized with how tar files work, but I guess Deno standard library should be able to handle sparse files. I'll post an issue to Deno.

Thanks again!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thanks @bglw. I can confirm that 0.10.7 works .
Comments.Author: shishkin

I_kwDOEYAvFc6za2XP.Title: lume upgrade --dev fails to parse deno.json as jsonc
I_kwDOEYAvFc6za2XP.Author: iacore
I_kwDOEYAvFc6za2XP.Body:
### Version

/

### Platform

/

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

Add an extra comma in `deno.json`, then run `deno task lume upgrade --dev`.

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

100%

### What is the expected behavior?

It should work.

### What do you see instead?

```
Task lume echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run -A - "upgrade" "--dev"
SyntaxError: Expected double-quoted property name in JSON at position 211 (line 9 column 3)
    at JSON.parse (<anonymous>)
    at https://deno.land/x/lume_init@v0.3.3/steps/load.ts:10:27
    at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:178:7)
    at async Init.run (https://deno.land/x/lume_init@v0.3.3/init.ts:115:22)
    at async default (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.5.3/cli/upgrade.ts:5:3)
    at async Command.fn (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.5.3/cli.ts:18:5)
    at async Command.execute (https://deno.land/x/cliffy@v0.25.7/command/command.ts:1794:7)
    at async Command.parseCommand (https://deno.land/x/cliffy@v0.25.7/command/command.ts:1639:14)
    at async https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.5.3/cli.ts:175:3
```

### Additional information

I believe Deno uses json5 for its config, not `JSON.parse`.

Maybe elsewhere in Lume has this bug too.

Comments.Body:
Deno allows jsonc (json with comments), but not json5 (that is a different format).

Lume can read `deno.json` and `deno.jsonc`. I guess you're using jsonc format with the json extension, right?
The load function only use jsonc if the file extension is `.jsonc` ([source](https://github.com/lumeland/init/blob/main/steps/load.ts#L17)) Maybe it should be changed to use it for all formats.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Maybe it should be changed to use it for all formats.

I see this behavior implemented now.
Comments.Author: iacore

I_kwDOEYAvFc6msVst.Title: Install all dependencies for local development
I_kwDOEYAvFc6msVst.Author: 9999years
I_kwDOEYAvFc6msVst.Body:
Hello, is there a way to install all dependencies for hacking on Lume, including for plugins? `deno install` doesn't seem to do anything. `deno task test` works, but I'm not sure how to install dependencies _without_ running tests.

Either way, this should be listed in `CONTRIBUTING.md`, as it's needed to get the project set up with the Deno language server!

Comments.Body:
For development, you can clone the lume repo locally. Then, in your project, map the lume specifier to your local copy:

```diff
{
    "imports": {
-       "lume/": "https://deno.land/x/lume@2.5.0/",
+       "lume/": "../lume/",
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5xWc9e.Title: Some images not generated when using imagick and picture plugins
I_kwDOEYAvFc5xWc9e.Author: cgwrench
I_kwDOEYAvFc5xWc9e.Body: ### Version 1.18.5 ### Platform Windows ### What steps will reproduce the bug? I'm trying to use the imagick and picture plugins and have found that these don't always process images as I'd expect. If I configure the imagick plugin with the following data: ```yaml imagick: resize: 300 format: webp ``` And include the following image in a HTML page: ```html ``` I get the image `fire.webp` generated in my `_site` directory. I would expect to get two images: `fire.webp` and `/fire-300w.avif`. If I change my imagick data to an array, for example: ```yaml imagick: - resize: 300 format: webp ``` I do get the two images that I expect. ### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition? _No response_ ### What is the expected behavior? _No response_ ### What do you see instead? I would expect to see the superset of transforms defined in the imagick and picture plugin configuration used to generate images. ### Additional information I _think_ this is because of [the handling of page data in the picture plugin](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/picture.ts#L70C1-L74): ```typescript const imagick: Transformation[] = page.data.imagick ? Array.isArray(page.data.imagick) ? page.data.imagick : [page.data.imagick] : (page.data.imagick = []); ``` When `page.data.imagick` is an object, not an array, a new array is created containing that object, but the page data is never updated with the newly created array.

I_kwDOEYAvFc5HJbHo.Title: JSX does not reload if included as import
I_kwDOEYAvFc5HJbHo.Author: RitzyMage
I_kwDOEYAvFc5HJbHo.Body:
(this may be related to [issue #135](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/135)) If JSX contains a component using the comp object (e.g. ``` export default ({comp}) =>
{comp.componentName({ prop: "value", })}
``` ), then the hot reloading works just fine. Specifically, Lume outputs ``` Changes detected: - /_components/componentName.jsx 🔥 / /index.jsx Changes sent to the browser ``` However, if included via an import (e.g. ``` import ComponentName from "./_components/componentName.jsx"; export default () =>
``` ), then the reload does not work for changes in the included component (`componentName` in this example). In fact, the server must be completely restarted to get these changes. Specifically, Lume outputs ``` Changes detected: - /_components/componentName.jsx ``` but does not output the hot reloading text.

Comments.Body:
This is a know limitation of Deno, see https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8327 ATM, there isn't a good way to implement a hot module reload system for imported modules. But you can use jsx with Lume modules. In your example, you could do this: ```jsx export default ({comp}) =>
``` This is the only way to use jsx modules supporting hot reloading in Lume. And it's even more convenient because you don't need to import the modules, they are automatically available.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Just tested on `v1.7.2` and the `<comp.componentName />` syntax does hot reload. Thanks!
Comments.Author: RitzyMage

Eta uses Denoify, a build tool to convert Node TS to Deno TS, and it should work. A JS library importing TS shouldn't cause an error, by looking at the error logs it seems like it is coming from Pug and esm.sh.

I_kwDOEYAvFc6Sparq.Title: Extra spaces after the opening dashes of the frontmatter trigger the TOC plugin to interpret them as h2
I_kwDOEYAvFc6Sparq.Author: ooker777
I_kwDOEYAvFc6Sparq.Body:
I initialize a bare project with `lume init`, then create this simple markdown file. Notice there is a space after the first 3 dashes: ```md --- key: value --- Body text ``` The result: ```html

key: value

Body text

``` If there isn't any space, then the result will be: ```html

Body text

```

Comments.Body: This bug is from Deno's `std/front-matter` package that returns the front matter as invalid due this space. I've created an issue: https://github.com/denoland/std/issues/5673
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: The bug has been fixed, I can close this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5S62In.Title: Unable to paginate generated pages
I_kwDOEYAvFc5S62In.Author: paulrobertlloyd

Comments.Body:
Yes, this is because all pages are generated in parallel, so the paginator may be rendered before the other pages even exists. You can configure the render order to ensure that the paginator is executed after the other pages are generated.
See this: https://lume.land/docs/core/render-order/
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Ah, I missed this. Works like a charm – thanks!
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd

Yes, good idea!
In fact, I'm doing something similar for other projects ([Example](https://github.com/oscarotero/Embed/releases/tag/v4.3.0)).
This is easy with github actions.

I_kwDOEYAvFc5Qjg9i.Title: Hide or de-emphasize live changes on unused data files
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Qjg9i.Author: SheetJSDev
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Qjg9i.Body:
Numbers creates a temporary file in the same folder whenever you save.  Lume shows both changes, as demonstrated in the video (repro https://docs.sheetjs.com/docs/demos/content#lume-demo)

Since all loaders must register file extensions, Lume should know which data changes will affect pages and which won't.  Ideally the other changes wouldn't be displayed or they would be shown in a different color.

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6070939/186775098-5380cf5d-1432-4fce-b99f-baf2d1d3723f.mov

MDU6SXNzdWU3NzA0NDYxNDY=.Title: Compilation of Layouts for Faster Builds
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzA0NDYxNDY=.Author: shadowtime2000
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzA0NDYxNDY=.Body: Many template engines (like Eta) support compilation of a template into a function which can make it faster. What if we allowed compilation with layouts to allow faster builds? Some template engines don't support this, so we could still keep the run every time rendering open as an option.

Comments.Body:
Yep, sure. Performance is super important.
Do you have any idea of how to implement this? If you want to contribute with a PR, that would be great.

Precompiled templates should be updated if we detect changes in the files (with `--serve`). For example, nunjuks has an internal cache that does not update so I had to clean it manually before every build https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/engines/nunjuks.js#L14
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I think I can implement this with a cache based on layout filepaths (possibly expand to just templates everywhere?) to hold the functions. Yeah, I am going to have to look into updating precompiled templates on detecting changes.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body:
Nice! Thank you.
FYI, this is the function called to perform an update after detecting changes: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/site.js#L232
This function receives an array of files that have changed, in order to be reloaded again (I know, with Typescript it would be more obvious :) and finally build the site again.
Maybe we can pass this array of files to every template engine used, so they can update its internal cache if they need to.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Now that precompilation and cache was implemented in nunjuks, eta and pug, I'm closing this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc7BzbFB.Title: JSX: `precompile` for `compilerOptions.jsx` is broken
I_kwDOEYAvFc7BzbFB.Author: satgo1546
I_kwDOEYAvFc7BzbFB.Body:
### Version 3.0.5 ### Platform Arch Linux (WSL 2); should be reproducible on other OSes ### What steps will reproduce the bug? 1. `deno run -A https://lume.land/init.ts` to initialize a new site. 2. Choose **Basic + plugins**, then select **jsx**. 3. Edit `deno.json` to use `precompile` for `compilerOptions.jsx`. ```diff "compilerOptions": { "types": [ "lume/types.ts" ], - "jsx": "react-jsx", + "jsx": "precompile", "jsxImportSource": "lume" }, ``` 4. Create a test page (`index.page.tsx`, for example). ```tsx export default () =>

test page

``` 5. `deno task lume` and inspect the output. ### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition? Always reproducible in Lume 3.0.5. ### What is the expected behavior? An HTML page with a heading. This is observed in Lume 3.0.4 and older. ```html

test page

``` ### What do you see instead? The `

` tags are escaped, so the browser renders the tags as text. ```html <h1>test page</h1> ``` ### Additional information Lume docs do not mention whether precompile is supported. #699 suggests it was supported for `jsx-preact` but not `jsx` (React) in Lume 2.x. The `ssx` package readme mentions that it's "compatible with the `precompile` option available in Deno", so I assume `precompile` is supported in Lume 3 as well.

Comments.Body:
Precompile mode is not used in Lume because it's incompatible with other plugins that use JSX under the hood, like MDX or OG Images.

But I agree that, if defined, I should work with `.page.tsx` files. I'll take a look.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I just fixed this, and will be available in the next Lume version (3.0.6).
You can use it upgrading your Lume version to the latest dev version `deno task lume upgrade --dev`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5TEDQq.Title: Include file slug value in `Page.src` object
I_kwDOEYAvFc5TEDQq.Author: paulrobertlloyd
I_kwDOEYAvFc5TEDQq.Body:
Lume does some calculations behind the scenes to determine information about the source of a generated page. For example, given a file saved at `/articles/2022-08-29_porto.md`, Lume provides the following data in `Page.src`:

```js
{
  path: "/articles/2022-08-29_porto",
  lastModified: 2022-10-01T17:46:49.762Z,
  created: 2022-10-01T17:16:39.480Z,
  remote: undefined,
  ext: ".md"
}
```

Lume will also add a `page.data.data` value if the base file name contains `YYYY-MM-DD_` (so for the above this would be calculated to be `2022-10-01T00:00:00.000Z`).

However, there is one other piece of information that may be useful to site authors: the file slug. In the case of the above, this value would be `porto`.

This value could be used in `url()` functions:

```js
export function url(page) {
  return `./example/${page.src.fileSlug}/`;
}
```

It could also be used by page (pre)processors. This is my use case, where I am currently having to calculate this value myself to generate a new URL.

(Eleventy provides a similar [`fileSlug`](https://www.11ty.dev/docs/data-eleventy-supplied/#fileslug) value).

Comments.Author: oscarotero
paulrobertlloyd
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> The `url()` functions are executed before the preprocessors.

Ah, of course. So to be clear, the only way to build a URL that includes a post count is using a preprocessor and using `page.updateDest()`, as I described above?

> A solution could be saving the `slug` property the first time it's calculated

Where would you do this? Any attempt to derive a slug from the original source file falls foil of the issue described above; I can’t seem to get a value for the original file slug if I’m using `page.updateDest()`.
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I’m still not sure this helps, and I think I know why. Because the URL is calculated before any preprocessor runs, and because, **with pretty URLs enabled, base names are considered to be `index`**, the destination value is no longer useful in this regard. For example:

```
page.src.path  = /articles/2022-03-10_my_example_post.md
basename(page.src.path) = 2022-03-10_my_example_post

page.dest.path = /articles/my_example_post/index.html
basename(page.dest.path) = index
```

So there’s currently no easy way to get the original file slug, without applying a regex to `page.src.path` to remove any dates. The same is true also true for page generators, whose base name includes the page index (i.e. `articles[2]`).
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Thanks for this, that works for now (bit better than my regex!)

I do think this value should be exposed somewhere, after all it’s a user defined piece of data, much like the date in the file name.

I wouldn’t mind this value being provided as a default value for `page.data.slug`, or even `page.src.path`, but you have a better idea of the overall architecture and project direction.
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd

Comments.Body: This will be available in Lume 1.13.0. Closing this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5Kmg08.Title: Support global templateEngine settings with images in content folders
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Kmg08.Author: Brixy
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Kmg08.Body:
I am just porting another site to Lume from Eleventy. And it works just great!

The old sites uses Eleventy’s Nunjucks shortcodes within markdown files. Lume supports this with `templateEngine: njk,md` in front matter.

Yet, I noticed an issue: Apparently you cannot use this setting globally for all blog posts in combination with images in folders, probably because images are pages in Lume:

```
# posts/_data.js
export const templateEngine = 'njk,md';
```

```
posts/
└── 2022-05-28_a-post/
    ├── index.md
    ├── file.pdf
    └── image.jpg
```

As soon as you remove the image file from the folder everything works as expected.

(Honestly speaking I have not tried Lume’s modules plugin with this combination.)

Thank you.

Comments.Body:
Mmm, I understand.

In Lume all files are "pages" but there are two types of pages: regular pages and assets pages (it's [explained here](https://lume.land/docs/core/concepts/#page-files)). Assets pages don't apply the `layout` variable (to avoid an image/css/js be wrapped in a layout), but it do apply the render. Maybe assets pages shouldn't be rendered, and only apply (pre)processors.

As a workaround, you can use a preprocessor to apply the templateEngine variable only to md files:

```js
site.preprocess([".md"], (page) => page.data.templateEngine = "njk,md");
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
This works perfectly! Thank you.

A global setting of `templateEngine` does not seem to have any disadvantages.
Comments.Author: Brixy

I_kwDOEYAvFc5S1kem.Title: Allow other ISO 8601 date representations in `date` field
I_kwDOEYAvFc5S1kem.Author: paulrobertlloyd
I_kwDOEYAvFc5S1kem.Body:
Pages can only include dates in the following formats:

* `2021-01-01`
* `2021-01-01 03:10:10`
* `2021-01-01 03:10:10 -0700`

This appears to follow RFC 3339. However, IS0 8601 dates typically don’t include spaces, but instead use `T` to separate the date and time. For example, the above can also be represented as:

* `2021-01-01`
* `2021-01-01T03:10:10Z`
* `2021-01-01Y03:10:10-0700`

Support for this representation in Lume would be useful as it would make migrating pages from an Eleventy-powered blog easier. For reference, Eleventy supports thew following [date formats](https://www.11ty.dev/docs/dates/):

* any valid [YAML date value](https://yaml.org/type/timestamp.html)
* any valid ISO 8601 string that [Luxon’s DateTime.fromISO](https://moment.github.io/luxon/#/parsing?id=iso-8601) can parse

It should be noted that Luke’s date format filters support both of the above representations (`ATOM` and `DATETIME`).

ISO 8601 dates also don’t need to include `-` or `:` separators, so the following dates are also valid:

* `20210101`
* `20210101T031010Z`
* `20210101Y031010-0700`

There may be less need to support this, although I’be come across it on my own site where I’m parsing iCalendar data which formats dates using this representation. As I can’t use them directly when generating pages, I need to manipulate them to the format Lume allows.

Comments.Body:
Deno' std modules include some datetime utils, like this parser; https://deno.land/std@0.157.0/datetime/mod.ts?s=parse but it doesn't auto-detect the format. Maybe the [date-fns](https://deno.land/x/date_fns@v2.15.0) library (a already used dependency) can handle these formats.

I like also the git related dates of Eleventy. It's something that @dz4k implemented in his site: https://github.com/dz4k/denizaksimsek.com/blob/master/_build/get-dates-from-git.ts
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc6oVJy1.Title: Add Partytown plugin
I_kwDOEYAvFc6oVJy1.Author: yeskunall
I_kwDOEYAvFc6oVJy1.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

While it doesn’t have to be installed by default like [search](https://lume.land/plugins/search/), it’d be great if Lume added Partytown to its set of core plugins. With that, one could relocate resource intensive third-party scripts off of the main thread and into a web worker. A good example would be an analytics script like [Umami](https://umami.is/) as it’s not needed for user interactivity, and only hurts [user-centric performance metrics](https://web.dev/articles/user-centric-performance-metrics).

---

[Partytown website](https://partytown.qwik.dev/)

Comments.Body:
I like the idea! I didn't know Partytown but looks promising.

Would you like to work on this? If so, I think it's better to add it into the [experimental plugins repo](https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins) to test it. And then move it to Lume repo.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Dang it. I did not know there was a dedicated repo for experimental plugins. @/kwaa has already [worked on it](https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins/blob/main/partytown/mod.ts).

It looks like their implementation will work just fine; if it doesn’t I’ll work with them to get it working.

---

My apologies for the noise; if you want you are free to close/delete this issue. 😅
Comments.Author: yeskunall

Comments.Body:
Oh, it's already in the experimental plugins repo! I forgot it 😅

The code looks fine, probably will be added to Lume main repo in the next minor or major version.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc6IhM58.Title: Lume serve: expected to respect ServerOptions for location
I_kwDOEYAvFc6IhM58.Author: Level8Broccoli
I_kwDOEYAvFc6IhM58.Body:
### Version

2.1.4

### Platform

Linux 

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

- Setup the config with a custom port (e.g. `lume({server: {port: 1234}})`)
- Using the `url()` from `Lume.Helpers` with `absolute` set to `true`
- Run `deno task lume --serve`


### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

always

### What is the expected behavior?

I am happy to be corrected if I misunderstand something, but I would have expected the output of `Lume.Helpers.url("/test", true)` to be `http://localhost:1234/test` (with the custom port `1234` defined in the config).

### What do you see instead?

It outputs `http://localhost:3000/test`, presumably because of the logic inside `lume/core/utils/cli_options.ts:getOptionsFromCli()`, where it falls back onto port 3000 when there is nothing passed by CLI.

### Additional information

I am new to Deno and Lume and am really liking it. Great job. (This is my first issue I open on a FOSS project on github, so please let me know if I can improve the issue).

Comments.Body:
Hi. Yes, this is a bug, sorry for the troube.
I can't believe I didn't catch this before, thanks for letting me know!
It will be fixed in the next release of Lume. Meanwhile, you can use the `--port` argument: `deno task lume --serve --port=1234`
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Fixed in Lume 2.2.0.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc6Vpnk_.Title: Can't use `outfile` in the ESbuild plugin configuration
I_kwDOEYAvFc6Vpnk_.Author: benjamingeets

Comments.Body:
In Lume, the files are typically loaded, transformed and saved. And the esbuild plugin does some automatic configuration to follow this rule.

For example, if you have two typescript files:

```txt
/scripts/
    one.ts
    two.ts
```

The esbuild plugin transform the two files and save them with the same name but the `.js` extension:

```txt
/scripts/
    one.js
    two.js
```

In other words: esbuild plugin generate as many files as were found in the source folder.
If you only want to generate one file, make sure that Lume only load one file. For example, you can create a `main.ts` file that imports the other modules. If you do that, make sure that Lume only load the main file, by ignoring the other modules. A way to do that is with `site.ignore()` function or simply prefixing the scripts folder with an underscore:

```txt
/main.ts
/_scripts/
    one.js
    two.js
```

```ts
// main.ts

import "./_scripts/one.ts";
import "./_scripts/two.ts";
```

In this way, the plugin only uses `main.ts` as the entry point and the rest of the dependencies are bundled in that file.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Oh, OK, thank you for this explaination. In this cas, perhaps should we remove the `outfile` property of [the ESbuild plugin doc page](https://lume.land/plugins/esbuild/)?
Comments.Author: benjamingeets

Comments.Body: yep, that's a good point. I will.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc60wttS.Title: The path to backlinks
I_kwDOEYAvFc60wttS.Author: boehs
I_kwDOEYAvFc60wttS.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Hi, I'm futzing with Lume for my [site](https://github.com/boehs/site), which already uses Vento and Eleventy. Reading the docs I had some philosophical reservations, but I suspect many of them will be alleviated once I actually get to using Lume as its a bit of a paradigm shift in many regards (and, to be clear, it already looks like there's a lot of decisions I think are huge improvements)

The one thing I can't conceivably envision a workaround for is the backlinks feature, which displays on pages the other pages that link to that page (holy word salad). In Eleventy, I do this with a disgusting computedData hack. As far as I can tell, Lume doesn't have computed data which was a big shocker, but quite honestly maybe is an improvement.

At its most basic, the backlink feature requires the following things

1. The ability to export data generated during markdown template rendering

If I wanted I could implement my own loader to do this. This would be a mild hack but tolerable. It is *possible* that, as objects are reference types, I could modify the value of `data`?

```ts
  async function render(
    content: string,
    data?: Record<string, unknown>,
    filename?: string,
  )
```

is this officially supported? can it be? What would happen is my wikilinks plugin would, for each wikilink, do `data.linksTo.push('/path/to/page')`

2. Use this data in a layout

The layout would do something like `search.pages("linksTo.includes('{{thisPageUrl}}')")` (yes this syntax is invalid but you get the idea).

A different page could specify `renderOrder` so that all data is available. This is not possible here because backlinks are displayed in the page itself, so the *layout* would need to set renderOrder. According to order of operations, "For each group of pages with the same renderOrder .... Render the page using the assigned [template engine](https://lume.land/docs/core/multiple-template-engines/) and [layout](https://lume.land/docs/creating-pages/layouts/)," so this is not possible.

A really easy path to this that occurs to me would be to internally model layouts as pages. How this would work is when lume encounters the `layout` specifier, the page queue is amended as if the layout was a page itself, and is just passed `{{ content, ...data }}` for what the value of data would be. That way, layouts would by *default* be rendered fairly close to directly after the content page, but if `renderOrder` was set higher this would be deferred for free.

---

If you see any other options for how backlinks could be done today, or other paths to lume supporting this, please let me know! I'd be happy to discuss.

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Update: [I just made a change](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/commit/2602c19f6683a9eb257288253621cc58147867a9) to render the layouts once all pages of all renderOrder are rendered and looks like it works fine! (I could even detect a bug in a plugin).

This allows to use a markdown-it plugin to collect all backlinks pages, no matter the renderOrder.

I want to test this with real projects to make sure it doesn't break anything, but it seems a good decision. If you want to test it, just upgrade Lume to the latest development version with `deno task lume upgrade --dev`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Happy to test this. How can I do so? @oscarotero
Comments.Author: RickCogley

Comments.Body:
Thanks @RickCogley!

Just upgrade Lume to the latest version `deno task lume upgrade --dev` and make sure it doesn't break any of your sites using Lume 3.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Ok I can try tomorrow. Do I add the above processor?
Comments.Author: RickCogley

Comments.Body:
@RickCogley No. They are just different options to solve the same problem (calculate backlinks).
The change I made aims to make it possible to implement it as a markdown plugin (a plugin that doesn't exist yet), but I just want to make sure this change doesn't break anything.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Woah, you’re an actual 10x developer. I did consider the processor route but I dismissed it due to the reasons you outlined, and *especially* given how close the codebase already was to a really elegant one. Super happy you agree! 

I have a large site with thousands of lines worth of configuration so I can’t test immediately but I hope I’ll be able to start and finish a first pass tonight. Will keep you posted on how it goes!
Comments.Author: boehs

Comments.Body: @oscarotero hi, I upgraded on my blog site that uses tailwind 4 (only about 25 pages), and after installing your plugin order deno lint plugin (on the new deno 2.3.1) and clearing all the order errors, I don't see any other errors related to this one. FYI.
Comments.Author: RickCogley

Comments.Body: Thank you @RickCogley !
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc479TiZ.Title: Provide system to force reload of files
I_kwDOEYAvFc479TiZ.Author: amatiasq
I_kwDOEYAvFc479TiZ.Body:
Following https://github.com/lumeland/lume/pull/134, we need a way to force lume to reload a file.

Example use case is if we know `a.ts` depends on `b.ts` whenever we receive a `beforeUpdate` event because `b.ts` has changed we want Lume to also re-evaluate `a.ts`.

We can add `a.ts` to `event.files` but that won't trigger a re-evaluation because of `Source` internal cache.

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Oh i see, you're proposing to split the Readding From Disk from execution
of the files.



We can use url parsing to evaluate the module's code but without the
sourcemaps we won't have the list of dependencies of the files. We could
use that rust library you mentioned before.



I was also considering we could check if the loaded file is outside 'src'
(but inside 'cwd()'?) and watch them too.
Comments.Author: amatiasq

Comments.Body:
> Oh i see, you're proposing to split the Readding From Disk from execution
of the files.

Yes, exactly. The same dependency can be used several times, but we only read it once.

> We can use url parsing to evaluate the module's code but without the
sourcemaps we won't have the list of dependencies of the files. We could
use that rust library you mentioned before.

I think it's better because the rust library only return the graph of dependencies (is the equivalent to run the comand `deno info a.ts`), but don't execute anything, so it's faster that bundle, create a source map, parse it and inspect.

> I was also considering we could check if the loaded file is outside 'src'
(but inside 'cwd()'?) and watch them too.

This can't be done. The watcher only see changes in the src directory. The idea is that all src files needed to build the site are in this directory. The cwd can change (you can build the same site from different cwd). Any external file (outside of this directory or a remote dependency) won't be watched.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I am just jumping into the middle of this and don't know anything, but I'm curious if doing the source maps helps with debugging? Ideally, it would be nice when having trouble to be able to step through code even in templates. I know that nunjucks is supposed to be adding source maps support at some point.
Comments.Author: ejsmith

Comments.Body:
SourceMaps were not used as such, we were using them just as a medium to
get the module's dependencies.
Comments.Author: amatiasq

Comments.Body:
@amatiasq I just [created a branch](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/tree/improved-watcher) implementing a new way to watch files, compatible with javascript modules:

- The build and watch are executed in a Worker [code here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/improved-watcher/cli/build.ts#L65).
- If at least one of the changed file is javascript or typescript, the worker is terminated, and a new one is created, so the entire site is built again with fresh content. 

This system has two drawbacks:
- On change a js/ts file, the entire site is rebuilt, so it may be slower than just update the changed file.
- There's no support for import maps in Workers [see issue](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/6675) so the `_config.ts` file cannot use bare imports.

I've added this mode under the `--rebuild` flag, so both modes are available. `lume --serve` will work as now, and `lume --serve --rebuild` enables this watch mode.

what do you think?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero running the code in a Worker is a great improvement regarding modules isolation!

I'll need time to carefully read and understand the new code so I can understand why those limitations apply, sadly I doubt I can get half as much time as last week.
Comments.Author: amatiasq

Comments.Body:
Don't worry, no rush :)

I_kwDOEYAvFc5Ob30P.Title: Invalid `url` values are not caught
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Ob30P.Author: bryophyta
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Ob30P.Body:
When specifying a `url` for a page in its yaml header, some values will fail silently. For example:

```markdown
---
# index.md

url: 0
---

# Hello world
```

This page will be built as `_site/index.md`. I believe that this is because the top-level checks that happen on the `url` value are to see if it is `false` or has `typeof string` or `typeof function`. https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/8e6bb7b112861b2699a37d89501aeb000768298f/core/renderer.ts#L175-L220

Because values like `0` are parsed as numbers, they fail these conditions (thereby bypassing the validation that happens for strings) and end up with the default `url`.[^1] My assumption is that this is not the desired behaviour?

Do you have a sense of what the desired behaviour would be, here? I'm not sure whether it would be better for it to crash, or to warn the user, or to coerce the value to a string. But if you have a view on what the desired behaviour should be, I'd be happy to try and write up a PR to implement it.

[^1]: Similarly, if an array is provided as the `url` value, this will also fail silently and the page will be built with its default url.

Comments.Body:
A quick workaround for your use case is force a string using quotes:

```md
---
# index.md

url: "0"
---

# Hello world
```

I think the behavior should be to throw an error with a message like `Invalid url value. It must be a string or a function returning a string`.

Your PR is very appreciated. Thank you!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5R70Hf.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5R70Hf.Body: It was renamed to lightningcss and parcel_css is just a temporary alias.

MDU6SXNzdWU3NzEzNTQxNTQ=.Title: Use esm.sh Nunjucks?
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzEzNTQxNTQ=.Author: shadowtime2000
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzEzNTQxNTQ=.Body: I was wondering if you will switch to use [esm.sh](https://esm.sh) for Nunjucks.

Comments.Body: +1 from me (assuming it works 😄). My experience of forks is that they are easy to create, but not at all easy to maintain (though Nunjucks is probably pretty stable and won't change very much).
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Yes, it's an option.
In fact, I'm using a fork of a fork (because the [original fork](https://github.com/denjucks/denjucks) seems like is not maintained anymore).
I did some changes to that fork, like removing some dependencies that was failing. Compare the [deps file of my fork](https://github.com/lumeland/denjucks/blob/master/src/deps.js) with the [original file](https://github.com/denjucks/denjucks/blob/master/src/deps.js), the only dependency is a Deno std library. So I feel that my fork is pretty stable.

The advantages to switch to esm.sh is that we can update anytime to use new features and improvements of the original nunjucks library. But the cons are that we are not really using the original library, but an autogenerated version that can introduce bugs and load more javascript (due dependencies and stuff).

I think that we can continue using the fork for now but keeping a eye [on the changelog of the original Nunjucks](https://github.com/mozilla/nunjucks/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md). When the new version is released, we can reconsider again if making the change worth it.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Can you use the browser version of Nunjucks? There are 2 basic issues here, right? Converting code from CJS to ESM; converting Node-specific functions like fs. The browser version should use standard js with no dependency on Node-specific functions. The current min.js has a UMD wrapper, which would need to be changed to `export default`. The best is for Nunjucks to produce ESM output, but downloading and editing the wrapper on each release should be easier than trying to maintain a fork.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: The browser version uses ajax to load the templates (You can [see the code here](https://github.com/mozilla/nunjucks/blob/master/nunjucks/src/web-loaders.js#L61)). Deno does not support XMLHttpRequest and [won't support it](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/2191) (it support `fetch`, a more modern API). And even if it were suported, it wouldn't be so efficient as using the filesystem API.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: ok. A pity. I suppose the underlying problem is that so much of the JS code out there is out-of-date. I see Nunjucks' build process is still using uglifyjs, which doesn't support ES2015, let alone more modern JS. Ideally, all this code would be updated, but that's a mammoth task.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Automatic conversors like esm.sh do not work always. For example, I had to port Pug to Deno by myself because the conversion from esm.sh conflicts with the Typescript of Eta: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/18
So the most stable/reliable solution is using a fork or port even if they are not updated so often as the original library.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5tKXHY.Title: Allow to customize the data merging from _config
I_kwDOEYAvFc5tKXHY.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5tKXHY.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

For example `site.mergeKey("keyname", "merge mode")`:

```js
site.mergeKey("site", "object");
```

It could allow functions:

```js
site.mergeKey("site", (parent, current) => ({ ...parent, ...current}));
```

And even define scope (for example, only in `/posts` folder:

```js
site.mergeKey("site", "object", "/posts");
```

MDU6SXNzdWU5MjI2MTEyOTg=.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5JBcn6.Title: Extract the Date from the directory path, not only filename
I_kwDOEYAvFc5JBcn6.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5JBcn6.Body:
Related: #197 

Currently, Lume only extracts the data from the filename (e.x: `/posts/2022-01-01_first-post.md`).
For better compatibility with Hugo, it should extract the date if it's in the directory (`/posts/2022-01-01_first-post/index.md`).

I_kwDOEYAvFc6xTUrR.Title: `@plugin "daisyui"` fails on latest tailwindcss plugin
I_kwDOEYAvFc6xTUrR.Author: kuboon
I_kwDOEYAvFc6xTUrR.Body:
### Version

v3 latest

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

```style.css
@import "tailwindcss";
@plugin "daisyui";
```

### What do you see instead?

```
TypeError: Attempted to load JSON module without specifying "type": "json" attribute in the import statement.
              const mod = await import(`npm:${id}`);
```

### Additional information

This simply fails because daisyui imports json in their `index.js`
```
> const mod = await import(`npm:daisyui`);
Uncaught TypeError: Attempted to load JSON module without specifying "type": "json" attribute in the import statement.
```

We need `with { type: 'json' }` for deno but they removed it.
https://github.com/saadeghi/daisyui/commit/24cd382bece0f072c220c51b966c56677d8ab10a

https://daisyui.com/docs/install/standalone/ is possible workaround, but currently `npm:` is hard-coded here 
https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/65a7ce4ec4572494987e9cd6758e0f160eeec2aa/plugins/tailwindcss.ts#L70

How do you think if plugin name starts with `./`, skip adding `npm:` ?

Comments.Body:
I use tailwind4 + daisyui5 with deno fresh with this unofficial plugin
https://github.com/pakornv/fresh-plugin-tailwindcss
They use postcss & `npm:@tailwindcss/postcss` to solve `@plugin`

It requires ` imports: {"daisyui": "npm:daisyui@^5.0.4",}` in deno.json but solves the problem straight forward.
Comments.Author: kuboon
oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc6YQ8Ai.Title: Using `outExtension` causes esbuild to copy original and processed file.
I_kwDOEYAvFc6YQ8Ai.Author: ghost
I_kwDOEYAvFc6YQ8Ai.Body:
### Version

2.3.2

### Platform

Windows 11 24H2 (26100.1742)

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

1. Use `outExtension` to append 'min' suffix to a minified bundled JS file:
```ts
site.use(esbuild({
  options: {
    bundle: true,
    minify: true,
    outExtension: { ".js": ".min.js" },
  },
}));
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

As long as `outExtension` is being present and used, it will cause this problem.

### What is the expected behavior?

It's supposed to process a `.js` file as usual, and then rename it to `.min.js` during processing.

### What do you see instead?

Instead, the user receives both `.min.js` and the original `.ts` file, when without `outExtension` the user only receives `.js`.

### Additional information

It would've been possible to prevent by being able to edit `outfile` but that causes the user to have an error where esbuild claims the usage of `outfile` and `outdir` cannot be utilised at the same time, despite `outdir` having never been specified by the user.

Comments.Body:
Done 13796a7fe66c007aaca6a23084233247a46235fb

It will be available in the next version (2.3.3)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5TtVuc.Title: slugify_urls replace Chinese character with empty string.
I_kwDOEYAvFc5TtVuc.Author: wangbinyq
I_kwDOEYAvFc5TtVuc.Body:
I have some tags is only chinese, and use with the simple-blog theme, it yield urls like `/archive/${tag}/`.
But the tag part is gone, so it generate same urls like `/archive//`, cause lume error.

What I want is keep the chinese character as it is or replace with something like pinyin.

@oscarotero if you want to fix this, I can make a pr.

Here is the testcase I added.
```
equals(slugify("img/中文.png"), "img/.png"); // pass
quals(slugify("img/中文.png"), "img/中文.png"); // fail
```

Comments.Body:
The `slugify_urls` default options has the `alphanumeric` option as true (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/slugify_urls.ts#L32), so it removes all non-alphanumeric characters. Try to set it to `false`.
There's also an option to replace specific characters.

But any improvement is welcome. Maybe chinese characters could be converted to pinyin if `alphanumeric` is enabled.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: If I add the funtional that tranform chinese or cjk characters to latin characters, may i need to add an option like `enablecjk`?
Comments.Author: wangbinyq

Comments.Body:
I think it should work out of the box. If we can detect cjk characteres and the `alphanumeric` option is enabled, it should make the replacement.
The current replacement implementation is this: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/slugify_urls.ts#L90 but I guess it doesn't support cjk characters.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: OK, I will try to add cjk support.
Comments.Author: wangbinyq

Any thoughts welcome, I wasn't able to create a working plugin after some hours.

I_kwDOEYAvFc6EufHb.Title: esbuild: `.ts` imports aren't transformed into `.js`
I_kwDOEYAvFc6EufHb.Author: scarf005
I_kwDOEYAvFc6EufHb.Body:
### Version

2.1.3

### Platform

linux

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

1. initialize lume project with esbuild.
```
.
├── _config.ts
├── _site
│   ├── a.js
│   └── b.js
├── a.ts
├── b.ts
└── deno.json
```

2. setup project as following:

`_config.ts`
```ts
import lume from "lume/mod.ts"
import esbuild from "lume/plugins/esbuild.ts"

const site = lume()

site.use(esbuild({
  extensions: [".tsx", ".ts"],
  options: {
    minify: false,
    keepNames: false,
    bundle: false,
    splitting: true,
  },
}))

export default site
```

`a.ts`
```ts
import { b } from "./b.ts"

console.log(b)
```
`b.ts`
```ts
export const b = 3
```

3. run `deno task build`.

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

it always happens.

### What is the expected behavior?

`.ts` extension in compiled output is converted to `.js`.

### What do you see instead?

`a.js`
```js
import { b } from "./b.ts";
console.log(b);
```

`b.js`
```js
const b = 3;
export {
  b
};
```

output extension is unchanged '`./b.ts`', thus fails to load in browser.

### Additional information

_No response_

Comments.Author: scarf005

Comments.Body:
Thanks for highlighting this.
I'll try if https://esbuild.github.io/api/#out-extension can fix that.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: After different attemps, I wasn't able to configure esbuild to make this change automatically. Seems like this decision is made by design, and the only way to change the extension is configuring the `bundle` option as true: https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/issues/3371
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@scarf005 I've been making a lot of changes in the esbuild plugin, and, among other things, I tried to fix this issue.

It's not a perfect solution but I think covers most of cases (even `npm:` and `jsr:` imports are replaced by `esm.sh`). You can take a look here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/9937f59ba9cf030b09bf01d6ad7289a1157f1e44/plugins/esbuild.ts#L342-L356

Could you test it to make sure it works fine?
Just ugrade Lume with `deno task lume upgrade --dev`.
