I_kwDOEYAvFc5H2WYm.Title: Cache utilitiy
I_kwDOEYAvFc5H2WYm.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5H2WYm.Body: Explore a way to cache some assets like images converted with imagick to improve performance. This should be abstract enough to be used not only by imagick but any other processor. It could use the `_cache` folder to store the cached files.

I_kwDOEYAvFc5TLnZY.Title: lume init: nano_jsx plugin listed instead of preact_jsx

I_kwDOEYAvFc5TELKj.Title: Imagick plugin: Option to filter transformations based on source file information
I_kwDOEYAvFc5TELKj.Author: paulrobertlloyd

Comments.Body:
Ok, I've implemented `matches` which accepts a `RegEx` or a `string` (that is used to create a RegEx instance).
Now, you can do:

```yml
imagick:
  - suffix: -summary
    matches: -main.jpg
    resizeCrop: [1200, 630]
    format: jpg
```

It's included in the coming Lume 1.12.0.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Amazing!! Thank-you so much for the quick updates. Excited to try out the next version 😄
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd

I_kwDOEYAvFc5H5QfX.Title: How to slot content inside template : Nunjucks
I_kwDOEYAvFc5H5QfX.Author: chadams
I_kwDOEYAvFc5H5QfX.Body:
Given the template ```
{{comp.Button({text:"example1"})}} {{comp.Button({text:"example2"})}}
``` What I really want is to slot content into a reusable component, like React ``` {{comp.Button({text:"example1"})}} {{comp.Button({text:"example2"})}} ``` My initial thought is to pass in like this... ``` {{comp.Container({content:[ comp.Button({text:"example1"}), comp.Button({text:"example1"}) ]})}} ``` but is starting to look messy, is there a way to put JSX components inside NJK? or somehow pass NJK blocks to a NJK component?

Comments.Body:
You can use lume components as react components. For example: ```jsx export default function Container({ comp }) { return <> } ``` And then this component can be used in other jsx component or nunjucks: ```njk
{{ comp.Container() }}
``` The `comp` argument is always available not only in the layouts but also the components. FYI, I'm planning to implement the following syntax for components in nunjucks: ```njk {% comp Container %} {{comp.Button({text:"example1"})}} {{comp.Button({text:"example2"})}} {% endcomp %} ``` So this ease the use of nested components. But it's not implemented yet.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5wpjk1.Title: Co-locating imported CSS files with the PostCSS plugin
I_kwDOEYAvFc5wpjk1.Author: cgwrench
I_kwDOEYAvFc5wpjk1.Body:
I'm getting started with Lume and trying to locate `@import`-ed CSS files in the same directory as the files that import them. I'm using the PostCSS plugin.

All the examples and documentation I've found so far place the imported files in a different directory, usually in a sub-directory of `_includes`. Is there a way to have CSS files and the files they import in the same directory?

For example, the [Lume base blog example](https://github.com/lumeland/base-blog) has a main CSS file, `styles.css`, in the root of the repository, which imports other CSS files from an `_includes/css` directory. These files are structured as follows:

```
├── _includes
│   └── css
│       ├── navbar.css
│       ├── post-list.css
│       └── …
└── styles.css
```

`styles.css` contains `@import` at-rules like:

```css
…
@import "css/reset.css";
@import "css/navbar.css";
…
```

I'd prefer to have files structured as follows:

```
└── css
    ├── navbar.css
    ├── post-list.css
    ├── …
    └── styles.css
```

And my `styles.css` would contain `@import` at-rules like:

```css
…
@import "reset.css";
@import "navbar.css";
…
```

In other words, the imports are resolved relative to the file containing the imports, not relative to the `_includes` directory.

I've managed to get this working with the following configuration:

```js
site
  .use(postcss({
    includes: "css/" // trailing '/' matters here
  }))
```

Note that the trailing `/` matters here - without it, no CSS files are created in the `_site` directory. This feels like a bit of a hack - is this the way to achieve the file structure I'm looking for, or is there a better way?

Comments.Author: oscarotero
cgwrench

MDU6SXNzdWU5Mjc3NDIyNTE=.Title: JavaScript source maps contain incorrect URL
MDU6SXNzdWU5Mjc3NDIyNTE=.Author: valtlai
MDU6SXNzdWU5Mjc3NDIyNTE=.Body:
Source maps generated by the Terser plugin have an incorrect URL `0` in the `sources` array.

A test case:

* `_config.js`:

  ```js
  import lume from "lume/mod.js";
  import terser from "lume/plugins/terser.js";
  
  const site = lume()
    .use(terser({ sourceMap: true }));
  
  export default site;
  ```

* `index.njk`:

  ```html
  <script src="/foo.js"></script>
  ```

* `foo.js`:

  ```js
  console.log("Test!");
  ```

That results the following source map:

* `foo.js.map`:

  ```json
  {"version":3,"sources":["0"],"names":["console","log"],"mappings":"AAAAA,QAAQC,IAAI","file":"/foo.js"}
  ```

  Should have `foo.js` instead of `0`.

----

**Edit**: The issue originally covered the PostCSS plugin too, but I think its source maps are correct after all.

MDU6SXNzdWU3NzcxNTEzMzM=.Title: Allow Plugin to Add JS to Build Output
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzcxNTEzMzM=.Author: shadowtime2000
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzcxNTEzMzM=.Body: This would be great for plugins for frameworks.

Comments.Body: I'm currently using afterBuild functions, but I too have thought that conceptually this sort of thing is part of the build. Scripts for zipping a file or deploying the output are clearly afterBuild processes, but building js files is much the same concept as building html.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Mmm, I'm not sure to understand.
Do you mean allow to load and bundle javascript files? This is something that can be done now with the `bundler` plugin (it uses the `Deno.bundle()` function to build the output).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Yes, I think I may have misunderstood too. Examples please.

If we're talking about bundling, `deno bundle` is very basic, and currently doesn't minify. Rollup, as used by Denopack, is very mature, does tree-shaking and code splitting, and has a large number of options, such as producing sourcemaps.
Comments.Author: probins
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
the standard convention is to have source code in `src` or `lib`, so you could ignore that and have one js file in your site folder that does `import from 'src/whatever.js'`. I'll try that out for running denopack. I think it would probably be better to be able to run a bundler on just one file, rather than everything with a `.js` extension.

I've just tried out `deno bundle`, and find it has a major issue. When you import modules and then run something, it should bundle the imported modules first and then your code, so any initialisation in the imported modules is run first. But, for some reason, it put some of the imported initialisation after my code in the bundle, so my code failed as the initialisation hadn't run. Duh!
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: @oscarotero @probins Sorry, I should have clarified on what I mean. I am talking about frontend frameworks with SSR support. What would happen is it would generate the hydratable HTML which can be used in pages, and then create a JS file which hydrates that HTML.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body:
I'm not sure if this is easy to implement. Remember that this is not a framework based SSG (unlike Gatsby, Next or Nuxt).
If it can be added easily using a plugin, I can consider it (I'm not an expert of React or Vue). Do you have any idea of how implement this?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: This should be relatively easy to implement, considering how we just need to use `site.pages.push` to create a JS file. I am just wondering if plugins like the bundling one were implemented, would they process the JS file? We would need to figure out how to add a `script` tag with the `src` set to the JS file to the HTML file.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: I'm interested in using Svelte on my site, which I recently converted to Lume. So, I may look into this sort of feature soon
Comments.Author: JordanShurmer

Comments.Body: I've created this example of react app with server side rendering using the bundler plugin: https://github.com/lumeland/react-todo
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5547zD.Title: Transform Images plugin should avoid upsizing images have smaller size than the resize options.
I_kwDOEYAvFc5547zD.Author: ngdangtu-vn
I_kwDOEYAvFc5547zD.Body:
### Version

2.0.1

### Platform

Linux/Deno

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

1. add an image worth 800 × 800
2. add another image worth 1280 × 1280
3. add resize like below:
    ```ts
    export default {
      transformImages: [
        {
          // origin
          format: 'webp',
        },
        {
          format: 'webp',
          resize: [300],
          suffix: '-xs',
        },
        {
          format: 'webp',
          resize: [900],
          suffix: '-xl',
        },
      ]
    }
    ```
5. run build script

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

always

### What is the expected behavior?

The 800 resolution image should not be upsize to 900.

### What do you see instead?

The 800 resolution image got upsize to 900.

### Additional information

I believe if we can ignore upsizing the image with smaller size can speed up the process. Moreover, there is no point to use a upsized images in web. And in case of sb really need it, we can add it as an optional feature.

Comments.Body: I think I put wrong label, maybe it should be `enhancement` :?
Comments.Author: ngdangtu-vn

Comments.Body:
Hi. Thanks!
I think this was discused somewhere and thought it was fixed with Sharp.
Will include in in Lume 2.1.0
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5lddCj.Title: 1.17.1 issue in static copy Generation
I_kwDOEYAvFc5lddCj.Author: mskian
I_kwDOEYAvFc5lddCj.Body:

I_kwDOEYAvFc5rA1R3.Title: Slugification for copied files
I_kwDOEYAvFc5rA1R3.Author: svlauer
I_kwDOEYAvFc5rA1R3.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Thank you for creating lume, I love it so far!

One thing that would be great would be a way to slugify also the names of `copy()`ed files. Because right now, the interaction of copying and slugification seems kind of bad.

Unless I am missing something (which might well be!), right now, the behavior is the following (assuming the slugify plugin is loaded):

* Suppose in my `.src` directory contains a path `with spaces and Capitals/`, which contains:
    *  a static file `with spaces and Capitals/more spaces and Capitals.bin` (with `site.copy(['.bin']`)
    *  a processed file  `with spaces and Capitals/With Spaces.md`
* I'll be getting two directories in `_site`:
    *  `with spaces and Capitals`, which contains `more spaces and Capitals.bin`
    *  `with-spaces-and-capitals`, which contains `with-spaces.html`
* Ideally, I would like to have:
    * One directory,  `with-spaces-and-capitals`, which contains `more-spaces-and-capitals.bin` and  `with-spaces.html`
* I know I can fiddle with the url of the `copy()`ed files via the second argument to `copy()`, but such a solution would be cumbersome and somewhat brittle (e.g., suppose the slugify options change).

I guess one of the reasons for the current behavior is that `copy()`ed files are not processed at all, hence the slugify plugin has no chance to intervene.

Maybe it would be an option to provide (in the slugify plugin?) a `copyAndSlugify()` method?

Or is my "ideally" above already possible, I just missed how?

If not, I'd be willing to work on a pull request for this.

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Thanks for the quick reply!

> ```js
> site.use(slugifyUrls({
>   staticFiles: true,
> });
> ```

Seems sensible - in principle, I think it would even make sense to have this option activated by default ... but that would be a breaking change. 

Thank you for the workaround, that makes things easier for me in the meantime!
Comments.Author: svlauer
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Works like a charm! 

Thank you for making this happen so quickly!

A suggestion/question: Wouldn't it make sense to change the default to `extensions: "*"` for lume 2? Like, if someone wants slugification, they probably want it for all/most things?
(I might be missing something that makes this a bad idea, and anyways, having an extra line for this in _config.ts is not a big deal, just wondering.)
Comments.Author: svlauer

Comments.Body:
Yes, good idea. I'll change it to `*` in v2.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5Tc5N7.Title: Relations plugin: Page rendering order not respected
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Tc5N7.Author: paulrobertlloyd
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Tc5N7.Body:
I’m using the Relations plugin to create relationships between different pages on my site. The following types of relationship work:

* Generated page relation to another generated page
* Markdown page relation to another markdown page

However, I am not able to get relations working from Markdown pages to generated pages. Even with the rendering order adjusted, and outputting to the console conforming that generated pages are being created first, relationships in Markdown pages don’t work, and the plugin displays a not found error (e.g. `Page not found: type=trip id=t5JD1`).

Adding logging to the plugin, `pages` that are integrated through don’t show any generated pages on the site.

From a thread on Discord:

> It looks like a bug. I didn't take into account the different rendering orders when I've created the plugin.

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @oscarotero It works *beautifully*! Thanks 😄
Comments.Author: paulrobertlloyd

Comments.Body: Awesome!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc6C9SUw.Title: Filter Pages plugin removes asset files (arguably unexpected -- documentation issue?)
I_kwDOEYAvFc6C9SUw.Author: ghost
I_kwDOEYAvFc6C9SUw.Body:
### Version

2.1.2

### Platform

linux

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

I use the [Filter pages plugin](https://lume.land/plugins/filter_pages/ ) to filter out 'draft' blog posts:

```typescript
site.use(filterPages({
  fn: (page) => {
    return page.data.draft === false && page.data.publish === true;
  }
}));
```
I am also using the [Lightning CSS plugin](https://lume.land/plugins/lightningcss/ ) which, per the docs, loads *.css files as 'assets'.

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

always.

### What is the expected behavior?

Intuitively, since the distinction between 'pages' and 'assets' seems fairly fundamental to Lume's architecture, I didn't expect it to affect 'assets'. I.e. the use of 'pages' in the plugin name suggests it won't affect assets since there is the distinction between `site.loadPages()` and `site.loadAssets()`.

### What do you see instead?

However, the plugin apparently affects all loaded files, and since the CSS files have no metadata, they fail the test and are removed.

### Additional information

Of course, this behaviour is perfectly reasonable, and it is easy to work around by checking for `asset === false` in the filter function. However, It might be a good idea to document this with an example. And perhaps consider changing this in a future major version: rename `filterPages` to `filterFiles`, or have two functions `filterPages` and `filterAssets` (though filtering assets seems an unlikely use-case)?

If the resolution is just a matter of documentation, I would be happy to provide a pull request if that is helpful for you.

Comments.Body:
Hi. 
Yes, probably it's unclear documentation. A pull request from you would be awesome, thanks!

Just clarify that the plugin has the `extensions` option, that by default is `*`. Maybe the default value should be `[".html"]` to only filter html pages.

And note that `draft` pages are ignored automatically, you don't need this plugin for that, unless something is not working fine...
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
great, sometime in the coming days I will submit a pull request.

>And note that draft pages are ignored automatically, you don't need this plugin for that,

I was porting a rather complicated filter flow from a different project ('publish' is actually a nested data structure with various fields to check), but I forgot that lume checks for draft. This might simplify things a bit, thanks!
Comments.Author: ghost

MDU6SXNzdWU3NzkyODE4NjU=.Title: Terser processor/plugin
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzkyODE4NjU=.Author: probins

Comments.Body: @probins I don't think we have to import the UMD build and use the `globalThis`, Terser exports native browser ES modules [in `/main.js/`](https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/terser@5.5.1/main.js).
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: I just tried that, but it fails trying to `import MOZ_SourceMap from "source-map";`. Could get around that with an import map, but ISTM it's simpler to use the browser version.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: @probins You could file a bug report for that, but until that is fixed we could import from [skypack](https://www.skypack.dev/view/terser), I checked it and it maps the `source-map` dependency to an ES module link. It should work, haven't tested it.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: yeah, but you're into the same issues discussed with other plugins. Using skypack, jspm or esm.sh is essentially a workaround, and you can't rely on Node code remaining compatible with Deno. Browser code OTOH should be compatible, even if using globals is not ideal.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: Ok. Do you like to create a PR with this plugin? Maybe modify the current bundler plugin to use Terser that seems like it produce better results for browsers.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
probins

Comments.Body:
To create a new page, just need to create the page and add it with `site.pages.push(page)`.
You can see an example in the `css` plugin: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/css.js#L31
Comments.Author: oscarotero
probins
oscarotero

Comments.Body: but the processors don't have access to the plugin options - `css` only works because the processor is in the same file as the plugin. Is the aim not for processors and plugins to be independent?
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: in any case, `_data` files are already used for configuration: `layout`, for example.
Comments.Author: probins
oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thinking about this a bit more, it's not just the sourcemap option that needs to be defined, it's all the terser options. In principle, these could be different for each file, but if that's what the user wants then they can use Terser directly. In a process like this, you would want the same for all files. A plugin could use sensible defaults, which users could change if they wish. The user could set this in `site.data("terserOptions")` which the plugin would have access to. If `terserOptions.sourceMap` is set, then it could create the extra files. `site` is already used in the processor, so this wouldn't be adding to the closure.
Comments.Author: probins
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I would call it 'configuration data'. :-) Have to say I don't see the difference between using data to specify whether a file should have a sourcemap, and using data to specify whether a file should be minified, as in https://lumeland.github.io/advanced/processors/

As sourcemap support comes from another module/package, alternative ways to include them would be:
* have 2 separate plugins, one with and one without
* use `import()` to fetch the sourcemap code conditionally:
````js
 if (sourceMap === true) {
  await import('sourcemapfile');
  output sourcemap
}
````
I think I'll raise an issue on Terser to ask them to create an ES-module version for use in Deno as well as browser. This implies they would have to either bundle in the source map code or import it where needed.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
I've just been looking at [the code](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/site.js#L357), and it looks like the processor function is passed the `site` instance `this`, so there's no need for the closure, and processors can be put in an independent module if wanted without any problem. I don't mind whether the Terser options are put in `site.extraData` or their own property.

One further question: is it possible to chain/pipe processors? It might be useful, for example, to take the output from `bundler` and run it through Terser.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
> it looks like the processor function is passed the site instance this, so there's no need for the closure

Yes, I've added this in the last version. It could be needed if you want to pass options, but I see that you're not convinced about that.

> One further question: is it possible to chain/pipe processors?

Yes, you can register multiple processors for the same extension, so they are executed in that order.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@probins I am testing your Terser plugin and in some cases it returns an empty content.
For example, this nunjucks file: https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/nunjucks/to-javascript.js.njk outputs the following javascript code https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/_expected/nunjucks/to-javascript.js.

But after run terser, the output file is empty. Any idea of why this happens?
Other files like these: https://github.com/lumeland/test/tree/master/scripts works fine.

A quick `console.log` in the plugin looks like is a bug with Terser library, not the plugin itself. This is the content passed to terser:

```js
const apiKey = {"bar":"foo","foo":"bar"};

const urls = [
  "/subdirectory/bar",
  "/subdirectory/foo",
  "./foo",
  "../foo",
  "https://example-site.com/subdirectory/foo",
  "https://example.com/bar/foo",
];
```
And this is the result (note that the code only has the sourceMappingURL reference.
```
{
  code: "\n//# sourceMappingURL=to-javascript.js.map",
  map: '{"version":3,"sources":[],"names":[],"mappings":"","file":"/nunjucks/to-javascript.js"}'
}
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Terser ignores code which doesn't do anything. If you add `console.log(apiKey, urls)` at the end, it will output minified code.

Or, more accurately, it leaves out unreferenced variables.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Ah, got it.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero
oscarotero

Comments.Body: ok, that makes sense. But the docs will have to be changed too. I think this should be mentioned in https://lumeland.github.io/plugins/about-plugins/
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: also, I think `sourceMap` is the better name for the option rather than `map`. `map` can mean many things, whereas `sourceMap` is specific.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Ok, I'll rename it to `sourceMap` to both.
I want to make Lume very easy to learn and use, so consistency in the API is key here. I think it depends of the type of plugin. For example, plugins that runs a processor like postcss, terser or svgo will use similar options structure. Other plugins to add support for template engines (nunjuks, eta, pug) don't need the same options (like `sourceMap`) but they should be consistent with each other.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5iaCoi.Title: includes folders should be ignored automatically
I_kwDOEYAvFc5iaCoi.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero  Documentation for SASS plugin:

"By default, the plugin is configured to look into the _includes directory to find the imported files (you can change this behaviour by editing the includes option)"

I have tried putting files under "_includes" but they are not getting picked up. I did a bit of debugging and the processor when going through pages does not include files under "_include" thus the ".scss" files are not found.

In summary, the SASS plugin includes mechanism seems broken. I am new to Lume so I cannot log a bug with confidence.
Comments.Author: patroytall

Comments.Body:
@patroytall Do you have any `.scss` file outside the `_includes` folder that imports these files? For example, let's say you have the following structure:

```
styles.scss
_includes/colors.scss
```

In your styles.scss file, you can have the following code:

```scss
@import colors.scss
```

and this import should be resolved to `_includes/colors.scss`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: OK, thank you. So yes, to get it working I added a /styles outside the "_includes". I now understand that "_includes" is for imports. Documentation is correct as is.
Comments.Author: patroytall

I_kwDOEYAvFc5PsmUw.Title: Allow to assign multiple template engines to a format.
I_kwDOEYAvFc5PsmUw.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5PsmUw.Body:
It's possible to assign multiple template engines to a page, using the `templateEngine` value. For example:

```md
---
title: Hello
templateEngine: njk,md
---

# {{ title }}
```

But it's not possible to register a format to use multiple template engines. For example:

```ts
site.engine([".njk.md"], nunjucksEngine, markdownEngine);
```

This would allows to bring a initial mdx support with:

```ts
site.engine([".mdx"], jsxEngine, mdEngine);
```

I_kwDOEYAvFc5VqXC1.Title: Unable to set absolute path for includes
I_kwDOEYAvFc5VqXC1.Author: ggodlewski

Comments.Body:
This is not a bug but the expected behavior. Everything in Lume must reside in the `src` folder.
If you want to load files from different places, you can configure remote files. For example:

```ts
site.remoteFile("_includes/layout.njk", import.meta.resolve("./theme/layout.njk"));
```

More info here: https://lume.land/docs/core/remote-files/
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero 
I really hope lume can have more freedom in path configuration.
I can't use a structure like this in lume:
```
├── _config.ts
├── deno.json
└── src
    ├── layouts
    │   ├── base.njk
    │   └── index.njk
    └── pages
        └── index.md
```
Comments.Author: shuaixr

Comments.Body: @shuaixr What's the problem with this structure? You can configure Lume to use the `src` folder.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc49qprr.Title: Built-in assets compression
I_kwDOEYAvFc49qprr.Author: x80486
I_kwDOEYAvFc49qprr.Body: It would be great if `Lume` supports this "natively". I've read that we should be able to accomplish something similar by using _processors_, but I think HTML and CSS compression/minification could be offered out-of-the box, since it's far too common, and (I would assume) a high percentage of developers would take advantage of this pretty much by default.

Comments.Body:
For CSS, it could be done with a postCSS plugin, maybe [cssnano](https://cssnano.co/). It could be enabled by default, like nesting and autoprefixer plugins.
For HTML, I have to investigate if there's any library for Deno to minify HTML. Any suggestion?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I think `cssnano` is [one of the best options](https://goalsmashers.github.io/css-minification-benchmark/) for this matter out there. On the other hand, trying to search for the HTML counterpart didn't yield anything super-useful, except for [this one](https://github.com/sno2/minifier/), but it looks very much abandoned at this time, even though the latest published version could be used to start with something, if desired.
Comments.Author: x80486
oscarotero

Comments.Body: That actually did a pretty decent job on the HTML side...but I don't see it's doing anything (in my setup) to the CSS files 😐
Comments.Author: x80486

Comments.Body: To compress css files, you need to load them first (with `site.loadAssets([".css"])`) and then, configure the `minify` plugin to accept `.css` files (see my previous example).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: The new incoming Lume version (1.5.0) will include the `parcel_css` plugin, to use [Parcel CSS](https://parceljs.org/blog/parcel-css/) minifier.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5VNqMC.Title: Modification of the translation system
I_kwDOEYAvFc5VNqMC.Author: AugustinMauroy

# how it is now

https://lume.land/plugins/multilanguage/

I_kwDOEYAvFc56Yl5p.Author: scarf005
I_kwDOEYAvFc56Yl5p.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

currently many features are packed into [`lume/mod.ts`](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/12f8dc64b08a004c273548c9a0954bd8ec137188/mod.ts#L3-L11).

MDU6SXNzdWU3NzYzODYzMDY=.Title: async filters
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzYzODYzMDY=.Author: oscarotero
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzYzODYzMDY=.Body:
Nunjucks allows to use async filters but they need to be specified on register:

`env.addFilter(name, func, [async])`

So, we need to add this argument to lume, with `site.filter(name, func, [async])` and check if it works fine.

Comments.Body: Released in `v0.10.7`
Comments.Author: oscarotero

MDU6SXNzdWU4NTI4NzI4NzY=.Title: Using `site.copy()` with glob
MDU6SXNzdWU4NTI4NzI4NzY=.Author: valtlai
MDU6SXNzdWU4NTI4NzI4NzY=.Body:
I have a directory with both `.svg` and other files. I would like to copy the files with `site.copy("./assets")`, but then the `.svg` files inside are copied without going through the plugin. If `site.copy()` would support globs, I could do `site.copy("./assets/**/*.!(svg)")` to copy all but the `.svg` files in the directory.

If this gets implemented, maybe `site.ignore()` should support globs too.

Comments.Body:
I didn't implement glob for performance reasons. Ignoring a directory is easier and faster because the [source scanner stops](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/source.js#L149) when it reaches to this directory so it doesn't continue scanning and evaluating the files inside this directory (think, for example in a node_modules folder).

Maybe, a way to do it without affecting to performance could be by separating the glob patterns and folders names in two different Map, but I haven't done much research.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
For `site.ignore()` you can just allow passing in a function like this: 

```
site.ignore((absFileName) => { // return true to keep, false to ignore })
```

This way you can support almost any rule.
Comments.Author: shah

Comments.Body: Implemented functions support to `site.ignore()`. I'm closing this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5NrQrz.Title: Generator plugin
I_kwDOEYAvFc5NrQrz.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5NrQrz.Body:
A plugin that inject the `lume` global variable with the lume version and other environment variables that can be used in the page, for example to create a `<meta name="generator">` tag.

"Inspired" by https://www.11ty.dev/docs/data-eleventy-supplied/#eleventy-variable

Comments.Body: I don't think this needs a separate plugin. Just expose a global `lume.version` data property. This also makes room for other build metadata, such as `site.options.dev` (as `lume.dev`), or `lume.mode` to represent whether lume is in build, serve, or watch mode.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body: It could be added to the "metas" plugin, for easy usage.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body:
> It could be added to the "metas" plugin, for easy usage.

Good idea!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I just added the `generator` property to the `metas` plugin. It can be `true` (so it's generated automatically) or a string (to customize the value).

I'm not going to add other values for now.
BTW, the `dev` mode will be deprecated soon: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/201.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Gotcha. I do think it would be useful to expose the Lume version as a global variable.
Comments.Author: binyamin
oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc60TW70.Title: Search results are not properly sorted if pages lack key fields
I_kwDOEYAvFc60TW70.Author: satgo1546
I_kwDOEYAvFc60TW70.Body:
### Version

2.5.3

### Platform

deno 2.2.7 (stable, release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu), should also reproduce on other platforms

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

Create the following files in a newly set up Lume site, then run `lume`.

`a.md`:

```md
---
title: A
---

Page A
```

`b.md`:

```md
---
---

Page B
```

`index.vto`:

```md
---
title: Z
---

{{ for post of search.pages("", "title") }}
{{ post.basename }}
{{ /for }}
```

We request the pages to be sorted by `title`, but Page B lacks a `title` field. The comparator function ([created in `buildSort` in core/searcher.ts](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/v2.5.3/core/searcher.ts#L418)) tries to compare `undefined` with other strings. `undefined` gets converted to `NaN` and compared numerically with other strings, returning false on all comparisons. This violates the properties of comparator functions and causes undefined behavior in `Array#sort`.

The bug also affects the nav plugin, where directories without an index page are represented as `{ basename }` objects, lacking every field other than `basename`. It is therefore not currently possible to sort a nav menu properly by a custom field if pages skip levels.

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

The bug is deterministically reproduced, but the precise result may depend on a lot of factors, including V8 version and filesystem state. Try changing the filenames and title fields, adding more files, etc., if the above example does not reproduce the bug.

### What is the expected behavior?

Possibilities:

- Throw an error if unsortable values exist.
- Force `undefined` to be sorted first or last.

### What do you see instead?

Pages are not sorted by `title`. In my case they are ordered as `index b a`.

### Additional information

_No response_

Comments.Body:
Thank you!
I'm working on the release of the new major version Lume 3 and will include a patch for this. Maybe a patch for Lume 2 can be released but I'm not sure yet.

As a temporary workaround, you can create a preprocessor to create the title variable in those pages that don't have it. For example:

```js
site.preprocess([".html"], (pages) => pages.forEach((page) => page.title ??= ""));
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I just made a change to ensure the field always exist using an empty string if the value is missing( `data[key] ?? ""`)

The fix will be published in Lume 3. If you want to try it, just run `deno task lume upgrade --dev`). Note that if your site is built with Lume 2, you will need to make some additional changes
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thanks for the quick fix! I can confirm that the latest commit works.
Comments.Author: satgo1546

I_kwDOEYAvFc5yeUVD.Title: A separated folder for content only like `_inc` folder
I_kwDOEYAvFc5yeUVD.Author: ngdangtu-vn
I_kwDOEYAvFc5yeUVD.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Right now, content has been mixed up with logic & templates. While it has some advantage to make the content even more flexible. I still need content (and data) in a separated folder. With this feature being implemented, we can gain benefits:

1. Ability to use repo module thanks to content can be keep in a separated folder
2. From point 1, it make the content easily to be backup.
3. Other Static CMSes, for instance [decapcms.org](https://decapcms.org), can easily support without breaking anything in the logic or template.

Is it possible to do it without mess up the core?

Comments.Body:
I think Lume does the opposite: all templates, layouts, etc are in one folder (`_includes`) and the site content is out of this folder.

I can think of different ways to do that:
- Use themes. You can load any not-content file remotely, so you only have content files in the repo. The `simple_blog` theme does that: https://github.com/lumeland/theme-simple-blog/blob/main/mod.ts#L12-L48
- Use symlinks. In Lume you can create symbolic links in the src folder to other files/folders outside this folder.
- Save all your content in a folder (like `/content/`). All pages will have the url `/content/...`. Then you can create a function to remove this part (`(page) => page.data.url === page.data.url.replace("/content/", "/")`)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
About the theme, do you think it can work with Modules template engine? Because I use it a lot.

I think I will combine symlinks and rewrite urls options. It is possible for lume to read content from symlinks right? I plan to put content outside the `_src` and link it to `_src/content`.
Comments.Author: ngdangtu-vn

Comments.Body:
> About the theme, do you think it can work with Modules template engine? Because I use it a lot.

It works for layouts (`layout: mytemplate.tmpl.js`), but not for direct imports (for example, `import foo from "./foo.ts"). although you can use import maps for that.

>  It is possible for lume to read content from symlinks right?

Yes, it was [added on version 1.18.0](https://lume.land/blog/posts/lume-1.18.0-release-notes/#symlinks-support).

BTW, In Lume 2.0 it will be possible to omit a folder name. For example, if a file is in `/content/articles/article-name.md`, you can configure the folder `content` to omit its name when generating the final urls. So the url will be `/articles/article-name/` (without the`/content` part).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Implemented in Lume 2 with the new `basename` variable.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5vXTpm.Title: Add a `.serve()` function to the site value
I_kwDOEYAvFc5vXTpm.Author: leighmcculloch

Comments.Body:
Lume local server uses the [`Server` class](https://lume.land/docs/core/server/) with some middlewares to handle 404 responses, prevent browser cache, watch the file changes and live reload.

Including all this logic inside the `Site` class would make the class more complicated, with too much responsabilities.

You can instantiate the Server class and add the [following middlewares](https://lume.land/docs/core/server/) to recreate the same server provided by Lume.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5n1zAI.Title: Cannot copy one file to multiple destinations
I_kwDOEYAvFc5n1zAI.Author: not-my-profile
I_kwDOEYAvFc5n1zAI.Body:
The `Site.copy` API is currently quite the footgun:

* You cannot copy one file to multiple locations.

   ```
   site.copy("foo", "bar1")
   site.copy("foo", "bar2")
   ```

   `foo` is only copied to `bar2`.

* You cannot copy a directory and one file in the directory elsewhere.

   ```
   site.copy("static")
   site.copy("static/foo", "bar")
   ```

   `static/foo` is not copied to `bar`.

I would consider both of these to be bugs.

Comments.Body:
Ok, I just saw your pull request (many thanks!) but I'd like to continue the conversation here about how is supposed to work and leave the PR for implementation details.

Is there any real use case in which you want to duplicate a file and save it in multiple destinations?

The most common use case is:

- I want to copy all files in the directory`/statics` to the destination root: `site.copy("statics", ".")`.
- But the files in `statics/favicons` should be in the root, so I copy them with `site.copy("statics/favicons", ".")`.

What you're proposing is the destination directory had the favicons in two different places: `/` and `/favicons` (one per each `copy`). I think the correct behavior is to have only once, because one copy overrides the other.

Lume doesn't handle this well, because once the `static` folder is found, it copies all its content to the destination folder without checking other cases inside this folder (like the `favicons` folder exception).

It's perfectly valid that the same file matches different `copy()` conditions. But this shouldn't output multiple copies of the same file, it should choose the most specific condition.

For example, I want to copy all `.jpg` files:

```js
site.copy([".jpg"]);
```

But I want to copy a specific jpg file to a specific destination:

```js
site.copy("/cover/image.jpg", "/img/cover/image.jpg");
```

The second condition is more specific than the first, so the image should be copied once to `img/cover/image.jpg`

To me, the priority is the following (ordered from less specific to most specific):

- Copy by extension is the less specific (`site.copy([".jpg"])`)
- Copy by folder or filename is more specific (`site.copy("/img/cover/image.jpg")`).
  - Subfolders are more specific than parent folders. `/img/cover` is more specific than `/img` and `/img/cover/image.jpg` is more specific than `/img/cover`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Is there any real use case in which you want to duplicate a file and save it in multiple destinations?

You could use Lume to generate multiple sites.

I agree that the "copy by extension" mechanism should have a lower priority than the "copy a specific path" mechanism. However I seriously don't think that we should do anything fancy about the "copy a specific path" mechanism because it breaks user expectations.

When it comes to an API the code should do what the code says literally. If a method is called `copy`, calling it should always attempt to copy. By that logic I'd also rename `copyRemainingFiles` to e.g. `setImplicitCopyDecider` and deprecate `copy(from: string[], ...)` in favor of something like:

```ts
site.setImplicitCopyDecider(
  (path: string, ext: string) => [".jpg", ".gif", ".png"].includes(ext),
);
```

This also makes it much easier to explain the behavior to the user: With the `copy` function you can perform an explicit copy operation. Files that aren't explicitly copied can still be copied by defining an implicit copy decider function.
Comments.Author: not-my-profile

Comments.Body:
> You could use Lume to generate multiple sites.

I don't get this. You cannot build multiple sites from a single `deno task build`. But you want to output different `deno task build` to the same folder with the `emptyDest` option set to `false`.

`copyRemainingFiles` is not the same as `copy(from: string[])`. Copying has priority over loading. For example, Lume loads by default all `.md` files (to parse and generate html pages). If you set `copy([".md"])`, `.md` files won't be loaded and only will be copied because Lume checks if a file must be copied before loading it. `copyRemainingFiles` is used only if the file is not copied nor loaded.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5bJvOt.Title: Improve choosing plugins/dependencies in init script
I_kwDOEYAvFc5bJvOt.Author: tristanisham
I_kwDOEYAvFc5bJvOt.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Hello all,

The most important interaction a new user has with a tool like Lume is its init script. A comprehendible, quick, and instructive experience can mean the difference between a lifelong user and a permanent dismissal. My first time experience using Lume was not the former. 

I’d like to update the initialization process to make creating a new Lume site simple. To do this, it’s like to add:

- A dynamic TUI where users can choose as many plugins as they want by arrowing through a list and toggling any with “space”
- A check to determine if any plug-in dependencies are missing and a prompt to automatically add and order them. 
- Instructions, informing the user to run `deno task serve` to initialize their project. 
- General error message cleanup and the addition of suggestions on how to fix errors when they occur. 

I hope you find these suggestions well, and hopefully can expect a pull request in the coming weeks.

Comments.Body:
Hey!
Thanks for your suggestions. Yes, an improved initialization is always welcome!
I'm ussing cliffy.io to build the command line interface, and it has a prompt functionality (https://cliffy.io/docs@v0.25.7/prompt) which is a bit better to the default prompt provided by Deno, so maybe it would be nice to use it.

Other instructions like `deno task serve` are good ideas. Feel free to create pull request, they are welcome! I just ask you to don't include all things in the same PR. One PR per feature is much better.

Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Awesome! Thanks for the quick response. I'll start working on adding some stuff soon! I appreciate you mentioning Cliffy. I hadn't heard of it before. I'll reply to this with some of the models I have and submit a PR when it's ready.
Comments.Author: tristanisham

Comments.Body: #374 Is the pull request for this issue, updating the plugin chooser in the cli.
Comments.Author: tristanisham

Comments.Body:
- [x] A dynamic TUI where users can choose as many plugins as they want by arrowing through a list and toggling any with “space”
- [ ] A check to determine if any plug-in dependencies are missing and a prompt to automatically add and order them.
- [ ] Instructions, informing the user to run deno task serve to initialize their project.
- [ ] General error message cleanup and the addition of suggestions on how to fix errors when they occur.

I_kwDOEYAvFc6mh-22.Author: sagikazarmark

Comments.Body:
By specs, `@import` must be before all other rules in the CSS file ([more info](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/@import#description))

The [postcss-import](https://github.com/postcss/postcss-import) plugin used to inline the CSS files follows the specs. I think the plugin logs in the console an error when a `@import` not following the specs is found.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Ah, my bad then. I can't see any logs, though.
Comments.Author: sagikazarmark

Comments.Body: Closing as it works as intended.

MDU6SXNzdWU5ODE2MzYyNjg=.Title: Issue with dot in file name that is not an extension
MDU6SXNzdWU5ODE2MzYyNjg=.Author: kevinlang
MDU6SXNzdWU5ODE2MzYyNjg=.Body:
If I have a markdown post named `blah.6.md`, it will generate an HTML file as usual, but the out path will be `_site/blah.6`. 

If I change it to `blah-6.md`, it will correctly generate the HTML file at `_site/blah-6/index.html`

Comments.Body:
This is the expected behavior. If the filename has a sub-extension, it's used to generate the output filename, instead of html. For example, `styles.css.njk` is a nunjucks file that generates a css file (`styles.css`).

If you want to avoid this, use the `url` variable in the front matter:

```md
---
url: /blah.6/
---
```

If you want to apply this automatically to all files in this directory, create a `_data.js` file with the following code:

```js
export function url(page) {
    return `${page.src.path}/`;
}
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5hI_Qr.Title: Question: how to setup a proxy server (in development mode)
I_kwDOEYAvFc5hI_Qr.Author: max-l
I_kwDOEYAvFc5hI_Qr.Body:
I'm developing a static site that needs to make client side rest call to another site, 
In production, the site will be served by nginx, and it will have the proper CORS header.

In dev mode, I would like the lume server to setup CORS correctly,
and I would also like to intercept calls to the dev server.


The doc shows how to create the lume server: 
 https://lume.land/docs/core/server/

I tried it (the first example), but when started, it only serves static pages,
I need it to do the same as "deno task lume -s", but with more configurability.

Comments.Author: oscarotero
max-l

Comments.Body:
I'm not an expert of CORs configuration, but I assume you want the local server (started with `deno task lume -s`) to send the responses with some additional HTTP headers, right?

What's exactly the problem?
- The middleware doesn't add any additional header?
- Or the middleware add some headers but that isn't enought to have CORS configurated propertly?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
The headers don't get added (I don't see them in the chrome console).

The logic is quite simple, here's how it's done with other servers:  https://supabase.com/docs/guides/functions/cors

or with nginx:  https://serverfault.com/questions/162429/how-do-i-add-access-control-allow-origin-in-nginx

Being a deno begginer (as well as a lume beginner), I'm strugling to implement this in Lume. I could perhaps try to first implemente it in "plain deno", probably with a middleware, 

Do Lume middleware functions have the same function signature and work the same way, as  deno middleware functions ?

In other words, can a "plain deno server" middleware just be "dropped in" a Lume development server config ? 

That would certainly make things easyer, then the problem of "how to setup CORS in Lume" become "how to setup CORS in Deno", and I ask the question to a wider audience.

Meanwhile (while I figure this out), a nice work around would be to just add a route to the development server, so I can serve certain requests from the lume dev server, and proxy the request.

In general, it would be nice to be able to add custom routes in the dev server.

Thanks a lot for the help BTW, I like the minimalism of Lume (and Deno). I've been using gatsby for years, it is also quite minimalist, but it's become too bloated to my taste.
Comments.Author: max-l
oscarotero

Comments.Body: @max-l I just released a new version of Lume that fixes this bug. So after upgrading to v1.16.0, it should work fine. Let me know if don't.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thanks !
Comments.Author: max-l

MDU6SXNzdWU3OTU2NzEwODA=.Title: Refactor CLI to use "commands"
MDU6SXNzdWU3OTU2NzEwODA=.Author: JordanShurmer
MDU6SXNzdWU3OTU2NzEwODA=.Body:
First, this project is great. Keep up the good work.

This is a proposal for a redesign of the CLI interface. I'd be happy to put in a PR for if it seems like a good thing to do.

# A Proposal

Redesign the CLI interface to use "commands" instead of options.

```
$ lume --help

  🔥lume v0.14.0
  A static site generator for Deno

  Docs:

    https://lumeland.github.io

  Usage:

    lume [OPTIONS] COMMAND

  Options:

    -h, --help     - Show this help.
    -V, --version  - Show the version number for this program.

  Commands:

    build [config]  - build the site
    init            - create a _config.js file for a new site
    serve           - Build the site and start a web server that refreshes automatically for every change
    upgrade         - Upgrade local lume install to the latest version
    update          - Update the lume version imported in _config.js to the latest
```

Each command would have its own `--help` output as well, specifying the relevant options for that command

---

If this looks like a good direction for **lume** I can get a PR ready sometime this week

Comments.Body: I really like this idea! 11ty is a lot simpler than Lume, and Lume is a lot more pluggable and flexible and configurable so I think it is fit that we adopt a more "powerful" CLI system. I think we could use [yargs](https://yargs.js.org) for this.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body:
I agree this would be better but, f you're going to do this, I think `lume COMMAND [OPTIONS]` would be more logical. The options only apply to some commands, and the help should make this clear. There is also the `run` command, and `dev`, though I'm not sure what effect this has.

I prefer `-v` to `-V` (and I wish there was consistency in this across all scripts/systems :-( ). I also think it would be useful if this command (or is it an option?) let the user know if a later version is available. Creating a separate `update` command raises another issue. The configuration docs use `import lume from "https://deno.land/x/lume/mod.js";`, i.e. always use the latest version. This means that if you have installed `lume` and you run it when a newer version is available, it uses the new version, but the installed script is not updated. So, `-v` gives you a different version from what is actually used. This needs redesigning, I think.

And one more thing: I think `build` should remain the default command.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Hi.
Thank you very much to bring this up. Casually, I have been working on a similar change a couple of days ago, in a different branch (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/feature/cli-refactor/cli/help.js). I like the idea of having a `--help` option for every command, I don't know if yargs can do that automatically.

I also agree that `build` should be the default command, so running `lume` or `lume build` are equivalent. To me, `--serve` is an option of `build`.

And about the `update`, this is an undocument command that I'm testing because Deno caches the modules forever, so no matter if you install the latest version of lume, if your `_config.js` file imports lume without version, it will use the cached version. Until now, I've fixed it by running [`deno cache` here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/cli.js#L111) but this should be used also with all plugins that are not loaded by default (`postcss`, `terser`, etc), so it's not the ideal situation. Maybe `update` is not the most apropiate name, and `fix-version` or something like that, is better. In addition to that, I found that deno checks always the types before runing:

```
lume
Check https://deno.land/x/lume/cli.js
Check file:///Users/oscarotero/my-site/_config.js
...
```

And using fixed versions, this check is done only the first time, then it's cached, so it's a lot faster.

If you want to contribute to the work done in this branch, PR are welcome.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Awesome. Thanks for the quick feedback. Good to see work already being done on this front @oscarotero.

I will check out that branch and contribute where I can.
Comments.Author: JordanShurmer

Comments.Body:
> if your `_config.js` file imports lume without version, it will use the cached version

I just retested this and you're right. A couple of weeks ago, I ran `lume` (the previously upgraded version, no longer the latest) and it fetched the latest version. I thought this was caused by the config file, but it must have been something else. So what's the use case for having both update and upgrade? Both the config file and the cli are importing `mod.js`, so these should surely always use the same version.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: @probins Yes, correct. It's a way to ensure that cli.js and mod.js and plugins are using the same version. This is also critical because all these modules [share the same cache system](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/mod.js#L16). And if they use different versions, they use also different caches.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
an alternative is to have not a config file but a build file. I just created a copy of `_config.js` with `site.build()` at the end instead of `export default site`. Then `deno run -A --unstable ./_build.js`. Seems to produce the same result. I can set the version in the import of `mod.js`, and if I want to use a new or other version of lume, I just change the import in the build file.

It's quite common in Node to have a JS API version as well as cli, and this is similar. It removes the need for an installed script, and the problem of updating it.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
The cli api has also `lume --serve` that not only runs a build but also watch changes and update the site accordingly. Or `lume --run` to run a script registered in the site. So this is why `_config.js` export the site instance, in order to perform different commands with the same site instance.

Btw, you don't need to copy `_config.js`, just import it with `import site from "./_config.js"`.

Anyway, why do you think this is an alternative for the caching issue?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: if I no longer use the installed script, I don't have to upgrade it. I specify directly in the build script which version I want to use, so the dependencies should all be loaded from that version. If I also define the same version for the plugin imports I'm using, any cache problems should go away, right?
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Yes, correct. In fact, Deno recommends to use always versioned imports (I read it, don't remember where). This is what `lume --update` does, normalize the imported lume modules in the _config.js file to the same version (it uses the same version of cli but is not required).

Because the problem is not that the installed version (cli.js) is a different version as the imported modules in _config.js, because the cli code only runs the site instance returned by _config.js. The problem is when the _config.js imports several lume modules from different versions (for example: `mod.js` is v0.8.1 and `plugins/terser.js` is v0.9.0.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
but that's precisely my point: if both the _config.js and the cli define the version no, and the 2 are updated by 2 separate commands, then there is always the chance they will be out of sync. It's also more work for the user. Perhaps `upgrade` could always run `update`.

Anyway, I'm changing all my configs to include `site.build()`, and will see if I encounter any problems. I'm also using this kind of process in other Deno systems I'm using, as ISTM it's simpler than maintaining installed scripts.

I think this issue can be closed.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Yes, if you don't need any extra functionality that cli provides (like run scripts, watch, server and live-reload features), it's perfectly fine don't install it.

The cli interface is similar to what other node libraries provide in a different package ([postcss-cli](https://github.com/postcss/postcss-cli), [webpack-cli](https://github.com/webpack/webpack-cli), etc). But in Deno, thanks to the ES module, we don't need to create different packages, because if you don't use it, this code won't be downloaded.

> Perhaps upgrade could always run update.

Maybe, but `update` updates the current _config.js file and you can have several projects with lume.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Closed by #34
Comments.Author: JordanShurmer

I_kwDOEYAvFc5lR1kk.Title: Add option for specifying the init directory
I_kwDOEYAvFc5lR1kk.Author: echoptic

I was surprised when i saw that i cant specify the init directory by adding the directory name after the init script like this:

Why the directory must be empty? `lume init` should work like `npm init`, `git init` or any other init script. You can use it to convert your existing site (built with other tool) to Lume. The only thing it does is to generate a couple of new files (`_config.ts` and `deno.json`).

I think you are thinking of `git clone` that requires an empty folder because there can be conflicts. But `init` is not the same.

I_kwDOEYAvFc5w50A1.Title: Page with `url: false` not retured by `search.pages`
I_kwDOEYAvFc5w50A1.Author: hannobraun
I_kwDOEYAvFc5w50A1.Body:
### Version

1.18.5

### Platform

Linux

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

1. Search for pages by tag in a JSX template (e.g. `search.pages("update")`).
2. Confirm that this works as expected.
3. Set `url: false` for those pages.

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Reproduces every time.

### What is the expected behavior?

I expect the behavior of `search.pages` to not change, as [the documentation states](https://lume.land/docs/creating-pages/urls/#setting-url-to-false) that "\[the page\] is still visible by other pages, for example, in paginations"

### What do you see instead?

The same call to `search.pages` that previously worked as expected now returns an empty array.

### Additional information

I did some digging around the code. The `Searcher` [gets a reference to `this.pages` in the `Site` constructor](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/b4b12ce0c11ff64d52703566463575bf6e40f415/core/site.ts#L190), then `this.pages` is [later modified to remove pages with `url === false`](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/b4b12ce0c11ff64d52703566463575bf6e40f415/core/site.ts#L642-L648). This looks like it would also modify the array that the `Searcher` got. Maybe the `Searcher` needs a copy of that array, not a reference to it? (Sorry if this analysis is wrong. My JavaScript skills are very rusty, and my TypeScript skills very new.)

Comments.Body:
I think the issue is in the documentation. Probably this is the old behavior and at some point this was changed but the documentation wasn't updated.

Reimplement this behavior is complicated, because `search.pages()` only returns HTML pages, Lume consider a page is HTML if the url contains the `.html` extension (no matter if it's normal URL `/foo.html` or pretty URL `/foo/index.html`). But pages with url as `false` cannot be considered HTML pages, so they wouldn't be returned by `search.pages()` even if they weren't removed from the `site.pages` array.

Why do you need this? Maybe I can help you with a workaround.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Ah, that makes sense. Thank you for the explanation!

> Why do you need this? Maybe I can help you with a workaround.

I'm [building a page](https://www.hannobraun.com/) with article-like pages that I want to keep updated. For each update, I want to display some information (date of the update, link to the page that was updated, and a short description of the update) in several places:

- A "latest updates" list on the home page (already implemented)
- An RSS feed (currently working on it)
- A changelog on each updated page

Storing these updates as pages works well, as that gives me access via `search.pages` where I need it (except in the feed; might open another issue about that later :smile:) and allows me to store all relevant information as Markdown + front matter.

However, I don't actually want a page per update, hence `url: false`. My current workaround is to have a template for these updates that displays a 404-like page instead.

This works well enough, but it's not exactly pretty. And a "page" is the wrong abstraction to use anyway, since I don't actually want a page. I did it because I'm not aware of any better abstractions within Lume. But now that I think about it, I can just store those updates as regular files, load them using Deno APIs, and circumvent Lume completely. That's probably the better solution.
Comments.Author: hannobraun
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Oh, nice, thank you!

So many useful plugins. Hard to keep them all straight :smile:
Comments.Author: hannobraun

I_kwDOEYAvFc5TlBQb.Title: Date value in remote pages.
I_kwDOEYAvFc5TlBQb.Author: oscarotero

The pages loaded remotely (with `site.remoteFile()`) don't have the date value (https://github.com/lumeland/theme-simple-blog/pull/5)

Use the current date as fallback.

MDU6SXNzdWU4NzQwODQwNjA=.Title: njk filter returns empty if there's another async filter
MDU6SXNzdWU4NzQwODQwNjA=.Author: fserb
MDU6SXNzdWU4NzQwODQwNjA=.Body:
here's the case.
If you have a config: `site.filter('x', async () => "hello", true);`
and a page A that uses `{{ x }}`.

If you need to render content from page A into page B: `{{ A.page.content }}`  (pre rendered content) will work. But `{{ A.page.content | njk }}` will return empty.

This is useful to render content of RSS feeds, where you don't want `A.content` (the full rendered page) but still want to resolve the templates.

I think the problem is that the njk plugin/filter should either assume it's async by default or have an option to become so.

@oscarotero Update: at the moment, the `@parcel/source-map` module doesn't work with Deno (See https://github.com/parcel-bundler/source-map/issues/110).
- Unpkg serves the code as CommonJS, which Deno doesn't like
  - May be fixed in https://github.com/parcel-bundler/source-map/pull/111
- Esm.sh _does_ serve the code as ESM. But, the way it servers WASM files means that the JS can't import it.
  - Open issue https://github.com/esm-dev/esm.sh/issues/358
- I managed to get it working, by rewriting some of the module locally, but that's not an ideal situation.

Comments.Body:
The url of the pages is `'/post/'+article.publish.slug`, which I assume it doesn't end with `/` or `.html`. 

`search.pages()` only returns html pages, and Lume only consider HTML pages those which the `.html` extension in the url or ending with `/` (which is equivalent to `/index.html`). [See this](https://lume.land/docs/creating-pages/urls/#the-url-variable).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Right, adding `/` to the end of the url makes the pages show up ...

So it is related to generating pages indirectly, in that when setting the `url` field yourself you need to be aware of the behaviour. Which is well-documented, but I lost sight of the connection. Good to have the two parts of the documentation linked together by this issue!

Thanks!
Comments.Author: ghost

I_kwDOEYAvFc5ew9le.Title: Picture plugin
I_kwDOEYAvFc5ew9le.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5ew9le.Body: ### Enter your suggestions in details: It would work combined with the Imagick plugin, to generate different versions of the same image by scanning the HTML and searching for `` elements with the `formats` and/or `sizes` attribute. For example: ```html ``` This would generate the following code: ```html ``` And all versions of `/my-image.jpg`. Related issue: #356

Comments.Body:
I'd love to see a picture plugin.

In terms of how I would like to see this work, rather than having to wrap every image in a site with a `picture` tag, I'd like to have a central place where the configuration of what formats and sizes I want (like already exists with the Imagick plugin). The plugin would then wrap any `img` element (that is not already wrapped in a `picture` element?) with a `picture` element and include the other formats. If that's too greedy, then having an attribute on an `img` tag to signal that it should be processed by the picture plugin (like the inline plugin) would be great too. Your suggestion of having the plugin search for `picture` elements and `formats`/`sizes` attributes could be a great way to override the central configuration of the plugin.

My motivation for suggesting this is that I often author pages in Markdown, and I'd like to not have to change how I'm including images. I'd like the standard way of including images to just work with this new plugin.

~It would also be nice to (perhaps as an option) include a hash of the generated images in the file name, to support long-term caching of these resources. Could that be supported here (or is that already handled by another plugin)?~ I've just seen that this is covered by https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/352.
Comments.Author: cgwrench

Comments.Body:
Okay, it makes sense. Maybe it could work in this way: ```html
``` ```html ``` ```html ``` I not sure about the name of the attributes. And I'd like to have only one attribute instead of two. Maybe something like `img="avif,jpg | 1x,300w,2x"`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Hi @oscarotero. That looks good, but I don't think I was clear with my suggestion. Sorry about that, let me try again.

When I suggested a default configuration, I was imaging something set when registering the plugin, something like:

```javascript
site.use(picture({
  widths: [300, 600],
  formats: ["webp", "jpg"]
}));
```

I've lifted these option names from [Eleventy's image plugin](https://www.11ty.dev/docs/plugins/image/#usage), feel free to pick different options.

Alternatively, the imagick plugin uses shared data. Is there a way we could do the same for a picture plugin, so there is consistency between the two plugins?

With this configuration in place, I'd imagine all images will be affected. Perhaps then your suggestion of `img-formats`/`img-sizes` or `img` attributes would then allow users to override the default configuration for a given image.

I hope this makes sense, do let me know if I've not been clear about anything. And, of course, feel free to ignore all of this if you choose!
Comments.Author: cgwrench

Comments.Body:
@cgwrench Yes, I got your suggestion, but don't think it's a good idea to specify this globally in the `_config` file. A website can have different sections, with different configurations for pictures (It can have a blog, a photo gallery, a landing page, etc). So I think this feature should be defined in the templates, in order to have different configurations per page (or even per section in the same page).

If you want to define this behavior for the entire site, you can add the attribute in a layout shared to all pages. For example:

```html
<!doctype html>
<html>
<head>
...
</head>

<body img="avif,jpg | 1x,300w,2x">
    {{ content | safe }}
</body>
</html>
```

To define this value using shared data, you can create a variable in a _data file and use it in the templates. For example:

```yml
# _data.yml

picture_config: "avif,jpg | 1x,300w,2x"
```

```html
<!doctype html>
<html>
<head>
...
</head>

<body img="{{ picture_config }}">
    {{ content | safe }}
</body>
</html>
```

Does it works for you?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: That does work for me, thanks!
Comments.Author: cgwrench

Comments.Body:
Work in progress here: https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins/tree/main/picture

You can see an example here:
https://github.com/lumeland/experimental-plugins/blob/main/picture/demo/index.njk

The current syntax is:

- `webp jpg 300` Create webp and jpg versions of 300px width
- `webp jpg 300@1,2` Create webp and jpg versions of 300px width for 1x and 2x resolutions
- `webp jpg 300@1,2  600@1,2` Create webp and jpg versions of 300px and 600px width for 1x and 2x resolutions
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: It will be available in the coming v1.18.0. I'm closing this
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5Qq_b7.Title: Inline plugin does not respect additional style tag attributes
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Qq_b7.Author: jrson83
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Qq_b7.Body:
When using the [inline plugin](https://lume.land/plugins/inline/) with `style` tag to inline CSS, the plugin removes the actuall `style` tag from DOM and [creates a new one](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/inline.ts#L108).  This has the consequence that additional attributes are not considered or removed. For `script` tags this is not the case, since the actuall script tag gets just modified.

The plugin should respect additional attributes like `nonce="whatever"` and keep those. An example of this is the use of the `nonce` attribute for the [CSP](https://content-security-policy.com/examples/allow-inline-style/).

Comments.Body:
`link` and `style` elements are of different types, so they cannot share all attributes. But I'm agree that some attributes like `nonce`, `id` or `class` must be keept.

By the way, I just realized that the attribute `media` is not taken into account. The expected behavior is the css code must be wrapped by a `@media` rule. For example:

```html
<link rel="stylesheet" media="print" href="print.css" inline />
```
should become to:
```html
<style>
@media print {
...
}
</style>
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Fixed.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5TTgQd.Title: Processed image files and filename parsing for dates
I_kwDOEYAvFc5TTgQd.Author: kari

Comments.Body:
Yes, they are loaded as pages, but not regular pages, but asset pages (See the difference here: https://lume.land/docs/core/concepts/#page-files).

But I agree that the date extracting from the file name makes no sense in the asset pages, I'll fix this in the next version. Meanwhile, your workaround is fine.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
After some tests, I think that the best solution is remove the interpretation of files starting with a number + `_` as a date. It doesn't make sense and it's a too generical pattern that can conflict with many files.
Due it's a kind of breaking change I re-schedule this change to 1.13.0.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Fixed. It will be available in the next version 1.13.0.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5vM6jO.Title: Import NPM modules using ranges
I_kwDOEYAvFc5vM6jO.Author: binyamin
I_kwDOEYAvFc5vM6jO.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

### Summary
When importing an NPM module, use a caret range (ie. `npm:preact@^10.16.0`). If a user has another compatible version of the module installed, Deno will just use that one instead of downloading another one. In other words, Deno's npm specifiers support semver ranges, so let's use them.

### Notes

Esbuild imports should probably use a tilde, so only patch updates will be affected, because [esbuild makes breaking changes in minor updates](https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md#0190).

#### Syntax
- caret "^" allows minor & patch updates
- tilde "~" only allows patch updates

Comments.Body: For now, I prefer to continue using fixed versions. It's much more stable and easy to debug. I know Deno is moving toward semver compatibility (as announced for Deno 2.0) but I'm not fully convinced about this change and rather to wait.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @oscarotero I haven't read the news about Deno 2.0. I did some testing, and ever since Deno stabilized NPM specifiers in v1.28.0, it correctly resolves semver in NPM specifiers.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body:
When I mention stability, I'm not referring to Deno but the packages in general. One of the good things of Lume is the simplicity. If you want to use postcss, just import the plugin and use it. You don't need to worry about which version is used or if you need to update it because a minor release just came out.

Using fixed versions allows to test Lume in a reliable way. Ranges can introduce new bugs or inconsitencies with the Lume plugin, even in patch updates. Every time a new version of Lume is released, all dependencies are updated and tested ([using udd](https://github.com/hayd/deno-udd)). In the edge case that you need a different version, it's possible to patch it with the import maps.

Maybe in the future I change my mind, but for now I'd rather to continue using fixed versions.
If you want to take a look what's coming to Deno 2.0, this video explains it well: https://youtu.be/5DX49vzLfqw?si=hkoKe3EWuHeez27K&t=820
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5SvS1N.Title: Imagick plugin: Reloading site with suffixed images creates images with repeated suffixes
I_kwDOEYAvFc5SvS1N.Author: paulrobertlloyd

Comments.Body:
Mmm, ok this is a bug.
As a temporary workaround, move the suffixed version to first or second position (not last).
It will be fixed in the next version.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

MDU6SXNzdWU4NzM5NzI3MzI=.Title: site.options.dev not set on _config.js when using --dev
MDU6SXNzdWU4NzM5NzI3MzI=.Author: fserb
MDU6SXNzdWU4NzM5NzI3MzI=.Body:
Is supporting `--dev` specific config a thing?

It seems that `site.options.dev` is always false inside `_config.js` and it's only set afterwards.

Comments.Body: `dev` mode is used to render pages in `draft`. And you can set it in `_config.js` by `const site = lume({ dev: true })` or override with the `--dev` option in CLI.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
yeah, I get that. But wouldn't it make some sense for it to be set on the `lume()` call, so it can be used inside _config?
For the a case like this:
```
const site = lume();

site.options.location = new URL(site.options.dev ? "http://localhost" : "https://mysite.com");
```

that can be controlled by the command line flag.

I'm not 100% sure if that's a reasonable use case.
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
For this specific use case, you can set a different location from CLI (`--location=url`). But I'm agree that dev configuration could be useful in `_config.js`.

Maybe a environment variable, like `DENO_ENV` (like `NODE_ENV`) or `LUME_DEV` (specific for lume) could help.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @fserb FYI, this is fixed in [0.22.2](../blob/master/CHANGELOG.md#0222---2021-06-07) (released 2021-06-07).
Comments.Author: valtlai

I_kwDOEYAvFc5A4HB3.Title: Support React?
I_kwDOEYAvFc5A4HB3.Author: audrow
I_kwDOEYAvFc5A4HB3.Body: Hey, Lume looks great. Is there a good way to use React with Lume? If not, can a plugin be added to use React? Not being able to use React is the main reason that I'm looking at other options for making a static site. I understand if that's not in the design goals of Lume.

Comments.Body:
Hi, @audrow 
React is available but not enabled by default. You have to use the JSX plugin, that uses React under the hood. More info: https://lumeland.github.io/plugins/jsx/

If you want to run React in the front side, you can take a look to this demo repo https://github.com/lumeland/react-todo
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Awesome, thank you @oscarotero and thank you for the quick reply!
Comments.Author: audrow

I_kwDOEYAvFc49pfzO.Title: Generate robots.txt automatically
I_kwDOEYAvFc49pfzO.Author: x80486
I_kwDOEYAvFc49pfzO.Body: I think it would be handy to have an option to generate a customized `robots.txt` in the same way as any other template, [in the same way Hugo does](https://gohugo.io/templates/robots/).

Comments.Body:
You can do that in two ways:

- Create a page file adding a subextension. For example `robots.txt.njk` will output the file `robots.txt`.
- Or create a page file and set the `url` variable to `/robots.txt`.

You have more info here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/discussions/113
Comments.Author: oscarotero
