I_kwDOEYAvFc5iSycs.Title: Search plugin: support logical NOT
I_kwDOEYAvFc5iSycs.Author: kwaa
I_kwDOEYAvFc5iSycs.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details: ### Discussed in https://github.com/lumeland/lume/discussions/405
Originally posted by **kwaa** March 31, 2023 I use [my own Frontmatter format](https://fff.js.org), where [`flags: ['unlisted']`](https://fff.js.org/concepts/flags.html) means that this post is not included in the list. So how do I accurately search for flags that contain unlisted? [The search plugin's documentation](https://lume.land/plugins/search/) doesn't seem to indicate this, or maybe it doesn't support it yet.
In this discussion I proposed `flags!*=unlisted`, but the logical NOT is more general. Example: ``` category^=A => !category^=A category$=B => !category$=B title*=security => !title*=security level>2 => !level>2 ```

Comments.Body:
Ok, it makes sense.

I just commited a first version. You can test it after updating Lume to the latest development version (`deno task lume upgrade --dev`).

It supports both versions: `category!^=A` and `!category^=A` are equivalents.

The reason is the NOT operator of `=` is `!=`, so it would be a bit confusing to have a different syntax for the other operators. Now you can do `!category=A` as an alternative way to `category!=A`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Ok, it makes sense.
> 
> I just commited a first version. You can test it after updating Lume to the latest development version (`deno task lume upgrade --dev`).
> 
> It supports both versions: `category!^=A` and `!category^=A` are equivalents.
> 
> The reason is the NOT operator of `=` is `!=`, so it would be a bit confusing to have a different syntax for the other operators. Now you can do `!category=A` as an alternative way to `category!=A`.

`!flags*=unlisted` and `flags!*=unlisted` have been tested to work! 🚀
Comments.Author: kwaa

I_kwDOEYAvFc54B8FY.Title: (remark): Support for passing through remarkRehype configuration
I_kwDOEYAvFc54B8FY.Author: marcus-crane
I_kwDOEYAvFc54B8FY.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Hey there,

I've been porting my site over to Lume, which has been quite enjoyable so far. My blog makes use of [Littlefoot](https://littlefoot.js.org/) which is a small JS library for inlining footnotes.

With Lume, I'm also using Remark which is nice but by default, generates some footnotes with syntax that Littlefoot can't quite work with.

The fix for this is just to override how the footnotes are rendered but doing that requires being able to pass through some configuration to [remarkRehype](https://github.com/remarkjs/remark-rehype).

Unfortunately, it isn't possible to do this, as remarkRehype is initialised with some default settings and isn't reachable from `_config.ts` so I'd like to request that it be opened up for passthrough.

On a side note, and some of this is really down to Deno using URLs as identifiers, I was pleasantly surprised at how easy it was to [pull the remark plugin into my site's repo](https://github.com/marcus-crane/utf9k/commit/7fc7ee6b1d8152275bd9d29769cbf98847def5fb) and make this modification locally. 

The references within will break over time of course so I'd still like to request it as something officially supported but it makes me feel a little easier that it's possible to reach into Lume's bowels a little bit for small patches without having to say; fork the entire codebase (not that I would bother to get that far)

I'd be happy to try and contribute a PR as well, although I'd have to have a think about what a good API might look like. I was a bit surprised to see that none of the existing remark config made use of types too 😅 

Thanks again!

Comments.Body:
Hi @marcus-crane 
Yeah, I think a `rehypeOptions` property would be nice. What do you think?
Feel free to PR this change, thank you!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Neat, I'll try and submit a PR sometime this week 🙂
Comments.Author: marcus-crane

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero Congrats on the launch of Lume 2! I was just about to get around to PRing this again and I've had it running for my site in production for a while now.

Maybe I missed it but I didn't see any explicit mention of whether the 1.x branch is frozen now? If so, I guess I can just reopen the PR I had previously that was created against Lume 2?
Comments.Author: marcus-crane

Comments.Body:
I think it has been implemented here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/commit/7f123b2cfac61d23d5c05236447865fdb9750be0

but the MDX plugin doesn't support this yet.

@oscarotero Maybe we should refer to the `@mdx-js/mdx` options?

https://github.com/mdx-js/mdx/blob/d1fface103b90d2ef8d14d50b77cbfcf20b4bb71/packages/mdx/lib/core.js#L205-L214

- `rehypeOptions => remarkRehypeOptions`
Comments.Author: kwaa

Comments.Body: Ah neat, thanks for the heads up
Comments.Author: marcus-crane

Comments.Body:
Hi @marcus-crane .
Yes, I implemented in Lume 2. But feel free to open a PR to add it to Lume 1 if you need it.
I will continue to give some support to Lume 1.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @kwaa good idea!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Ah neat, I've upgraded my own site to v2.0 now and am successfully using the updates (from main branch currently) so I'm going to mark this issue as closed. Thanks again!

MDU6SXNzdWU4NzQwMDI0OTQ=.Title: Tags don't propagate on layouts
MDU6SXNzdWU4NzQwMDI0OTQ=.Author: fserb
MDU6SXNzdWU4NzQwMDI0OTQ=.Body:
I'm not sure if this is a feature request or a bug.

if you have:

_include/mylayout.njk
```
---
tags: mytag
---
```

page.njk
```
---
layout: mylayout.njk
---
```

Then I'd expect that `page.njk` would be part of `mytag`. Other data seems to propagate properly, but tags seem to be ignored. When used like this:

```
---
renderOrder: 1
---
{%- for post in search.pages('mytag') -%}
```

Comments.Body: we seem to only save `content` from `#renderPage`. I think it would make sense to update `data` too?
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
Data in layouts doesn't affect to the pages, it's used only for rendering. You can use `_data.*` files to apply the same tag to all files in a directory.
Why do you want to do this?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
It's a bit quality of life. It feels it should just work.

My use case is: I have a special type of post, that has a different layout and I want to search all of those. Mostly to avoid having to do:
```
layout: x
tags: x
```
everywhere. In my case, `_data` will probably solve it.
Comments.Author: fserb

Comments.Body:
Note that you can search a page not only by tags but any other value. For example:

```njk
{%- for post in search.pages('layout=mylayout.njk') -%}
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
That totally solves it. :)

"The purpose of layouts is rendering pages, not assign data. You have _data.* files and folders for that purpose."

That's fair, I guess.
Comments.Author: fserb

Hi! Thanks for a great site generator! Solid work on docs and general quality of the tool 👏

ReferenceError: module is not defined

I_kwDOEYAvFc6MBWdy.Title: InvalidStateError: readyState not OPEN
I_kwDOEYAvFc6MBWdy.Author: iacore
I_kwDOEYAvFc6MBWdy.Body:
### Version

v2.2.1

### Platform

Linux

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

1. `deno task serve`
2. modify a page, save a page, modify the same page, save the same page. do this very fast

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

the crash is probabilistic. the faster you do the sequence of actions, the more likely that deno will crash.

### What is the expected behavior?

No crash

### What do you see instead?

```
error: Uncaught (in promise) InvalidStateError: readyState not OPEN
    sockets.forEach((socket) => socket.send(message));
                                       ^
    at WebSocket.send (ext:deno_websocket/01_websocket.js:326:13)
    at https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/middlewares/reload.ts:24:40
    at Set.forEach (<anonymous>)
    at https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/middlewares/reload.ts:24:13
    at Events.dispatchEvent (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/core/events.ts:46:19)
    at SiteWatcher.dispatchEvent (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/core/watcher.ts:155:24)
    at https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/core/watcher.ts:166:12
    at Events.dispatchEvent (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/core/events.ts:46:19)
    at Site.dispatchEvent (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/core/site.ts:272:24)
```

### Additional information

seems to be a race condition

Comments.Body:
Hmm.
WebSockets are added to the sockets list only when they are open, and removed on close ([See code here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/main/middlewares/reload.ts#L35-L37))

So perhaps a socket is closed and, before being removed of the sockets list, the send method is invoked.

Can you try [changing this line](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/main/middlewares/reload.ts#L24) adding an `if` to check if the socket is already open?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5hxoKD.Title: Incorrect typing for `jsx_preact.ts`
I_kwDOEYAvFc5hxoKD.Author: indifferentghost

Yeah, good catch. `renderSync` must return a string or an object with the `toString()` function defined (which is the case here): https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/renderer.ts#L310

I'll fix it.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc6qgqt4.Title: RSS (Atom) feeds are not parsed as valid XML when using the `feed` plugin
I_kwDOEYAvFc6qgqt4.Author: yeskunall
I_kwDOEYAvFc6qgqt4.Body:
### Version

v2.5.1

### Platform

macOS 13.7.1

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

1. Use the [feed](https://lume.land/plugins/feed/) plugin to generate an RSS feed

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

> How often does it reproduce?

Always. Tested on Brave Browser.

The required condition is to use the [feed](https://lume.land/plugins/feed/) plugin as stated above.

### What is the expected behavior?

The feed should be parsed as valid XML.

### What do you see instead?

It is not parsed as valid XML. As a result, Brave Browser—and I believe all other browsers—render the feed as a regular text file, which is not ideal if you want to work with the XML file in the browser.

### Additional information

The `application/rss+xml` media type by the RSS Advisory Group [never took off](https://www.rssboard.org/rss-mime-type-application.txt). Instead, most modern browsers use the media types standardized in [RFC7303](https://www.iana.org/go/rfc7303), which are:

- `application/xml`
- `application/xml-external-parsed-entity`, and
- `application/xml-dtd`

I think Lume should use the first one on the list and perhaps fallback to `text/xml` (as an alias, as suggested in RFC7303). I tested both `application/xml` and `text/xml` by modifying the response headers in Chrome DevTools and it works as expected.

LMK if you need more info!

---

[IANA Media Types](https://www.iana.org/assignments/media-types/media-types.xhtml)
[StackOverflow discussion from 2009](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/595616/what-is-the-correct-mime-type-to-use-for-an-rss-feed)

Comments.Body:
Ok, to clarify: the issue is the value of the content type serving the `.rss` file, right?

This content type is returned by `@std/media-types` library (https://jsr.io/@std/media-types/1.1.0/vendor/db.ts). I can fix it temporarily in Lume but the ideal solution would be to fix it in the origin of the problem.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Ok, to clarify: the issue is the value of the content type serving the .rss file, right?

Correct.

> I can fix it temporarily in Lume but the ideal solution would be to fix it in the origin of the problem.

Absolutely correct. I will open an issue with the Deno guys, too. I just wanted to bring this to your attention. Thanks as always, Óscar!
Comments.Author: yeskunall

Comments.Body:
Hey, just wanted to drop by to give some more info:

`@std/media-types` depends on [`jshttp/mime-db`](https://github.com/jshttp/mime-db), which actually pulls the non-standard `application/rss+xml` from [Apache common media types](https://svn.apache.org/repos/asf/httpd/httpd/trunk/docs/conf/mime.types). I will open an issue in the `mime-db` repo asking them to remove it. If it does, great, but it is unlikely it will be.
Comments.Author: yeskunall

Comments.Body:
If they don't fix them, maybe Deno's std package can make an exception for this.

Anyway, I just fixed it (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/commit/5ea3f2a70bc022f703e8d5b0349b6491c001afb9)
Can you try it and confirm if it works fine now?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Hey Óscar, I didn’t have time to test it when you pinged me. Just did, and it works as expected. Thanks so much for the quick turnaround on this. 💖

**PS**: tracking your work on Lume 3, excited to try it out!
Comments.Author: yeskunall

I_kwDOEYAvFc6dg6ge.Title: ImageTransform broken after 2.4.1 upgrade?
I_kwDOEYAvFc6dg6ge.Author: RickCogley
I_kwDOEYAvFc6dg6ge.Body:
### Version

2.4.1

### Platform

macOS, Netlify

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

If you clone this and run lume locally:
https://github.com/eSolia/japanactivationcapital.com/tree/redesign20241031
... you might see that `_site/media` has no generated images at all. 

I had removed `transform-images` tags from images, and removed the `/media/_data.yml` for testing when I was not sure what was causing trouble with a different, now-resolved problem. Then I upgraded to 2.4.0 yesterday (?) and 2.4.1 this AM, and decided to revert those to get the benefit of the transforms. 

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Seems always

### What is the expected behavior?

Transformed images appear in `_site/media`

### What do you see instead?

No transformed images in `_site/media`

### Additional information

_No response_

Comments.Body:
I upgraded a different repo, and noticed this: 

```
❯ cd ../julie_portfolio/
❯ lume upgrade
Task lume export TZ='Asia/Tokyo' && echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run -A - "upgrade"
Warning The following packages contained npm lifecycle scripts (preinstall/install/postinstall) that were not executed:
┠─ npm:sharp@0.33.5
┃
┠─ This may cause the packages to not work correctly.
┠─ Lifecycle scripts are only supported when using a `node_modules` directory.
┠─ Enable it in your deno config file:
┖─ "nodeModulesDir": "auto"
Update successful!
Your Lume version is: v2.4.1
```

Is this relevant?
Comments.Author: RickCogley

Comments.Body:
It's not relevant. This happens in Deno 2.x for npm packages that have lifecycle scripts (that must run `preinstall`, `install` or `postinstall` events). Deno only can run these scripts is the package is instaled in a node_modules directory.
Fortunately, the script of sharp is only to check and validate the installation, so it works without running it.

I'll take a look to your problem.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
The problem is you're statically copying `.jpg`, `.png` and `.avif` files here: https://github.com/eSolia/japanactivationcapital.com/blob/redesign20241031/_config.ts#L128

Copied files are not processed (another case of confusion between copying and loading files, I hope to change the behavior in Lume 3).
After removing these extensions (to keep only `.pdf`), it works fine.

BTW, the `matches` option doesn't exist. https://github.com/eSolia/japanactivationcapital.com/blob/redesign20241031/_config.ts#L56
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Oof, sorry about that and thanks for setting me straight. I bet I assumed something worked as I was changing things, because files got left in _site.
Comments.Author: RickCogley

Comments.Body: Just to close the loop, everything is working as expected now. Thanks again!
Comments.Author: RickCogley

MDU6SXNzdWU4NzE0MzM0OTY=.Title: Outputting extensionless file (like `_headers`) from template
MDU6SXNzdWU4NzE0MzM0OTY=.Author: valtlai
MDU6SXNzdWU4NzE0MzM0OTY=.Body:
I would like to dynamically generate a `_headers` file to configure HTTP headers for Netlify. (And I’m going to do something similar with `_redirects` too.)

There’s no way to output a file named `_headers` from a template (i.e. without using `site.copy()`). Setting `slugifyUrls: false` in the config file and `url: /_headers` in a front matter results a file `_headers/index.html` (or `_headers.html` if `prettyUrls: false`).

The problem is that the `_headers` file has no file extension and Lume adds `.html` for missing one.

Should we stop adding the extension and only add `index.html` if `url` has a trailing slash? Then `url: /foo` would output `/foo` and `url: /foo/` would output `/foo/index.html`. Or should there be an option (such as `forceExtensions: false`) for this behavior?

Comments.Body:
I see some problems requiring trailing slash. For example if `prettyUrls: false`, you have to include the .html extension to all urls defined in the frontmatter.

One solution could be allow to set an object with properties for the url value. For example:

```yml
---
url:
  filename: _headers
  extension: false
---
```

The [`urlPage`](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/site.js#L447) function could use these properties to define the final name, instead the default behavior. I imagine available possible properties: `directory`, `filename` and `extension`. Not defined values (directory in the example above), will use the default value.

What do you think?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I like it. Should `filename` be `path`, because its value could include directories? What would `directories` do? Also, could there be `pretty` and `slugify` for overriding `prettyUrls` and `slugifyUrls` for a particular template?
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
`directory` would be the directory where the file will be saved. For example, the output file `/posts/hellow-world/index.html` would be:

```yml
url:
  directory: /posts/hello-word/
  filename: index
  extension: .html
```

If the directory is not defined, the src directory of the file will be used.

But I like more your idea of using `path` and `extension`, because it's the same values that we have in `src` and `dest` of every page, so it's more consistent:

```yml
url:
  path: /post/hello-word/index
  extension: .html
```

The difference between using this object and a string like `url: /post/hello-word`, is that with the object we are defining the final output filename (the value of [`dest`](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/site.js#L464)) so I wouldn't apply the `pretty` and `slugify` transformations.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
That makes sense and I think this looks pretty good now.

Should the value used to omit the extension be `null` or an empty string instead, because the variable isn’t a boolean?

So the solution for this specific use case would be the following:

```yaml
url:
  path: /_headers
  extension: null
```
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
> Should the value used to omit the extension be null or an empty string instead, because the variable isn’t a boolean?

Yes, a null or empty string is better. Or maybe nothing:

```yml
url:
  path: /_headers
```

In this case, the extension would be empty.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @valtlai I just implemented this new feature.  You can [see an example here](https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/headers.njk) and [the expected output here](https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/_expected/_headers).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: That’s nice. However, relative paths aren’t supported with an object. Should them?
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body: Good point. I don't see why not. I'll add it.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
One more question: How should a path with a trailing slash work?

```yml
url:
  path: /foo/
```

This currently throws an error. Is it ok or should it output `/foo/index.html` instead?
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body: I think it's ok the error. This is the explicit way to define the output file, so I wouldn't change the value introduced by the user.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: That’s fine. I think this is ready, so closing.
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
The commit 772f9e0fab314cd15cebb1482086f3b07e30e24a also changed how the following works:

```yml
---
url: /404.html
---
```

Before the commit, `/404.html` was outputted. After it, `/404/index.html` is outputted. Was that an intended change?
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body: No, it's not intended. Let me see.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: This didn’t work even before the commit, but I think an input file `404.html.md` should output `404.html` and not `404/index.html` as it currently does.
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body: I think it's fixed now. And subextensions like `404.html.md` are supported too (in fact this was implemented some time ago, but stopped working at some point).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thank you! It seems working now, so closing. :)
Comments.Author: valtlai

I_kwDOEYAvFc5_ImbP.Title: Merge site.copy and site.loadAssets?
I_kwDOEYAvFc5_ImbP.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5_ImbP.Body:
This is an idea that would like to discuss and hear the opinion of more people. If it's approved, it's a huge breaking change so it would be implemented in Lume 3 (that I don't have plans to work on that anytime soon).

Currently, Lume has several ways to output files to the site:

- **Pages:** They are the files intended to generate HTML files. They use template engines and layouts to render. The output path is the filename but replacing the original extension with `.html` or `/index.html` (depending on the prettyUrls configuration). For example `/dir/page.md` generates `/dir/page/index.html`.
- **Assets:** Similar to Pages but they are not rendered, only (pre)processed. The output filename is the same as the source filename. For example `/dir/file.css` generates `/dir/file.css`.
- **Static files:** They are files that are not loaded in memory but copied when all pages and assets are saved to the dest folder. They are configured with `site.copy()`.

The problem is **assets** and **static files** serves a similar purpose: output non-html files. But due static files are copies as is, they are not processed, so plugins like postcss, transform_images, sass, svgo, etc don't work with these files.

Let's see the following example:

```js
site.copy([".css"]);
site.use(postcss());
```

In this configuration, the build copy all CSS files and you may expect these files to be transformed with the `postcss` plugin. However the plugin only works with assets. I think this behavior may be confusing.

Static files exist to avoid loading in memory files for no reason. Only files that must be transformed (compiled, minified, etc) should be loaded in memory. It's a copy of the [Passthrought File Copy](https://www.11ty.dev/docs/copy/) feature of Eleventy.

But I think Lume should be more clever than this, instead of delegating to the user the decision of configuring a file to be loaded or copied, depending on the selected plugins. My idea is to merge `site.copy()` and `site.loadAssets()`. This will remove duplicated code and simplify the internal implementation. This new feature could have the generical name of Files.

Files are not loaded initially (like static files) but will be on demand. If a file is being processed, the content will be loaded automatically. For example:

```js
site.files([".css"]); // Copy files
```

```js
site.files([".css"]); // Copy files

site.process([".css"], (files) => {
  // The content of the .css files is loaded before run this processor
});
```

## How to rename files?

Due to files are not loaded initially, we cannot include data like a frontmatter with the `url` value to change the output path. We can use `_data` files to define a `url` function or change the `basename` of the folder.

We can also use preprocessors to assign data to the files without loading it (file content would be loaded only for processors, not preprocessors). For example:

```js
site.preprocess([".css"], (files) => {
  files.forEach((file) => changeUrl(file);
});
```

Static files allow to rename of the file in the `copy` function. For example `site.copy("assets", "")`. We can implement the same feature on files.

Thoughts?

Comments.Body: Hi, I certainly think it would be good to simplify this! It took me a while to figure out why things weren't working when I was trying to get a js file loaded while exploring #580. using `site.copy()` seems to override an earlier `site.loadAssets()`. This is definitely confusing. If the API surface is reduced from two to one functions, there's less opportunity for unsuspected interactions.
Comments.Author: ghost

Comments.Body: This was fixed in Lume 3. The new function `site.add()` replaces `site.copy()`, `site.loadAssets()` and `site.copyRemaining()`, making everything simpler.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5Tl20Q.Title: When serving, asset paths are not reset after modifying with updateDest
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Tl20Q.Author: valpackett
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Tl20Q.Body:
When running a dev server, something like this:

```js
let cnt = 1

site
  .loadAssets([".css"])
  .process([".css"], (page) => {
    cnt += 1
    page.updateDest({ path: page.dest.path + `.${cnt}` })
  })
```

keeps modifying *the same* path string, building up new additions all the time, i.e. like `s.2.3.4.5.css`:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/208340/194784399-94f75dbe-bb3a-4d55-b6bd-68edab9e3b5a.png)

This is not the expected behavior :)

(version 1.12.0)

Comments.Body:
Yes, this is how it works now, but I guess it's not what users expect.
I have to think in a way to reset the page data to prevent this incremental changes, without affecting to the performance.

But this may take a while. For now you have to replace the old value with the new.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Fixed. It will be available in Lume 1.13.0
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5eYIPk.Title: Reflect base subdirectory when serving site through lume server
I_kwDOEYAvFc5eYIPk.Author: bglw

Comments.Body: I think it's easy to implement, but why do you want this? I want to understand the use case for this behavior.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Mainly consistency. If I have the following template: ```html Home Home ``` When serving in dev mode, both will render as `/` and it won't be obvious that the `| url` filter was missing in the second case. Only when building the final site will they render as `/documentation/` and `/` and cause an issue. In general my desire is to setup dev mode as close to the final build as possible
Comments.Author: bglw
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
>The best way to handle subfolders is by using the [base_path plugin](https://lume.land/plugins/base_path/)

Good tip, I'll look at using that more. The `url` filter still holds true for loading assets though, which I would also like to be consistent between dev/prod.

>It's possible override it in your _config.ts file

That does override the location for the purposes of the `url` filter and such, but the Lume server continues to serve requests without that location taken into account. (i.e. I would like my `index.mdx` file to be at `http://localhost.com:3000/documentation/`, but it is still at `http://localhost.com:3000/`)
Comments.Author: bglw
oscarotero
bglw

Comments.Body:
Yeah, I think Eleventy does what you are requesting, but to me it isn't very practical and portable.

- The output is different depending on whether the site location is in a subpath or not.
- If you want to deploy your site with, say `rsync`, you have to take that into account.
- The index page of the local server always returns a `404`, because you need to go to the subpath (although this could be fixed with a redirect).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Yeah definitely pros/cons, and I don't know what is a better default, I just like to have the option :) (and with `preprocess`, I effectively do 🎉)
Comments.Author: bglw

Comments.Body:
After thinking of this a bit more, I decided to not implement this option, because it's a edge case and would complicate the internal code of Lume to take all cases into account (it would affect to some plugins, urls, etc).

For now, the preprocessor solution works fine. If there are more people requesting this feature, I can change my mind :)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc6okbF1.Title: Override SASS options.importer
I_kwDOEYAvFc6okbF1.Author: tdillon
I_kwDOEYAvFc6okbF1.Body:
Does the Lume SASS plugin allow the `importer` option to be overridden?  From the code, it doesn't appear possible since the options object is destructured before the default `importer` is defined.  Is that by design, or should the options destructuring occur later?

https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/60892e9551bb2936d3d1637f43d8c5e04c42644c/plugins/sass.ts#L73

I'm asking because I'm trying to use https://github.com/uswds/uswds in a Lume project and it doesn't seem like the Lume SASS plugin can handle the paths as defined by the USWDS.  Any help would be much appreciated.

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
It might.  I'd be happy to test anything out if you'd like.


My current attempt is:

```typescript
site.use(sass({includes: "node_modules/@uswds/uswds/packages/"}))
```

This only works for a few imports.  Eventually it errors based on which type of path USWDS uses in their `@use` statements.

For example, this file https://github.com/uswds/uswds/blob/develop/packages/uswds-core/src/styles/tokens/color/shortcodes-color-system.scss has 2 `@use` statements.

```sass
# Works with Lume
@use "../../settings" as *;

# Errors with Lume
# Looks for <repo-root>/node_modules/@uswds/uswds/packages/uswds-core/src/styles/tokens/color/uswds-core/src/styles/function/color/get-system-color
@use "uswds-core/src/styles/functions/color/get-system-color" as *;
```

I assumed I (or someone) needed to implement a "[Node package importer](https://sass-lang.com/blog/announcing-pkg-importers/)" for Lume to make this work, but if you can handle it with an includes map that would be just as good.

Thank you very much.
Comments.Author: tdillon
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I've been trying to modify the plugin to support uswds and similar packages but I can't make it to work. 

According to the docs, seems that node imports must be prefixed with `pkg`. For example `pkg:uswds`


I've also tried this:

```js
site.use(sass({includes: "node_modules/@uswds/uswds/packages/"}))
```

As you said, this works only for some imports, but Sass resolves automatically some urls before passing to the importer. For example the code `@use uswds-core` in the file `file:///project/example.sass` is passed to the importer as `file:///project/uswds-core`, instead of simply `uswds-core`, so it's not possible to know if the original import is relative (`./uswds-core`) or not.
I'll keep investigating.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Setting `loadPaths` is working for me (at least in the simple POC I have).

Thank you very much for the quick turnaround.

FYI: I did not test adding an importer.
Comments.Author: tdillon

Comments.Body: Great. Thanks for testing!
Comments.Author: oscarotero
folliehiyuki

Comments.Body: @folliehiyuki I just tried the example [in the documentation page](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/modules/#global-functions) in Lume 2.5.2 and it works fine in my end: What doesn't work is if the value is assigned to a CSS custom property: So maybe it's a limitation of Sass.

I_kwDOEYAvFc6OXa5O.Title: Transform Images plugin: "VipsOperation: class "jxlsave_buffer" not found"
I_kwDOEYAvFc6OXa5O.Author: johanbrook
I_kwDOEYAvFc6OXa5O.Body:
### Version 2.2.2 ### Platform macOS Sonoma 14.4 ### What steps will reproduce the bug? **_config.ts** ```ts import lume from "lume/mod.ts"; import picture from "lume/plugins/picture.ts"; import transform_images from "lume/plugins/transform_images.ts"; const site = lume(); site.use(picture()); site.use(transform_images()); export default site; ``` **index.page.ts** ```ts export default function () { return `
`; } ``` Then run: ``` deno task serve ``` ### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition? Every time. ### What is the expected behavior? The correct images are generated into `_site/uploads`. ### What do you see instead? ``` Loading config file file:///Users/brookie/code/travelling-brooks/_config.ts Error: VipsOperation: class "jxlsave_buffer" not found at Sharp.toBuffer (file:///Users/brookie/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/sharp/0.33.4/lib/output.js:161:17) at transform (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/plugins/transform_images.ts:203:45) at eventLoopTick (ext:core/01_core.js:168:7) at async processPage (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/plugins/transform_images.ts:143:13) at async Promise.all (index 0) at async concurrent (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/core/utils/concurrent.ts:21:3) at async process (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/plugins/transform_images.ts:101:7) at async Processors.run (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/core/processors.ts:32:7) at async Site.#buildPages (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/core/site.ts:687:5) at async Site.build (https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.2.1/core/site.ts:530:9) { message: 'VipsOperation: class "jxlsave_buffer" not found' } ``` ### Additional information Removing `jxl` from `transform-images="jxl avif jpg 647@2"` fixes it.

Comments.Body: It seems a bug in sharp library. I've created this issue: https://github.com/lovell/sharp/issues/4153
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: I got response. Seems that it requires to compile libvips with support for libjxl. It will change when [jpegxl gets a bigger adoption](https://caniuse.com/jpegxl)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Probably, Lume shouldn't allow this value meanwhile.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Yep, looks like it. Good to know!

I’ll leave this issue open until you feel mentions about JPEG XL are removed from code and docs.
Comments.Author: johanbrook

Comments.Body: I'm closing this because I just released a new version of Lume without the JXL format, and it's now mentioned in the docs.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

as recomended on https://esm.sh/#docs


> Specify CJS Exports
> If you get an error like ...not provide an export named..., that means esm.sh can not resolve CJS exports of the module correctly. You can add ?cjs-exports=foo,bar query to specify the export names:
> 

```
import { NinetyRing, NinetyRingWithBg } from "https://esm.sh/react-svg-spinners@0.3.1?cjs-exports=NinetyRing,NinetyRingWithBg"
```



But I still get the error "Uncaught ReferenceError: Deno is not defined"

Comments.Body: Ok, it seems it comes from `namor` dependency. It uses Node APIs (like [crypto](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v19.x/docs/api/crypto.html)) so it's not browser friendly.

Comments.Body: As commented in https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/400#issuecomment-1483199836 the new `esmOptions` key configures the options to send to esm.sh, so you don't have to include it in your code.

Interesting !

I'm a bit puzzled that Deno is involved here, because that code never runs on the server (or at least it shouldn't), if esm does the compilation, the only thing Deno should do is to send the code (as inert data) to the client. 

I noticed something interesting: if I remove the offending code, start Lume, and then add the code again, the code reloads and it works correctly.   

It seems as if the offending code prevents something on the server to initialize correctly, because once you remove it, and the initialisation gets done, the offending code has lost it's corrupting effect !
Comments.Author: max-l

Comments.Body:
The to-do app repository (that you used as a template) pre-renders the code at buildtime in order to be hydrated later on client side.

At buildtime, the App is imported in the `index.tmpl.ts` page and rendered here: https://github.com/max-l/lume-cjs-exports-test-case/blob/master/index.tmpl.ts#L6

On the client side, the app is imported and hydrated here: https://github.com/max-l/lume-cjs-exports-test-case/blob/master/main.jsx#L6-L11

So if you don't need buildtime pre-rendering, you can remove the code in the index.tmpl.ts file.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Interesting, there was much more magic going on than I thought !

I suppose I should use useEffect to switch on/off the prerendering strategicaly ?
Comments.Author: max-l

Comments.Body:
The prerendering is handled in the Lume template, before any React code is executed.

There's no much magic: just render the root component `App` with `ReactDOMServer.renderToString()` function and place the HTML result in the page (here: https://github.com/max-l/lume-cjs-exports-test-case/blob/master/index.tmpl.ts#L20).

The hydration step on the browser side just complete the HTML adding the event listeners (onclick, etc). It only makes sense if the component's data is available at buildtime, so the output HTML is similar to the code generated on the client side. In the to-do example, it didn't make sense because the data is stored in the localStorage on the browser side, so at build time only generates an empty to-do list. But it's just an example to demostrate that this is possible.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5hvjjk.Author: nycki93

Comments.Author: oscarotero

MDU6SXNzdWU3NjU0NjIzNzg=.Title: Nunjucks in md files
MDU6SXNzdWU3NjU0NjIzNzg=.Author: probins
MDU6SXNzdWU3NjU0NjIzNzg=.Body: I'm converting an old Jekyll site to Lume, and one issue I'm running into is that the md files contain `{{ ... }}`, but it looks like these are not being parsed. https://lumeland.github.io/plugins/nunjucks/ says "just place the .njk or .html file outside the _includes folder", but in my case these are .md files, not .html. I tried a simple test with a layout which includes an md file with `{{ content | safe }}`: if I put `{{ myData }}` in the layout file, it is parsed correctly, but if I put it in the md file, it is included unparsed in the output html. Is this intentional, or a bug of some sort? Or do I have to define something else to get the Nunjucks variables in md files parsed?

Comments.Body:
At this moment, there's no way to mix nunjucks and markdown. Maybe I could create a new file extension, let's say `*.md.njk`, so the file execute nunkjucks first and then render the result as markdown.

Anyway, one way to do this now is by changing the extensions of these files. For example, instead `index.md`, change to `index.njk`. So, even if the content is markdown code, it's transformed by nunjuks. Then, use the wrapper layout of the page to render markdown, for example:

index.njk:
```njk
---
title: Hello world
layout: main.njk
---
# {{ title }}
```

main.njk:
```njk
<html>
    <body>
         {{ content | md | safe }}
    </body>
</html>
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
yes, that works, thanks.

Rather than creating a new extension, I'd suggest making the whole process more configurable. Static sites can be built from any number of sources, for example, from cloud-based databases. They aren't useful for data that changes frequently, but they're perfect for publishing other data at little or no cost. Templates can be used in any type of file, for example, in js or css. So, it would be good to have the option to define in the config file which part of the build is run in which order, for example, run this script to fetch data from database, then run Nunjucks on the md files in dirA, then convert md files in dirB to html, and so on. There's currently the ability to run scripts from the command line, but it would also be useful to be able to just run Nunjucks or md conversion on a directory or even a single file, without having the recreate the whole site every time.

No hurry, of course, I can use what's there atm. Just something to think about for future development. 😄
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
1tty has a nice option `templateEngineOverride` to do that: https://www.11ty.dev/docs/languages/#overriding-the-template-language

Having something like that, you could configure how a specific file is rendered or, if you save the config value it in a `_data.*` file, it would affect to all files in that directory.
What do you think?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
yes, having the ability to specify md only or md+template language both globally and at a directory level would solve this particular problem. I found a further problem here yesterday: I set up a couple of njk files as you suggested, which worked, but I then added some md files in the same directory, and it stopped working. I had to put the md files in a separate subdir for it to work again.

I can put other suggested improvements to the flow in separate issues.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Ok, I've added support for the `templateEngine` variable.
Starting from v0.10.0 (not released yet) you can do things like this: https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/custom-template.md (run `njk` first and then `md`) and the result is https://github.com/lumeland/test/blob/master/_expected/custom-template.html
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: yeah, that's much more flexible, thanks. Are you planning on releasing 0.10 soon? If so, I'll wait. Otherwise, I could download the current master and try with that.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: Probably today
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: v0.10.0 released.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I can't get this to work. To simplify, if `mydata` consists of a list of `title` and `no`, in my page file `page.html`, I have:
```
---
layout: layout.html
templateEngine: njk,md
---
{% for title, no in mydata %}
* {{ title }}: [{{ no }}](/items/{{ no }}.html){% endfor %}
```
and I'm ending up with lines of:
```
* title: [no](/items/no.html)
```
where title and no are the correct values for each line.

So, Nunjucks is substituting the data correctly, but the result is not being converted from md into html.

Am I missing something?
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
I just tested your example and it works fine to me:
https://github.com/lumeland/test/commit/4550c5b2c430930004b3d95627636f96a3618c91

Maybe it is something related with cache (again). Try to clean deno cache and reinstall lume again.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: yes, now works; it was indeed the cache. Looks like cache reload does not work properly. ☹️
Comments.Author: probins

I_kwDOEYAvFc5urygD.Title: Share optimized image into several locations if size and format are identical

I_kwDOEYAvFc5urygD.Body: ### Enter your suggestions in details: Let's say I have two components for showing same image: ```html ``` First one resolved into `my-pic-300w.png` and `my-pic-300w@2.png`. Second one into `my-pic-600w.png` and `my-pic-600w@2.png`. I_kwDOEYAvFc5tScOS.Title: copying "Hello World" example renders garbled text
I_kwDOEYAvFc5tScOS.Author: stevietrouble
I_kwDOEYAvFc5tScOS.Body:
### Version 1.18.3 ### Platform Windows ### What steps will reproduce the bug? ``` deno run -Ar https://deno.land/x/lume/init.ts > _config.ts > Maybe Later // Important: Copy-pasted from terminal echo "hello world" > index.md deno task serve ``` ### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition? Every time I try it. ### What is the expected behavior? ```

Hello world

``` ### What do you see instead? ```

��H�e�l�l�o� �w�o�r�l�d� � �

``` ### Additional information Running latest Deno built from source with `cargo install deno --locked` **The issue is that Windows Powershell's echo command outputs text in UTF-16, and Lume reads it as UTF-8.**

Comments.Body:
Thanks for reporting this bug, but I'm not sure if this can be fixed easily. Lume uses [`Deno.readTextFile`](https://deno.land/api@v1.35.3?s=Deno.readTextFile) function to read the text content and, indeed the function description makes it clear that the file is read as UTF-8 decoded string.

I guess this is a very edge case, but l'm leaving this open just in case someone can provide quick and easy solution for that.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: One easy fix could be suggesting the user "create an index.md file" rather than suggesting using echo. Or, having the template start off with the hello world file already added.
Comments.Author: stevietrouble

Comments.Body:
Okay, I just changed the quick start tutorial with your suggestion. It will be available in the next version.
thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5GkzNE.Title: Distribute Lume through asdf plugins
I_kwDOEYAvFc5GkzNE.Author: x80486
I_kwDOEYAvFc5GkzNE.Body:
`asdf` is a CLI tool that can manage multiple language runtime versions on a per-project basis.

While Lume is quite easy to install, sometimes it's desirable to handle multiple versions. I think it might look very similar to the [asdf-deno](https://github.com/asdf-community/asdf-deno) repository.

Comments.Body:
I'm not sure if this is useful. `lume import-map` generates the `import_map.json` and `deno.json` files, so you have the lume version number there. It also creates two tasks in the `deno.json` file, so you can run `deno task build` and `deno task serve` directly, without using the installed lume cli (example: https://github.com/lumeland/lume.land/blob/main/deno.json)

So, in theory you can manage different lume versions just editing these files.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I saw that, and it looks very much like some other platforms are doing, but I think this time `https://deno.land/x/lume@vX.Y.Z/ci.ts` is downloaded all the time for every command?

At any rate, I think this could/should be a community effort instead of being maintained by `Lume` owners — until proven sufficiently useful.

As a side note, in some places people aren't able to be connected all the time.
Comments.Author: x80486

Comments.Body:
> but I think this time https://deno.land/x/lume@vX.Y.Z/ci.ts is downloaded all the time for every command?

It's downloaded only the first time, the next time Deno use the cached version, so you don't need to be connected all the time.

If you want to work on a `asdf` plugin, I'm ok with that, but I don't know what's the advantage over the import map file.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
OK, I think from the moment users can get into any recent version of `Lume` that wouldn't be needed. It's more convenient to have that in the `deno task`s.

Wouldn't that approach retire as well the Docker images? Basically with the proper `Deno` version, `Lume` will be able to run without problems.
Comments.Author: x80486

Comments.Body: Good point. Yes I think `deno task` combined with the `import_map.json` file makes more convenient to run any lume version without install it. Users can install Deno from the official docker image and run lume directly. The only problem is if an outdated deno version is used, but in this case lume shows an error message.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Great! Then at some point we can set the path to start retiring the `Docker` images. I think whenever `Deno` tasks become stable could be the starting point :thinking:
Comments.Author: x80486

I_kwDOEYAvFc5CCfs8.Title: Referring to `page.document` breaks non-HTML file
I_kwDOEYAvFc5CCfs8.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body:
Mmm, good point.
Maybe the `document` property should return something only if the page has `.html` extension.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
aiotter

Comments.Body:
I just committed a change to fix this issue (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/commit/c1666a761c0b27bea480ea44cac8320b8e59e028#diff-1efcf4bbdf6611ee325c25c2f2581b150825745bc0734d67dbe7d03adb003eb4)

Regarding to document fragments, yes it's not already implemented. But what's your specific use case? Why do you want pages without html/body tags?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Nope, I don't have particular case for document without html/body tags. Someone want to provide a fragment of document for embedding purpose? IDK, it's just imagination.
I just worried about modifying something just by referencing. Maybe I'm a big worrier.
If you feel it okay, then go ahead!
Comments.Author: aiotter

Comments.Body:
I understand. A page represents a complete html page of the site, so I don't think this is a common casuistry.
Anyway, with the latest change, only pages with `.html` or `.htm` extensions are parsed, so if anyone want to provide a fragment of a document, a different extension can be used, so the document won't be modified.

I'm closing this. Thanks!!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

MDU6SXNzdWU5MjU0NTkzMTU=.Title: Performance observability
MDU6SXNzdWU5MjU0NTkzMTU=.Author: shah
MDU6SXNzdWU5MjU0NTkzMTU=.Body:
As you start your TypeScript journey (or even before!) I suggest adding some _observability_ across all the major pipeline steps. For example, if I have 100 pages being generated but 5 of them take 95% of time to generate, we should be able to see that when the pages are being tracked. For large sites some parts of site take much longer to generate so the developer should be responsible for caching those pages but she'd need to be told which ones they are.

Something like this?

```bash
🔥 Deno prepare (900ms)
🔥 /images /images (copy: 25ms)
🔥 /index.html /index.tmpl.ts (load: 1025ms, render: 255ms, process: 190ms, etc: ??)
🔥 /styles.css /styles.css (??)

🍾 Site built into ./_site (1255ms)
```

It would be good to build [OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/) and [OpenMetrics](https://openmetrics.io/) (Prometheus) into Lume so that large sites can fine tune performance through observability.

Comments.Body: That's good idea. Deno has support for the performance web API, so it should be something easy to do.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Also @oscarotero when there are thousands of pages, how does one turn off the generated file details list and show only a summary of pages generated? Is that in `_config.js`? I did not see a `--verbose` CLI option either.
Comments.Author: shah

Comments.Body:
@shah I've added performance metrics functionality to Lume. It's not released yet but you can test it installing the last commit with `lume upgrade --dev`.

Runnign `lume --metrics` will display some metrics from the build process. You can save them to a file for better inspection with `lume  --metrics=filename.json`.

And about the `--verbose` option, it's a good idea. I'll add it. 
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Great, thanks @oscarotero - I'd consider adding an `observability` property in `_config.ts` so that it can be controlled from CLI or config. Something like this:

```js
observability: {
    telemetry: { ...what to capture for traceability  },
    metrics: { ...all metrics options  },
}
```
Comments.Author: shah
oscarotero

Comments.Body: I just added a `--verbose` option. [More info](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero here's what I see with `--metrics` please LMK if I'm doing something incorrectly?

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/47497/122679509-267fd980-d1b9-11eb-8a87-7a0ce427082e.png)
Comments.Author: shah
shah

Comments.Body: I think it's something related with Deno's cache. Try to remove the cache folder. I had the same problem and after remove the cache it works fine.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: btw, `ignore` doesn't support patterns like `*.code-workspace`. Just real files or directories.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: mmm, no it's not the cache. I have to investigate more
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Yep, it's not the cache, same output after clearing caches.
I'll add a ticket to add support globs or `*.code-workspace` style for ignore.
Comments.Author: shah

Comments.Body: there's already a ticket (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/issues/53) but it's not easy to support for performance reasons.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Also, while you're checking the `--verbose=0` or `--verbose=1` or `--verbose=2` did not seem to change any behavior.
Comments.Author: shah

Comments.Body: I think there's a problem detecting these new options (metrics and verbose) in --dev installation.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Got it, no worries - you can put it in with the next release. Thanks!
Comments.Author: shah

Comments.Body:
I know what's happens. You have to import the same lume module defined in the import_map. I mean, this works:

```js
import lume from "lume/mod.js";
```

But this doesn't work:

```js
import lume from "./my-lume-fork/mod.js";
```

Because the `mod.js` module is not the same as the one used by cli, so the options passed to cli aren't applied to the final Lume instance.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
shah

Comments.Body:
Import maps are configured automatically by `lume upgrade` command. So they are always available, just need to change your deps.ts to use them.
Anyway, I'll try to change the way the cli arguments are applied to the final Lume instance for better portability.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
shah

Comments.Body: Ok, but note that on importing the dependencies statically, `lume upgrade [--dev]` won't work because you are stuck to a fixed version of lume.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Agreed, I use `udd` to update Deno dependencies and I'll have to be careful about that and switch to import map manually. Having something special for `upgrade --dev` makes sense as long import maps aren't required for non-devl versions.
Comments.Author: shah

Comments.Body: Overall the metrics look good now but we should create the output in more useful formats, starting in OpenMetrics (which very close to Prometheus exposition format or PEF). By emitting in both JSON and OpenMetrics/PEF we'll be able to use proper metrics ingestion systems for alerting. When you're ready to implement, [this is a good Deno library](https://github.com/base698/promts) as is [this one](https://github.com/marcopacini/ts_prometheus) to use. If we need ability to track Deno metrics we can also check out [this library](https://deno.land/x/observability).
Comments.Author: shah
oscarotero

Comments.Body: Great point @oscarotero and I _completely_ agree that everything should be a plugin whenever possible. _Collecting_ the metrics and providing observability _capabilities_ (e.g. traceability) should be in the core. I love the idea of _exporting_ the observability to different formats/file with events in `config.ts` or plugins.
Comments.Author: shah

I_kwDOEYAvFc6Ly_ox.Title: How to use "lume-code" and "Tip caution..." in "tailwind typography"

### Enter your suggestions in details:

![捕4获](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/assets/45355403/e5dd1d52-7ae4-45de-9d44-9732894a4926)
![捕获](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/assets/45355403/e81a5c6b-85bf-4030-ae64-54d35df4822c)

Comments.Body:
Captions and `lume-core` are not part of Lume. They have been created specifically for their use cases.

Captions are generated with this plugin for markdown-it: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mdit/plugin-alert and the styles are applied here: https://github.com/lumeland/ds/blob/main/src/components/body.css#L65-L116

I don't know if tailwind has a plugin for that, but you can copy/paste these styles (or create others by your own) if you want.

`lume-code` is a web component I created for lume.land site. The css code is here https://github.com/lumeland/lume.land/blob/main/_includes/styles/components/code.css and I have a processor to change the HTML code in the build here https://github.com/lumeland/lume.land/blob/main/_config.ts#L66

I_kwDOEYAvFc541Y8Z.Title: nunjucks include in for loop breaks build
I_kwDOEYAvFc541Y8Z.Author: allanberry
I_kwDOEYAvFc541Y8Z.Body:
### Version v1.19.3 ### Platform Macintosh ### What steps will reproduce the bug? Hi there. I can't build my site when I have includes (SVG) within a Nunjucks `for` loop. The problem seems to be when the `include` is within certain Nunjucks tags. For example, this builds OK ```njk {% include "test.svg" %} {% for item in ["a", "b", "c"] %}

{{item}}

{% endfor %} ``` But this does not: ```njk {% for item in ["a", "b", "c"] %} {% include "test.svg" %}

{{item}}

{% endfor %} ``` `if` tags also are broken: ```njk {% if true %} {% include "test.svg" %}

True

{% endif %} ``` but `block` tags seem to work **OK**: ```njk {% block test %} {% include "test.svg" %}

Test

{% endblock %} ``` ### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition? This happens each time I try and build my site with `deno task build`. It also happens when using `deno task serve`, but _it resolves itself after the first build_: if you edit the Nunjucks file, when Lume re-renders the HTML, the include is rendered OK. It will continue to render correctly until you stop the server and run either `deno task build` or `deno task serve` again. ### What is the expected behavior? The SVG should render. ### What do you see instead? The SVG silently fails to render, and the rendered HTML gets borked. ### Additional information It seems to me there's some sort of (async?) race condition, where the initial build can't find the include, but on subsequent renderings it already has the data, so it renders. Perhaps this is due to asynchronous template rendering. Nunjucks has an [`asyncEach` tag](https://mozilla.github.io/nunjucks/templating.html#asynceach) for async `for` rendering, but I tried it and it didn't work. Thanks very much, great software. Cheers.

Comments.Body:
Nunjucks is primary a sync template engine, but Lume needs to load the files in async. This is why in Lume 2 the default template engine will be [Vento](https://vento.js.org/), that works much better with async code.

For Nunjucks, the workaround is using `asyncEach / endeach` and `ifasync / endif`. If that don't work for you, make sure you don't have anything wrong because it always worked fine to me. This is an example: https://github.com/lumeland/theme-simple-blog/blob/1211528b0f4ea1364d1856e6531a53898a17e074/src/_includes/templates/post-list.njk#L2
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Thanks @oscarotero!  I appreciate your attention.  I tried `asyncEach / endeach` and it worked OK this time, I guess I did something wrong before.  :/

Looking forward to Vento!
Comments.Author: allanberry

I_kwDOEYAvFc6HA5fw.Title: Lume failse to build/serve with atypical Linux directory structure.
I_kwDOEYAvFc6HA5fw.Author: computerscience-person
I_kwDOEYAvFc6HA5fw.Body:
### Version

2.1.4

### Platform

Linux (NixOS)

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

1. Setup Lume (`deno run -A https://lume.land/init.ts`
2. Run lume task (`deno task lume`)

Error:

```
Task lume echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run -A - "--" "version"
Loading config file
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::filesystem::__cxx11::filesystem_error'
  what():  filesystem error: recursive directory iterator cannot open directory: No such file or directory [/usr/share/fonts]
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Probably required by lume to load fonts.

### What is the expected behavior?

_No response_

### What do you see instead?

```
Task lume echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run -A - "--" "version"
Loading config file file:///mnt/Shtuffs/home/Dev/CCS222/final/_config.ts
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::filesystem::__cxx11::filesystem_error'
  what():  filesystem error: recursive directory iterator cannot open directory: No such file or directory [/usr/share/fonts]
```

### Additional information

NixOS doesn't follow a typical Linux filesystem, and thus, when Lume tries to load fonts from `/usr/share/fonts`, it errors. Maybe I missed a configuration option to change default font directory?

Comments.Body:
Hi.
I'm not familiarized with NixOS filesystem. It seems the project directory is `file:///mnt/Shtuffs/home/Dev/CCS222/final/`. Why is trying to read the `/usr/share/fonts` directory? Is there any symbolic link to this folder?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Probably to look for system/user fonts, I'd imagine.
Comments.Author: computerscience-person

Comments.Body:
> Probably to look for system/user fonts, I'd imagine.

I may have missed it in the documentation, but it would be nice if there were a way to configure where lume looks for fonts.
Comments.Author: computerscience-person

Comments.Body: Lume doesn't look for fonts. It only processes the files in the `src` directory of your project. Can I see your _config.ts file? Maybe there's a plugin or something that want to access to that folder
Comments.Author: oscarotero
computerscience-person

Comments.Body: It was a shiraha problem, not lume.

I_kwDOEYAvFc5Zvv6u.Title: Fresh lume site fails: "could not find npm package markdown-it-attrs"
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Zvv6u.Author: Sam-Spencer
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Zvv6u.Body:
### Version

1.14.2

### Platform

macOS

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

 1. Install the latest stable version of node (18.12.1)
 2. Create an empty directory
 3. Follow the instructions to get started with Luke **exactly** as they are provided in the docs
 4. Use a typescript config
 5. Do not add any plugins
 6. Create your first page: `index.md` (again, as stated in the docs)
 7. Run `deno task serve` (as per the docs)
 8. The following error prints in the terminal:

> error: Could not find npm package 'markdown-it-attrs' matching 4.1.6. Try retrieving the latest npm package information by running with --reload


### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Every time

### What is the expected behavior?

Should be able to initialize a new lime site by following the provided instructions

### What do you see instead?

There is an ambiguous npm package error:

> error: Could not find npm package 'markdown-it-attrs' matching 4.1.6. Try retrieving the latest npm package information by running with --reload

Interestingly, that specific version of that specific npm package *does* exist and was published 10 days ago (at time of writing).

### Additional information

_No response_

Comments.Body:
Hi, @Sam-Spencer 
This is a bug from Deno, related with how they cache the NPM packages. (See https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16901)

There are two possible solutions:

- Purge the Deno's cache folder (run `deno info` to get the cache path)
- Or run `echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run --unstable -A --reload -` (The same command of `deno task lume` but with the `--reload` flag). This will refresh the Deno's cache.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
The following  ` echo "import 'lume/cli.ts'" | deno run --unstable -A --reload -` command does not work for me, then I run the following command, it will be fine for me.

```bash
 deno cache --reload ./_config.ts 
```
Comments.Author: officialrajdeepsingh

Comments.Body: This was fixed in Deno. So I'm closing this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

MDU6SXNzdWU3NzAxMjg0ODU=.Title: Nunjucks for js files
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzAxMjg0ODU=.Author: probins
MDU6SXNzdWU3NzAxMjg0ODU=.Body:
In #11, I mentioned being able to use templates with js files, so I can for example populate an array from shared data. You can do this In Jekyll (and can define `layout: null` for non-html files). I hoped that if I specified a `_data.json` with `templateEngine: njk` it would work, but it ignores the directory. If I rename the js file to `*.njk`, then it creates an output html, but doesn't parse the templates.

In the particular case I'm working on, I can get around this by including the js in the html file, but that's not very efficient if I have several html pages that use the same js.

Comments.Body:
I'm not sure to understand your case. Can I see an example?
Note that `.js` files are not interpreted as pages (they could be javascript that you want to execute in frontend). You need to use the extension `.tmpl.js`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
probins

Comments.Body:
Understood.

lume ignores all files with extensions not recognized by any template engine. So, for example `page.foo`, or `document.doc` are ignored (you can copy them but not render as a page).

In theory, if you save the file as `my.js.njk`, it should be loaded and rendered by njk, remove the `njk` extension and save it as `my.js`. This is not what happens now (it's saved as `my.js.html` because the html extension is added always), but I'm going to release a new version with this fixed.

Other option is include a frontmatter with the permalink value, so you can decide the exact output name of this file. Create a `my.njk` file with this content:

```
---
permalink: my.js
---
let apis = {};
{% for type, key in apikeys %}
apis["{{ type }}"] = "{{ key }}";
{% endfor %}
```

Or even shorted

```
---
permalink: my.js
---
let apis = {{ apiKeys | dump }};
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
ok, thanks - I'll try this out this afternoon. (Thanks for reminding me of `dump`, too; Jekyll's default templating has `jsonify` which is much the same.)

To give you some context, I used to use PHP servers, and used this sort of templating a lot. However, for sites where the data doesn't change very often, static site generators will do just as well. I started off using Github Pages, which was limited to Jekyll, but I switched to GitLab a few years ago, where I can run anything. Deno/Lume is way faster than Jekyll: the site I'm working on took several minutes with Jekyll, but <1 minute with Lume, and most of that is loading the Docker image.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Oh, interesting! Thanks for let me know :)

I did some performance tests some time ago (using this repo: https://github.com/seancdavis/ssg-build-performance-tests) but I think there's plenty room for improvements yet.

FYI, Yesterday I've released a new version that allows to have support for `my.js.njk` file, so you don't need to create a frontmatter with the permalink value (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md#0102---2020-12-17).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: this doesn't seem to work. I renamed the file to `xxx.js.njk`, and changed the code to `let apis = {{ apiKeys | dump | safe }};` (it's json, so has to be 'safe', otherwise I get a lot of `"`). It correctly renames the file to `xxx.js`, but outputs `let apis = ;`. This time it isn't a problem with the cache, as I deleted the cache and re-ran - but same problem.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: oh, and another problem is that it wraps it in the default layout file. I could get around this by creating an empty layout. Jekyll has the option `layout: null`.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: I'll take a look.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I just tested your use case and it works fine to me.

In this directory: https://github.com/lumeland/test/tree/master/pages

There's a `_data.yaml` file with a default layout and some variables. And the `styles.css.njk` file outputs a css file with these variables and override the layout value with `null`. Here you can see the result: https://github.com/lumeland/test/tree/master/_expected/pages

So I suspect something is wrong in your side.

Comments.Body: yes, sorry, my bad. I had the js in a new dir, and forgot to copy `_data` over. Duh! Works fine, including `layout: null`. 👍
