MDU6SXNzdWU3NjY4OTIwNTc=.Title: Outward Facing Types
MDU6SXNzdWU3NjY4OTIwNTc=.Author: shadowtime2000
MDU6SXNzdWU3NjY4OTIwNTc=.Body: Deno has builtin Typescript support so I was a little surprised to see this written in JS. I can understand not wanting to use Typescript, but I think this should have files like the plugins and the site written in typescript so people can use ts if they want it without writing there own types. We could then keep the internals of it written in JS. I could do this.

Comments.Body: Alternatively we could do a `.d.ts` for every file that should be used externally, though that could be a little harder to maintain with new features and stuff.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body:
I'm not a big fan of Typescript but understand that for some people it's useful. Is required to be lume in Typescript to create 3rd party stuff in Typescript?

All the public api is in the `site.js` file (it has all functions to configure, register plugins, loaders, template engines, etc).
Is the file `site.d.ts` enough? Or we need to create a `.d.ts` for every file?

Is possible to have the types as jsdocs?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero 

> Is possible to have the types as jsdocs?

[Types as JSDocs is available in the Node Typescript version](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/type-checking-javascript-files.html) though I don't believe it works in Deno. Also, I believe the JSDoc typings are only used internally and aren't used to type check when someone imports it outside of the project (possible wrong here).

> All the public api is in the `site.js` file (it has all functions to configure, register plugins, loaders, template engines, etc).
Is the file `site.d.ts` enough? Or we need to create a `.d.ts` for every file?

A `site.d.ts` could work, but it would require you to put a comment like this above the import i believe
```js
//@deno-types="https://cdn.jsdelivr.com/github/lumeland/lume/site.d.ts"
```
Since all the plugins are closures, I am assuming that is so because later options and configuration support would be added to them. In that case there would need to be a `.d.ts` for every plugin file, and they would have to write (in my opinion an ugly) comment above the imports. So if a somewhat advanced static site used all builtin plugins (12 atm), they would have to write 12 of those comments above every plugin import, and another for the site import.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body:
Ok, understood.
So there are two options to add typings: by creating a bunch of `.d.ts` files or rewriting the whole library to Typescript.
At this moment, I don't want to do any of them. `.d.ts` requires a lot of work and maintenance (and I agree that it's ugly). And rewrite all lume to Typescript requires work too. Note that this library uses some dependencies converted directly from jsdelivr, jspm and esm.sh. I think Typescript can introduce more complexity and I'd like to keep things simple.

Maybe, in a future `1.0` version, when all dependencies are loaded from deno.land, a Typescript version makes more sense.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @oscarotero Or we could convert the `site.js` and the plugins to `.ts` and put in an `any` or an `unknown` for internal stuff.
Comments.Author: shadowtime2000

Comments.Body: Related #104
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Ok, finally lume [0.25.0](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/releases/tag/v0.25.0) is fully written in Typescript.
So I can close this.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5x550O.Title: Ordering of `source` elements generated by the picture plugin
I_kwDOEYAvFc5x550O.Author: cgwrench
I_kwDOEYAvFc5x550O.Body: ### Enter your suggestions in details: I'm trying to use the picture plugin to add support for serving multiple image formats. I've found that the order of `source` elements isn't ideal. With the following in my `_config.ts`: ```typescript site.use(picture()); site.use(imagick({ extensions: [".webp", ".jpg"] })); ``` I've added two images to a markdown file: ```markdown ``` Note that the order of the formats in the `imagick` attribute differ. I also don't have any `imagick` shared data, so the imagick plugin shouldn't do anything other that what's set up by the picture plugin. This generates the following output: ```html ``` For the first image, the JPEG format is included twice: once in a `source` element, and once in an `img` element. However, the `img` element refers to the original image, not the transformed image. As this isn't generated or copied into the build folder, this `img` returns a 404 (but the first `source` element is selected and displayed). Also, the `webp`, included in the second `source` element format will never get used, as browsers that support this format also support JPEGs, so the first `source` element is always used. The order of source elements is fine for the second image, however, this still has the issue that the `src` for the `img` element doesn't exist. Also, I think it would be preferable for this `src` to point to the generate JPEG, since this would have broader browser support. The seems picture plugin adds `source` elements for each format defined in the `imagick` attribute, but it also copies the original `img` as-is. I think it would be better for the plugin to disregard the original `img` element and select one of the formats in the `imagick` attribute to be used as the source for a new `img` element that is generated along with the `source` elements. There are a couple of things that would need a little care here: 1. If generating a new `img` element, which source format should be used? It might make sense to specify an ordered list of preferred `img` source formats (something like `["jpeg", "png", "webp", "avif"]`, perhaps). The first matching source should be used. If there are multiple sizes, then I'd suggest the largest image size is selected. 2. If generating a new `img` element, we'd loose any attributes on the original `img` element. It might make sense to introduce a `copyAttributes` option, like the one provided by the inline plugin. Rather than creating a new `img` element, an alternative approach might be to update the `src` for the original `img` element to point to a generated image. Note that what I've described above is for the case of a single image with. I think it's correct to still include a `source` for all image formats, even the one used for the `img` element, if there are multiple sizes in play. However, in the example above, there's an unnecessary `source` element. I hope this example illustrates the issue I've seen. Please let me know if you need any more information. --- As an aside, I did try to use an attribute of `imagick="webp jpg"`, i.e. without a width specified, as all I'm trying to do is generate multiple image formats, and I'd like to retain the original sizes of images. I don't think this is currently supported by the picture plugin. Is that correct?

Comments.Body: Hi @cgwrench Thanks for the detailed explanation. Indeed, the picture plugin generates the `source` elements in the same order as the formats defined in the `imagick` attribute. It's possible to reorder the formats from the most modern format (avif, jxl) to the oldest format (jpeg) and provide an option to configure a different order if desired. The image in the img probably should be the latest image in the priority list. For example: ```php ``` The last image in the priority list should be the best supported format (jpg in this example) so it should be the image choosed for the img, to ensure old browsers not supporting the `picture` element will be able to display this image. And about the lack of width values, I'll take a look. The `@1` pixel density should be added automatically.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thanks @oscarotero, and thanks for such a quick response. In your example: ```html ``` I don't think the `source` element with `srcset="/assets/images/test2-600w.jpg"` is needed - browsers would just fallback to the same image in the `img` if that `source` element wasn't there. In the interest of minimising the markup generated, would it be possible to omit a `source` element for the source used in the `img` element? This `source` element would be needed if there were multiple sizes for the JPEG, but in the example I provided, I'm only interested in generating multiple formats, rather than multiple sizes. I appreciate that any change to this plugin would need to support both use cases though.
Comments.Author: cgwrench

Comments.Body: Okay, I'll work on that in order to have it ready for the incoming Lume 1.19
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: @cgwrench Can you try it now? Upgrade Lume to the latests development version `deno task lume upgrade --dev`
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero That works an absolute treat. Thanks for such a quick turn around!

One think I did notice is that if I have some imagick plugin configuration in a global _data.yml file like:

```yml
imagick:
    format: [jpg, webp]
```

I get an error:

> A page will overwrite another page. Use distinct `url` values to resolve the conflict.

Is that expected? If so, no worries. I just expecting that the configurations set by the picture plugin and imagick plugins would work together, rather than clash like this.
Comments.Author: cgwrench

Comments.Body: Hmm, this is because the image is exported twice, one by the imagick plugin and other by the picture. I just fixed it by removing the duplicated transformations (two transformations with the same format/extension and suffix).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc6lS64V.Title: Relative Import Path of package dependencies throw error on build
I_kwDOEYAvFc6lS64V.Author: tspears1
I_kwDOEYAvFc6lS64V.Body:
### Version

2.4.3

### Platform

OSX

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

I'm using the `radix-ui` library for building a React client-side app on my site with esbuild to compile. I've tried adding these packages with ESM and NPM and in both cases, if the package has dependencies `lume-loader` throws an error like the following for `@radix-ui/react-dialog`:

`deno:file:///Users/user/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/@radix-ui/react-dialog/1.1.4/dist/index.mjs:16:29:ERROR: [plugin: lume-loader] Relative import path "react-remove-scroll" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../ and not in import map from "https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.4.3/plugins/esbuild.ts"`

I even have those particularly directories ignored by LUME however it seems they're still being processed. I've gotten around it a few times by adding the package dependency to the import map, but its a tedious process and not always successful. Any suggestions?

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

_No response_

### What is the expected behavior?

Libraries to be imported without conflict.

### What do you see instead?

```bash
Loading config file file:///Users/user/Sites/project/_config.js
✘ [ERROR] Relative import path "react-remove-scroll" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../ and not in import map from "https://deno.land/x/lume@v2.4.3/plugins/esbuild.ts" [plugin lume-loader]

    deno:file:///Users/user/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/@radix-ui/react-dialog/1.1.4/dist/index.mjs:16:29:
      16 │ import { RemoveScroll } from "react-remove-scroll";
```


### Additional information

_No response_

Comments.Body:
Yes, this is due a bug (or a behavior change, I don't know) of Deno.
I've reported to them here but didn't get any response yet: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/27436

I'll try to find another way to resolve npm dependencies in Deno.
Meanwhile, can you try importing the package from `esm.sh`? 

```js
import radix from "https://esm.sh/radix-ui";
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
That's very helpful to know! I'll keep an eye on that thread. (and thanks for the quick reply)

SO with ESM, if I mark it as external (`"@radix-ui/react-dialog": "https://esm.sh/v135/*@radix-ui/react-dialog@1.1.4",`), I get the same issue as with NPM.

If I dont mark it as external I keep getting errors like `Cannot read properties of null (reading 'useState')` which seems to be coming from a conflict of different version of React being used. (though for the life of me I can't tell where. everything is using React 18.3.1 yet React 19 keeps popping up in my deno.lock file.)
Comments.Author: tspears1

Comments.Body:
I suspect this bug won't be fixed anytime soon, because they didn't respond in 3 weeks.

I've trying to fix the plugin using the [import_map](https://github.com/denoland/import_map) package instead of relying on Deno's `import.meta.resolve()` function.

This has the benefit of defining a custom import map for the esbuild, independent of the `deno.json` file.

Can you try it now? It's available in the latest development version of Lume. You can upgrade it with `deno task lume upgrade --dev`.

If it doesn't work and you have a public repo with your code, I can take a look.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero - I think that solved it, at least for ESM imports. (Still running into some issues with React conflicting, but I think those are probably ME problems lol and nothing to do with Lume.)

There may still be an issue with `npm` when getting subfolders though. With `esm` you can solve it with a second import map entry. So for `import ReactDOMServer from "react-dom/server"` you need the following.
```
"react-dom": "https://esm.sh/react-dom@18.3.1",
"react-dom/": "https://esm.sh/react-dom@18.3.1/",
```
Which if you use the ESM CLI, it actually adds both automatically.
If you try to do that with the npm, you run into the same problem still.
Comments.Author: tspears1

Comments.Body:
If you want to take a look you can see the repo here: https://github.com/tspears1/projects.bu.edu
I just pushed a new branch `update-lume` with the new dev version.
Comments.Author: tspears1

Comments.Body:
Ok, I've commited new changes to Lume (you can run `deno task lume upgrade --dev` again, to fetch the latest version).

- npm specifiers don't need to add the trailing slash. Only http specifiers need that. For example this works:
  ```
  "react-dom": "npm:react-dom@18.3.1"
  ```
  (no need to add `"react-dom/"`)
- Now, changing all dependencies to `npm` makes the build to work and there are no errors. I have the following:
   ```json
   "@radix-ui": "npm:@radix-ui@1.0.1",
    "@radix-ui/react-dialog": "npm:@radix-ui/react-dialog@1.1.4",
    "@radix-ui/react-dropdown-menu": "npm:@radix-ui/react-dropdown-menu@2.1.4",
    "@radix-ui/react-separator": "npm:@radix-ui/react-separator@1.1.1",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "npm:@radix-ui/react-slot@1.1.1",
    "@radix-ui/react-tooltip": "npm:@radix-ui/react-tooltip@1.1.6",
    "classnames": "npm:classnames@2.5.1",
    "react-dom": "npm:react-dom@18.3.1",
    "react-inlinesvg": "npm:react-inlinesvg@4.1.5",
    "react": "npm:react@18.3.1"
   ```
- But the app doesn't work (there are errors in the browser). Some components work fine, others not. Maybe the error is caused by some dependency that is not correctly bundled by esm.sh. I don't know.
- Note that esm [accept some configuration](https://lume.land/plugins/esbuild/#esm). For example, you can configure the dependencies of some packages, and this could fix errors like having different react versions. 
- I can't help more at this point. If you discover the dependency that is causing the error and there is something that I can do in Lume, please let me know.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Thanks @oscarotero - this is all extremely helpful and I can take it from here. 
The fix works great for getting the build to work. I'll chip away at the React issue - I'll let you know if I find anything related to Lume.

Appreciate all your help - loving Lume and the Lume CMS!
Comments.Author: tspears1

Comments.Body:
I've been playing with your repo a bit more and discover that esm.sh mixes React 18 and 19.
It seems to be a bug on esm.sh and i've opened an issue here: https://github.com/esm-dev/esm.sh/issues/994

Maybe this is why JS breaks in the browser.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Ah! This is what I suspected but wasn't able to prove yet. Yeah I think that's right. React 19 keeps popping up in my `deno.lock`. Thanks for opening that ticket - I'll keep an eye out.
Comments.Author: tspears1

I_kwDOEYAvFc5Su4eb.Title: Imagick plugin: Allow `format` property to accept an array
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Su4eb.Author: paulrobertlloyd
I_kwDOEYAvFc5Su4eb.Body:
Currently, `format` only allows a single value. If you want to output multiple formats, you need to declare separate transformations

```yml
imagick:
  - resize: [200, 200]
    suffix: -small
    format: jpg
  - resize: [200, 200]
    suffix: -small
    format: webp
```

To simplify the configuration of different image transformations, it would be helpful if the `format` property could accept an array. This would allow the above to be rewritten as follows:

```yml
imagick:
  - resize: [200, 200]
    suffix: -small
    format:
      - jpg
      - webp
```

Comments.Body:
That's a good idea. I'll work on it.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc696Nrr.Title: Access to All Environment Variables Is Required
I_kwDOEYAvFc696Nrr.Author: foster-hangdaan
I_kwDOEYAvFc696Nrr.Body:
### Version

3.0.4

### Platform

Linux

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

Running the development server using the following command:

```sh
deno run --allow-write='.' --allow-read='.' --allow-net='deno.land,cdn.deno.land,esm.sh,0.0.0.0:3000,localhost:3000,jsr.io,lumeland.github.io,code.hangdaan.com' --allow-import=code.hangdaan.com,deno.land,jsr.io,cdn.jsdelivr.net --allow-sys=networkInterfaces --allow-env=JSR_URL lume.ts -s
```

As well as building the site:

```sh
deno run --allow-write='.' --allow-read='.' --allow-net='deno.land,cdn.deno.land,esm.sh,0.0.0.0:3000,localhost:3000,jsr.io,lumeland.github.io,code.hangdaan.com' --allow-import=code.hangdaan.com,deno.land,jsr.io,cdn.jsdelivr.net --allow-sys=networkInterfaces --allow-env=JSR_URL lume.ts
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Everytime.

### What is the expected behavior?

To take advantage of Deno's security features, Lume should require the minimum amount of permissions. Building the site, and running the development server, should work **without** granting permission to all environment variables with the `--allow-env` flag.

### What do you see instead?

Lume will ask for permission to access all environment variables:

```
┏  Deno requests env access.
┠─ Requested by `Deno.permissions.request()` API.
┠─ To see a stack trace for this prompt, set the DENO_TRACE_PERMISSIONS environmental variable.
┠─ Learn more at: https://docs.deno.com/go/--allow-env
┠─ Run again with --allow-env to bypass this prompt.
┗ Allow? [y/n/A] (y = yes, allow; n = no, deny; A = allow all env permissions) >
```

Denying the access request results in an error:

```
x Denied env access.
Loading config file file:///home/user/projects/website/_config.ts
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Module not found "https://deno.land/x/lume@v3.0.4/jsx-runtime".
    at https://deno.land/x/lume@v3.0.4/deps/ssx.ts:1:33
    const mod = await import(url);
                ^
    at async createSite (https://deno.land/x/lume@v3.0.4/cli/utils.ts:26:17)
    at async buildSite (https://deno.land/x/lume@v3.0.4/cli/utils.ts:45:16)
```

### Additional information

- Deno Version: 2.2.13
- I'm updating from Lume 2.5.3 which did not require access to all environment variables.
- This issue can be reproduced using [my repo](https://code.hangdaan.com/foster/website/src/commit/a6d4271d898dcef0f324e2f2cb9489917fccdf9f).
- This seems to be a regression of #551.

Comments.Body:
Okay, that's fair.

The `env` Lume version sent a request to access to the environment variables [here.](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/00eb7e28f9bd0475a4e9ea62f31ffaedac8f85c5/core/utils/env.ts#L21)

I just removed this and now it only checks if it has access but doesn't request access ([new changes here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/main/core/utils/env.ts#L36)).

If you want to test it, upgrade Lume to the latest development version editing the lume import to this:

```json
"lume/": "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/gh/lumeland/lume@9b09a8e2089f75ae9949ad541e65ba0147a72959/",
```

The error when the permissions are denied is not related with that. It's caused by the JSX configuration that has changed in Lume 3 (it no longer use React or Preact, [more info](https://lume.land/blog/posts/lume-3/#one-jsx-plugin)).

To fix it, add the following import in your deno.json file:

```json
"lume/jsx-runtime": "https://deno.land/x/ssx@v0.1.10/jsx-runtime.ts",
```
and configure the compiler to use `lume` as the JSX import source:


```json
"compilerOptions": {
    "jsx": "react-jsx",
    "jsxImportSource": "lume",
    "types": [
      "lume/types.ts"
    ]
  },
```

Other changed needed in Lume 3:
- Rename `index.tsx` to `index.page.tsx` (Lume 3 requires the `.page` subextension in jsx pages)
- Add `site.add([".scss"])` to the config.ts file (Lume 3 requires to [add the assets](https://lume.land/blog/posts/lume-3/#plugins-no-longer-load-files-automatically) explicitly)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> Okay, that's fair.
> 
> The `env` Lume version sent a request to access to the environment variables [here.](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/00eb7e28f9bd0475a4e9ea62f31ffaedac8f85c5/core/utils/env.ts#L21)
> 
> I just removed this and now it only checks if it has access but doesn't request access ([new changes here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/main/core/utils/env.ts#L36)).
> 
> If you want to test it, upgrade Lume to the latest development version editing the lume import to this:
> 
> "lume/": "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/gh/lumeland/lume@9b09a8e2089f75ae9949ad541e65ba0147a72959/",

I have tested on my end and can confirm that latest dev version above has fixed the permission issue.

> The error when the permissions are denied is not related with that. It's caused by the JSX configuration that has changed in Lume 3 (it no longer use React or Preact, [more info](https://lume.land/blog/posts/lume-3/#one-jsx-plugin)).
> 
> To fix it, add the following import in your deno.json file:
> 
> "lume/jsx-runtime": "https://deno.land/x/ssx@v0.1.10/jsx-runtime.ts",
> 
> and configure the compiler to use `lume` as the JSX import source:
> 
> "compilerOptions": {
>     "jsx": "react-jsx",
>     "jsxImportSource": "lume",
>     "types": [
>       "lume/types.ts"
>     ]
>   },
> 
> Other changed needed in Lume 3:
> 
>     * Rename `index.tsx` to `index.page.tsx` (Lume 3 requires the `.page` subextension in jsx pages)
> 
>     * Add `site.add([".scss"])` to the config.ts file (Lume 3 requires to [add the assets](https://lume.land/blog/posts/lume-3/#plugins-no-longer-load-files-automatically) explicitly)

Thank you. I'm no longer getting the error after making the above changes and consulting the [Lume 3 Migration Guide](https://lume.land/docs/advanced/migrate-to-lume3/).
Comments.Author: foster-hangdaan

I_kwDOEYAvFc5TqpMt.Title: Remove `page.dest`.
I_kwDOEYAvFc5TqpMt.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5TqpMt.Body:
Currently, the `Page` class has the `dest` property with info about the destination.

The `dest` value must be in sync with the `url` value. For example, a page with the url `/foo/` will have the following dest:

```js
{
  path: "/foo/index",
  ext: ".html"
}
```

This is why we have the `page.updateDest()` function, that change not only the `dest` value of the page but also regenerate the url  accordingly.

The problem is this only work by changing the `dest` property, but not the `url`. If the `url` of a page is changed directly, the `dest` keeps the old value.

I think `dest` is a redundant value. It should be calculated only at saving time, according to the page's `url`. So we have a single source of truth that processors and preprocessors can change at any time.

Comments.Body:
@oscarotero How about the other way around? Meaning, if `page.data.url` is never stored, only calculated. Maybe with a "getter" which references `data.dest`.

My reasoning is twofold:
1. Lume's primary purpose is to generate a website, and write it to disk. It _can_ be used as a server, but it's really a static-site _generator_.
2. It sounds easier to tell plugins to edit `page.dest`, which is always either a `string` or `false`. If plugins try editing `page.data.url`, they might get a function by accident, since the user can specify a function for `page.data.url`.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body:
It's more difficult and probably confusing for the users.

- People are used to define the `url` variable and use this variable in the templates. `page.dest` is an internal value.
- Getters for dynamic values are problematic in some template engines (like nunjucks). 
- The functions of the `url` values are invoked very early, plugins never get the function.

I'm aware of the multitype problem of `url`. Ideally it should allow only `string` and maybe allowing `false` was a bad idea. I'm thinking of removing this in Lume 2.0 and use a plugin or something to filter the pages that you don't want to export.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Done in v2 branch
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5vWiHT.Title: Support content blocks?
I_kwDOEYAvFc5vWiHT.Author: mlanza
I_kwDOEYAvFc5vWiHT.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

Nunjucks has a feature called blocks.  It's useful when you have different sections of a certain page type.  Without blocks you can inline your markdown in the yaml.  

```yaml
credits: |
  * <label>Designers</label>: Michael Kiesling & Wolfgang Kramer
  * <label>Illustrators</label>: Paul Mafayon & Christophe Swal
```

Inlining in the yaml is fine with small bits of content but is awkward when you have several sections each with substantial content.  Blocks are better for this.

```md
{% block credits %}
* <label>Designers</label>: Michael Kiesling & Wolfgang Kramer
* <label>Illustrators</label>: Paul Mafayon & Christophe Swal
{% endblock %}
```

The blocks feature exists in Nunjucks but it didn't seem to work for me, so I wasn't sure if Lume as a wrapper implements it.  I didn't see any examples taking advantage of blocks, even in the other templating languages.  

I saw it rejected in #314 but I wasn't sure if it was the feature as a whole or a user-specific issue, since it seems relatively important when it comes to structured content writing.  Maybe one of the other templating languages supports the concept?  With the current design, I have pages with 3-4 sections in which I have to jam much of the prose into the front matter.  I was looking at it and it didn't seem right my content should live up in the yaml.

Comments.Body:
Blocks cannot be used with the Lume layouts because it's not possible to pass the blocks defined by one template to other template. For example:

```njk
---
layout: main.njk
---

{% block credits %}
* <label>Designers</label>: Michael Kiesling & Wolfgang Kramer
* <label>Illustrators</label>: Paul Mafayon & Christophe Swal
{% endblock %}
```

Lume cannot get the `credits` block and pass to `main.njk` (at least I wasn't able to find a way to do that).
But you can use the nunjucks native layouts, that does that:

```njk
{% extends "main.njk" %}

{% block credits %}
* <label>Designers</label>: Michael Kiesling & Wolfgang Kramer
* <label>Illustrators</label>: Paul Mafayon & Christophe Swal
{% endblock %}
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thank you.
Comments.Author: mlanza

MDU6SXNzdWU3MTgyOTg3MTY=.Title: How to generate output in same directory structure as src?
MDU6SXNzdWU3MTgyOTg3MTY=.Author: probins
MDU6SXNzdWU3MTgyOTg3MTY=.Body: I'm wanting to convert a simple metalsmith site to run on deno, and Lume looks like a reasonable fit. One thing I'm stuck on is the structure of the output site. I want to convert `src/abc.md` to `site/abc.html` and `src/xyz.md` to `site/xyz.html`, but it puts each src file in its own directory, which I don't want. I tried using the `permalink` variable, but this makes no difference. Seems to be in `site.js` but I can't see any way to define this.

Comments.Body:
Hi.
Yes, Lume creates pretty urls, so if you export a page as `/about-us`, the real filename is `/about-us/index.html`. But I understand that this is not always desirable. A quick fix is adding the `.html` extension to the permalink variable (ex: `permalink: about-us.html`). Didn't that work for you?

Anyway, I'm going to create a configuration option to disable permalinks, so you don't have to edit the permalink in every page.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Done!
The new version `0.7.0` has an option do disable prettyUrls. Just set `false` to this option in the `_config.js` file:

```js
const site = lume({
  prettyUrls: false
})
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
wow, that was a fast response :-)

Adding the permalink option to the pages makes no difference.

Now I'm having another problem, which is probably due to my limited knowledge of deno. I installed 0.6.0 yesterday with `deno install`. How do I update to the new version? There doesn't seem to be an update or uninstall option, and running install without a release no uses 0.6.0. Using `deno run` without a release no uses 0.6.0, and specifying 0.7.0 loads 0.7.0 `cli.js`, but then uses 0.6.0 for all other files, including `site.js`, which is the one that's changed. 😕
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body: PS permalink works in 0.6.0 if I use `.htm`. `.html` is treated in the same way as no permalink.
Comments.Author: probins

Comments.Body:
Yes, I didn't find a good way to manage and update versioning.
I've released `0.7.2` that fixes the problem with permalink.
Updating a package is the same as installing it, but you need to add the `-f` to force the installation (and override the previous installation), and `--reload` to remove the cache and reload all dependencies:

```sh
deno install -f --reload --unstable --allow-read --allow-write --allow-net https://deno.land/x/lume/cli.js
```

Note also that in `_config.js` you can return a lume instance of any version, different to what do you have installed in cli, for example:

```js
import lume from "https://deno.land/x/lume@v0.7.2/mod.js";
  
const site = lume();

export default site;
```

So, other way to update the version is just editing the version in the `_config.js` file.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
yay, that now works. Thanks for the lesson in using Deno. I see you can also use `-r` instead of `--reload`, so a bit less to type.

Moitas grazas (as you name your software with a Galician word, I assume you are galego :-))
Comments.Author: probins

I_kwDOEYAvFc5H2VtR.Title: New middleware for headers
I_kwDOEYAvFc5H2VtR.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5H2VtR.Body:
To add custom headers to some urls.
So it could support something like this: https://docs.netlify.com/routing/headers/

Comments.Author: jrson83

Comments.Body: No, I didn't start. If you want to give a try, I'll appreciate it :)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I'm guessing this is related, but it would be nice if we could cache HTTP requests. It seems there is middleware for 'no-cache' but when I push a copy of (https://github.com/lumeland/base-blog/)[https://github.com/lumeland/base-blog/] to Vercel it does not cache any HTTP requests. I'm guessing the example at (https://lumeland.github.io/base-blog/)[https://lumeland.github.io/base-blog/] has HTTP headers automatically set within the GitHub service since there does not appear to be anything in the code setting the caching header.

(I just began playing with Lume today. I love it.)
Comments.Author: kuhlaid

Comments.Body: @kuhlaid These middlewares are used only for Deno runtime environment. Vercel doesn't run Deno (just serve statically the pages build by Lume). For cache, Lume has the expires middleware, that set the `Expires` header to the http responses. I think you can achieve something similar in Vercel by configuring your headers (more info: https://vercel.com/docs/project-configuration#project-configuration/headers)
Comments.Author: oscarotero
kuhlaid
oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc5XM0n4.Title: Add webc support
I_kwDOEYAvFc5XM0n4.Author: oscarotero
I_kwDOEYAvFc5XM0n4.Body:

I_kwDOEYAvFc58s2ox.Title: add arrow indicator for url transformation
I_kwDOEYAvFc58s2ox.Author: scarf005
I_kwDOEYAvFc58s2ox.Body:
### Enter your suggestions in details:

It took me embarrassingly long time figuring out which is the original URL and which is the modified one, but making it explicit would be nice.

#### As-is

```
🔥 /index.html /posts/index.tsx

#### To-be

🔥 /posts/index.tsx -> /index.html

I_kwDOEYAvFc6ksP8E.Title: relative_urls plugin has trouble with trailing slashes in URLs
I_kwDOEYAvFc6ksP8E.Author: satgo1546
I_kwDOEYAvFc6ksP8E.Body:
### Version 2.4.3 ### Platform Linux (WSL), should also reproduce on other platforms ### What steps will reproduce the bug? In a directory with two files: - `_config.ts`: ```ts import lume from "lume/mod.ts"; import relativeUrls from "lume/plugins/relative_urls.ts"; const site = lume(); site.use(relativeUrls()); export default site; ``` - `test.md`: ```md --- url: /a.html --- [Go to index](/) ``` Run `lume`. ### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition? Always reproducible. ### What is the expected behavior? The URL `/` should be translated to `.`, so that the link leads to `index.html`. ### What do you see instead? In `_site/a.html`, the URL `/` is translated to an empty string. The link leads to `a.html` itself. ```html

Go to index

``` ### Additional information There are many other cases where the plugin fails; the above is just an example. It is problematic even if `url` is not set explicitly: Trailing slashes in URLs are removed, but `prettyUrls` (on by default) requires them. While Lume dev server handles missing trailing slashes by redirection, [not all static hosting providers do](https://www.zachleat.com/web/trailing-slash/#results-table). The plugin uses `jsr:@std/path/posix`, a module intended for POSIX filesystems. In POSIX path operations, trailing slashes are ignored. But in URLs, they are significant.

Comments.Body:
Thank you for reporting this!
I just commited a fix that will be available in the next Lume version.
If you don't want to wait to the next version, run `deno task lume upgrade --dev` to use the latest development version.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc6pX5bH.Title: Open Graph locale format set by Metas plugin is incorrect
I_kwDOEYAvFc6pX5bH.Author: yeskunall
I_kwDOEYAvFc6pX5bH.Body:
### Version

v2.5.1

### Platform

macOS 13.7.1

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

1. Set a `lang` (say, in `_data.yml`)
2. Use the `metas` plugin

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

If you set a `lang`, then it is [copied](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/main/plugins/metas.ts#L113) as-is into `og:locale`.

### What is the expected behavior?

The locale set in `og:locale` should be of the format `language_TERRITORY`. This can be confirmed by visiting the [spec](https://ogp.me/#optional).

### What do you see instead?

The `locale` is the same as the `lang` attribute.

### Additional information

_No response_

Comments.Body:
That's a fair point, but I don't know how to address it, because it conflicts with other plugins using `lang` variable (multilanguage plugin, for instance). The requirement of including always the territory is a bit annoying because there are many territories using the same language. If you have versions in `en_US` and `en_UK`, it make sense, but if you only have a single version of english, which territory should be used? And Spanish, Portuguesse and many languages have the same problem.

Any ideas?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Hey, thanks for the well-thought response. I’ll try my best to do the same.
> If you have versions in en_US and en_UK, it make sense, but if you only have a single version of english, which territory should be used? [RFC5646](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5646) requires hyphens but the Open Graph protocol (OGP) uses underscores. If we go by the RFC, then the only **required** part by the `lang` attribute is the 2 (or 3) [language subtag](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Global_attributes/lang#language_subtag). Based on that, if you do not want to specify a regional dialect, then you should simply pass `en`, or `es`, or `gl`. In setting a `lang` with a [region subtag](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Global_attributes/lang#region_subtag), I am saying this document must be parsed as if it was English as spoken in Canada (`en-CA`). But AFAIK, it doesn’t really make a difference to the browser. Maybe it does make a difference if you’re using assistive tech like a screenreader? I cannot confirm this myself. --- Can we do something like this? `ogLocale = (page.lang || site.lang || "en_US").replace("-", "_")`? If a user passes a `lang` that is valid according to the RFC (en-CA), then it will be written as `en_CA` for `og:locale`, otherwise it will be left as-is (which is the case today). The downside will be that if the `lang` is not of the format `language_TERRITORY`, then it will be marked as invalid according to OGP, which is already the case today. The above suggestion is just pseudo-code, but if you’re OK with the change, I can make a PR.
Comments.Author: yeskunall

Comments.Body:
> Can we do something like this? `ogLocale = (page.lang || site.lang || "en_US").replace("-", "_")`?

The ogLocale is not detected automatically from the page. Metas plugin get the values from the `metas` object. So you can have the language page defined in two places: the `lang` variable and the `metas.lang` variable.

```yml
lang: en
metas:
  lang: en-US
```

The `lang` variable can be used in your templates, or even the multiligual pages (for urls like `/en/whatever/`) and the `metas.lang` variable is used by metas plugin to create the `og:locale` tag.

To use the full language in both places, you can create an alias:

```yml
lang: en-US
metas:
  lang: =lang
```

This indicates that the value of `lang` is the same value of `metas.lang`. So I'm okay to make the conversion from `en-US` to `en_US` to meet the OG specifications.

You're very welcome to work on a PR, I think this replacement must be done here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/main/plugins/metas.ts#L100
Comments.Author: oscarotero

I_kwDOEYAvFc6JjL96.Title: lume().copy() is a no-op when lume({src: "docs" }) is set
I_kwDOEYAvFc6JjL96.Author: thetanil

Comments.Body:
If static folder is outside the docs folder, it isn't copied because Lume works only with files inside the `src` folder.

All paths in your document are resolved to `./docs/static` (or `./static` if src is empty), but `./docs/../static` is not allowed because it would be outside the src folder.

Maybe it should show a warning to make it more clear?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thanks for the tool, and the quick response. Yeah, some indication of a failed copy would be great I think. Also in the documentation I think it just said that copy is relative to src, so I assumed "../some/path" would be fine. Warning would be best, but some hint in the docs there would also be nice.
Comments.Author: thetanil

Comments.Body:
Agreed. 
I'll update the documentation to make it more clear. And will include a warning in the next version of Lume.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
https://lume.land/docs/configuration/copy-static-files/

2nd paragraph
Comments.Author: thetanil

I_kwDOEYAvFc5S1qqx.Title: Connect source-maps with source-files
I_kwDOEYAvFc5S1qqx.Author: binyamin
I_kwDOEYAvFc5S1qqx.Body:
### Summary
When **plugin a** creates a source-map `Page` for a given file, **plugin b** should have access to the same source-map `Page` via the given file.

### Scenario
1. User writes a file `input.scss`
2. The `sass` plugin transforms it to CSS, and adds a source-map
3. The `lightningcss` plugin minifies the CSS, and updates the source-map
4. Source-map is accurate!

### Solution
- Store the source-map on `Page` as either JSON or a string.
    - This would mean checking for a source-map when writing any file, and dealing with it accordingly.
    - This comes with the complexity of whether to write it inline or externally, and whether to add a "source-mapping URL" comment to the source's content.
- The source file's `Page` instance has a property which references a source-map `Page` instance.
    - It only needs to be one-way.
    - It should probably be a getter/setter, so we can validate it, and so it won't get in the way when debugging.

Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Agreed, the 2nd solution sounds better that the 1st.

I'm not sure whether the 3rd solution is useful. If the source-map is inline, then it is included with the content. Any further plugins that need the source-map will also be accessing the content, so they'll have what they need. For example, LightningCSS just reads any inline source-map, and parses it automatically.

Also, I just realized that the 2nd and 3rd solutions would break once a plugin changes the name of the source file.
Comments.Author: binyamin

Comments.Body:
> Also, I just realized that the 2nd and 3rd solutions would break once a plugin changes the name of the source file.

What source file do you mean? For example, if the `styles.scss` is converted to `styles.css`, with a source map, but then minified by other plugin and renamed to `styles.min.css` but the source map is not updated accordingly?

Another solution, more inline with your 1st solution is creating a plugin to generate source maps. Something like this:

```ts
site.use(sass())
  .use(postcss())
  .use(lightningcss())
  .use(sourceMaps());
```

The three first plugins would consume and save the source map in the `page.data.sourceMap` variable, and the last plugin would generate the source map page (or inline the code, depending on the configuration).

This would remove the `sourceMap` option that is already available for the first plugins. The source map will be generated always and stored in this variable. And from this unique plugin you can configure what do you want to do with it (css and javascript).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
@binyamin I have created a branch ([feat/source-map](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/tree/feat/source-map)) to test the idea the sourceMaps plugin. You can see it in this commit: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/commit/3209cb98cbca8265893b7a43a384dbf8c5b0ec8b

It's implemented in sass, esbuild, terser, postcss and lightningcss. The idea:
- All plugins can read the previous source map from `page.data.sourceMap` and replace this value with the new source map.
- I've created a helper to normalize the sourceMap urls and ensure all plugins generate the source maps in the same way: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/3209cb98cbca8265893b7a43a384dbf8c5b0ec8b/core/utils.ts#L273-L302
- The new `source_maps` plugin outputs the source map of all pages containing the `sourceMap` data: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/3209cb98cbca8265893b7a43a384dbf8c5b0ec8b/plugins/source_maps.ts
- This plugin can be configured to inline the code, generate an external file, include the source content, etc.

I've tested and seems to work fine. What do you think?
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
After some iterations, I'm confortable with the new `source_maps` plugin and merged it to master. 
It will be included in Lume 1.12.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

PR_kwDOEYAvFc5W7FSr.Title: Output HTML with PHP extension
PR_kwDOEYAvFc5W7FSr.Body:
## Description

Add PHP to pages that can be rendered with templates.
Previously only `.html` was applied to layout rendering, so I added `.php`.

## Related Issues

#460

  - [x] Document any change in the `CHANGELOG.md`.
PR_kwDOEYAvFc5W7FSr.Author: kouwtkz

Comments.Body: I don't understand. Why do you want to use PHP here? Deno cannot render PHP files.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> I don't understand. Why do you want to use PHP here? Deno cannot render PHP files.

Because I want to use it as an html template for use with PHP.

As you pointed out, Lume cannot render PHP.
I thought it would be better named "Output HTML with PHP extension", so I'll change the title.

When outputting as HTML, DocType output first hindered PHP execution, so I wanted to add it.
In fact, everything I wanted to solve after adding this was solved, so it was a necessary item for me.
I think it should be one of the options.
Comments.Author: kouwtkz

Comments.Body:
Also, this execution environment is
HTML Generator: Deno + Lume
Server: Nginx + PHP

Since it is very useful for high-performance HTML output,
I wanted to be able to use PHP extensions in the HTML output using Lume's Layout to keep the site layout consistent.
Comments.Author: kouwtkz
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Thank you for understanding.

In my case, the page is a mixture of HTML and PHP, and at present it was enough to process the "layout" in the file specified by "php", but I think the proposal to implement conversion processing as an option is good.
In that case, it might be useful to have an option to specify the extension to output to PHP or HTML if no standard file extension is specified.

Default case: `example.njk -> example/index.html`

My personal ideal when changing extensions: `Layout extension: [".html", ".php"]`
```
example.php.njk -> example.php
example.njk -> example/index.php
example/index.njk -> example/index.php
example.html.njk -> example.html
example/index.html.njk -> example/index.html
```

Also, if the layout side can handle each extension, it will be more flexible, so it would be better if the extension conversion process is before the line that proposes my this change or "renderedPages.push(page)".

It's my personal opinion, so decide for yourself whether to implement it or not.
Comments.Author: kouwtkz

Comments.Body:
If you want to implement the option in my ideal form above,
https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/01c269b171b893bc96a668bd8d84241b03a45efb/core/filesystem.ts#L82
It seems to work just to apply it here.
(When I changed `"index.html"` to `"index.php"` as a trial, I confirmed that all my ideals for changing the option extension were met.)

Looking at the variables and so on, it seems that it is not possible to obtain which extension to output from the layout side, so it would be personally nice to be able to obtain it.

Looking at the source code, I have a feeling that there are quite a few items that are processed only when it is an html file, and it seems a little difficult to deal with.
Regarding the server etc., I will debug it with my own Nginx, so I will repeat it, but I am personally satisfied if I can just apply the HTML layout with the PHP extension, so my solution may be sufficient.
Comments.Author: kouwtkz
oscarotero
kouwtkz
oscarotero
kouwtkz

Comments.Body: Great! Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0NjI4NDgwNjYx.Title: Optionally support async render functions for JSX pages
MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0NjI4NDgwNjYx.Body: It looks like engines already support async `render` functions, so I figured it would make sense to enable this functionality for JSX pages. Note that I'm using `await Promise.resolve(...)` as [according to the MDN docs](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Promise/resolve#parameters), this method can both safely wrap an existing Promise or a regular value. This means that this change should remain backward compatible for normal non-async page functions.
MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0NjI4NDgwNjYx.Author: jerluc

I've left a comment to simplify the code :)

PR_kwDOEYAvFc54Qbh7.Title: fix: stale live reload due to dropped watch events
PR_kwDOEYAvFc54Qbh7.Body:
<!--Please read the [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md)-->

## Description

<!-- Write a brief description of the changes introduced by this PR -->
While working on docs.deno.com we noticed that the page shown in the browser would often go out of sync with the actual content on disk. Debugging this further lead two a discovery of two separate bugs in the watcher + live reloading code.

When you edit a file and hit save while a previous change is still being processed it would be dropped here:

https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/abefe9fac36df830446acf679adadc64787d9893/core/watcher.ts#L87-L89

This lead to generated files lagging behind one or potentially more changes depending on how many watch events were dropped. I've resolved this by adding a queue of events that is processed in order. To avoid processing the same file more often than necessary there is a small optimisation that merges queued events if they are the same.

But with this fix in place the browser was still showing a previous version of the page. Turns out that we frequently run into the situation where a watch event is received, whilst the browser is disconnected and is in the process of being reloaded due to an earlier change. These events were dropped here:

https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/abefe9fac36df830446acf679adadc64787d9893/middlewares/reload.ts#L16-L19

To resolve this, I've added a simple "revision" tracking mechanism. When a new connection is opened the server immediately sends the current revision to the browser so that it can check if it has become stale and potentially reload the page. The initial revision is included in the HTML and passed to the `liveReload()` function as an argument.

## Before

Notice how even refreshing the browser shows the stale version.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2699c590-8731-4576-a687-9fb11f00f269

## After

Notice how the changes are all sent to the browser and it has the correct state even if you reload it. It seems like during processing the lume server is blocked from responding to the browser, otherwise the intermediate states would also be shown in it.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/09e16d73-f674-4464-a536-69cd7985e72c

## Related Issues

<!--
  Link to the issue that is fixed by this PR (if there is one)
  e.g. Fixes #1234, Addresses #1234, Related to #1234, etc.
-->

### Check List

- [x] Have you read the
      [CODE OF CONDUCT](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md)
- [x] Have you read the document
      [CONTRIBUTING](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md)
  - [x] One pull request per feature. If you want to do more than one thing,
        send multiple pull request.
  - [ ] Write tests.
  - [x] Run deno `fmt` to fix the code format before commit.
  - [x] Document any change in the `CHANGELOG.md`.

PR_kwDOEYAvFc6ZATWp.Title: Add macos-15 to test matrix
PR_kwDOEYAvFc6ZATWp.Body:
As I investigated #759, I found that `macos-latest` implies `macos-14`.

macOS 14.x  has an estimated less than 20% market share compared to macOS 15.x's 80%+.

Source: https://telemetrydeck.com/survey/apple/macOS/versions/
PR_kwDOEYAvFc6ZATWp.Author: Garciat

Comments.Body:
Oh, that's interesting!
It's weird, because I have an old macOS with version 12 (can't upgrade more) and a more modern with version 15. And the tests pass in both.
Is it because you have installed Deno with Homebrew? (I used the official script).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
That's right, I installed Deno with Homebrew. And that is indeed the core issue in #759.

But here for this PR I was just thinking about adding macOS 15.x to the test matrix since only 14.x is being tested.
I am not sure to what extent Deno's features are well-contained in terms of depending on dynamically linked libraries.
Per `otool -L $(which deno)`, it seems that some system libraries are relevant and those could vary by system version.
(Though for example `libz` is only used in Deno's nodejs-compat layer and not its core.)
Comments.Author: Garciat

Comments.Body: It's weird that `macos-latest` is not the latest version, in Github. But okay, let's add both versions for now.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

PR_kwDOEYAvFc5U_v3C.Title: Lume 2
PR_kwDOEYAvFc5U_v3C.Body:
This is a Pull Request to track all changes for Lume 2.0

- All changes are documented in [MIGRATION.md](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/v2/MIGRATION.md).
- The release of Lume 2 is planned for when Deno v2 comes out
PR_kwDOEYAvFc5U_v3C.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
May I suggest to rename the core plugin `search` to `query`? It could be useful in if people want to searching for help. I've posted about this case in discord bot ai.

I also think make `search.pages` be more explicit in searching. Like `search.pages('html')` should be `search.pages('tag=html')`. It may be longer but it can be very clear intention.
Comments.Author: ngdangtu-vn

Comments.Body:
@ngdangtu-vn I'm not sure it's worth changing the name, but open to it if other people support this idea.

About the tags, maybe you're right. `search.pages` initially only searched by tags, the possibility to search for other properties was added later. But it's a bit more complicated. `search.pages('tags=html')` searches pages whose property `tags` is `html`, but `tags` is an array that can contains multiple values. So the correct query is `tags*=html` (tags contains the value `html`). If you want to search for pages containing several tags, it's `tags*=html tags*=css tags*=js`, which is much more verbose than simply `html css js`.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Speaking of array value, the other properties could use it as well...

```js
// 1. string query
`genre=comedy`

// 1.a value prefix
`genre=!comedy` // (!) is prefix of negative not
`genre=^c` // (^) is prefix of beginning
`genre=*c` // (*) is prefix of middle
`genre=$y` // ($) is prefix of ending

// 2. array array
`genre=[comedy]`
`genre=[comedy,romantic]` // (,) means and
`genre=[comedy|romantic]` // (|) means or

// 2.a array item prefix
`genre=[!comedy,romantic]` // (!) is prefix of negative not
`genre=[^c,romantic]` // (^) is prefix of beginning
`genre=[$y,romantic]` // ($) is prefix of ending

// 3. math query
`genre=comedy` // is

// 3.a for number type only
`episode==69` // equal
`episode>69` // greater
`episode<69` // lesser
`episode>=69` // greater or equal
`episode<=69` // lesser or equal

// 3.b for array type only
`genre==[69]` // array length has 69 items
`genre>[69]` // length is greater
`genre<[69]` // length is lesser

// 4. combination query
`genre=[comedy,romantic]&theme=superhero` // (&) means and
`genre=[comedy,romantic]|theme=superhero` // (|) means or
`genre=[comedy,romantic]|(theme=superhero&episode=2)` // (parenthesis) group
```

Basically, we have 6 operators in total (relation between key & value):    
(all num only can be called math operators as well)

- `=` is : () if num is used, it will be treat like string
- `==` equal : (compare op)
- `>` greater : (compare op) 
- `<` lesser : (compare op) 
- `>=` greater or equal : (compare op) 
- `<=` lesser or equal : (compare op) 

The syntax also uses 5 prefixes (patterns) (value trait):

- `` no pattern, looking for the value
- `!` negative pattern, anything but the value
- `^` beginning pattern of looking for str starts from the value
- `$` ending pattern of looking for str finishes with the value
- `*` include pattern of looking for str contains the value

There are 2 types of wrappers:

- `[]` array value has multiple combination
  - with `=` and 5 patterns, it looks for pages with those patterns (values' relation depend on dividers, see below)
  - with compare op and 1 numeric value only, it looks for pages has the valid array length. `genre==[69]` genre property must have 69 items, for ex.
- `()` group is high priority (no nesting, it's too complicated)

And 3 dividers of wrapper (relation in wrapper):

- `,` and (for array)
- `&` and (for group)
- `|` or (for all)


Even though I'm the culprit did this proposal. I can sadly say, I don't know how to implement this shit :'( Even if you approve, it may take long time to deal with this logic. But at least, it doesn't as confuse as OR condition in the doc right?
Comments.Author: ngdangtu-vn

Comments.Body:
In my opinion, your proposal is too complex, and currently there's an `or` operator (for example `genre=comedy|romantic`).

For now, I won't make changes in `search` plugin because the current API is enough for the 99% cases and if anyone needs something more powerful, it's possible to create a tailored solution.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thank you!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

PR_kwDOEYAvFc6KzK0M.Title: middlewares/reload: use site's location pathname as websocket basepath
PR_kwDOEYAvFc6KzK0M.Body:
## Description

I know that this is very much an edge case but.. I noticed that the websocket client code in the hot reload module does not honor the `location` path.

For example: when I have `--location=https://mysite/subpath/`, then the websocket client connects to `wss://mysite/` and not `wss://mysite/subpath/` as I'd expect.

I ran into this while using Apache as a proxy to the Lume dev server like so:
```
RewriteEngine on

RewriteCond %{HTTP:Upgrade} websocket [NC]
RewriteCond %{HTTP:Connection} upgrade [NC]
RewriteRule ^/?lume/(.*)$ "ws://127.0.0.1:3000/$1" [P,L]

RewriteRule ^/?lume/(.*)$ "http://127.0.0.1:3000/$1" [P,L]
```
And running Lume like so:
```
deno task serve --location="https://mysite.com/lume/" --hostname=localhost --port=3000
```
For the time being, I just do the following. Though, it is limiting if I tried to develop more than one app at a time.
```
RewriteRule ^/?(.*) "ws://localhost:3000/$1" [P,L]
```
I understand that the dev server is for local use only and that it rightly expects to be accessed via `http://localhost:3000/` (root path) for 99% of use cases. But the `serve` command _does_ accept the `--location` argument and it worked well with the proxying above. It's just the websocket part that broke my expectations.

wdyt?

- `~= `to `*=`: Obviously.
- `ALL` TO `&=`: `&` is like AND operator, so makes sense because we want all values are present
- `SOME` TO `|=`: `|` is like OR operator, so makes sense because only one match is required.

PR_kwDOEYAvFc4sMyiI.Title: Track files' dependencies when watching filesystem
PR_kwDOEYAvFc4sMyiI.Body:
This is implemented by changing `loader/module.ts`, adding a step before the module to be loaded is actually evaluated. We use `Deno.emit()` to bundle the module with all it's dependencies, this way Deno cache will be bypased and we'll always load the latest version from disk.

`Deno.emit()` also returns source maps for the generated bundle. We use this information to extract the dependencies of the module, this way when we detect one of such dependencies has changed we know we need to also load the dependant module.
PR_kwDOEYAvFc4sMyiI.Author: amatiasq

Comments.Author: oscarotero
amatiasq

Comments.Body:
I know people using Lume for big sites with thousand of pages, and they use Typescript to create several pages. So, performance is really important.

I'm agree that complicating the config with two loaders and let the user to decide which one use is not a good from DX. But I'd like to test this approach with real sites and give some time before replace the current loader with the new one. Build a site with 100 pages would create 100 bundles, and I want to make sure this is the only way to do it.

Perhaps we could create a new plugin for that? Something like:

```js
import modulePages from "lume/plugins/module_pages.ts";
site.use(modulePages());
```

And this plugin would load, for example, pages with the `.page.js` and `.page.ts` extensions (so they don't conflict with the current `.tmpl.js` and `.tmpl.ts` extensions that use the current loader. This allows to test properly this, and even use it in real sites without introduce breaking changes or big drops in performance of the existing sites.

Things to consider:

- I'm not confortable with the idea of files exporting their dependencies (this is the reason of [why this tests fails](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/tests/loaders.test.ts#L38)). The list of dependencies of a file is something that should be handled internally by Lume and shouldn't affect to the data used to build the site.
- As an idea, maybe the dependencies and cache could be stored globally (instead in the Source instance), because they are referring to unique files, using the full path, so I don't see any conflict here, even if there are multiple instances of Sites. This would simplify a lot the things because the cache would be something defined in the loader module:
```js
const cachedDependants = new Map();

export default function loader (path: string) {
    // ...
}
```
- In the [bundler plugin](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/bundler.ts) I download all dependencies and save them in the `sources` variable. Then, I pass this variable to the bundler [here](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/bundler.ts#L95), so the bundler don't need to download any code, because the content of all files is already downloaded. This ensures that, two entry modules sharing the same dependencies, will use the same code (without download it twice), and only update the files that really have changed. Maybe it can help to improve performance.
- One problem that I see with this approach is that every page is executed isolately, so they never can share the same module. For example, imagine a page with this code:
```js
import config from "./config.js";
config.add("foo");
```
This code will die when the page is loaded, so it's not possible to recover this value from other page.

The same would happen for modules that connect to external apis, like this real example: https://github.com/tarugoconf/tarugo21/blob/master/_data/eventbrite.js#L64

This site use the eventbrite API to retrieve data about sold tickets. Two pages that imports this file would make two API calls.

This and other undetected problems can happen. This is why I'd like to implement this carefully.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
The idea of exposing dependencies was to allow other kind of loaders to also provide dependencies knowing Lume would watch them.

Anyway, focusing on the blocker:

> this will not work without affecting lume's internals because of `Source.#cache`

The issue is that we can't invalidate the cache from outside `Source`. One way to do that, as you propose, is to move the cache somewhere else.

If we move them to the loaders then all loaders will have to duplicate cache implementation and their public API who implement this will have to change:

```ts
type OldLoader = (path: string) => Promise<Data>;
type NewLoader = (site: Site) => (path: string) => Promise<Data>;
```

In order to listen (only once, since we can't remove listeners) to `beforeBuild` and `beforeUpdate` (the two events where the cache is manipulated in the [current implementation](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/source.ts#L31-L42)). This is a breaking change so I don't think that's an option.

Another, more senseful, option would be to keep the cache in `Source` and expose methods to clear the cache.

This discussion is no more about tracking dependencies, which is clear to me now will not make it to the core, but about invalidating internal cache so I'll close this PR and open an issue instead.
Comments.Author: amatiasq

PR_kwDOEYAvFc47_fXH.Title: Warn user when empty files are skipped
PR_kwDOEYAvFc47_fXH.Body:
## What?

Logs a warning to tell the user when a file is being skipped from the build because it's empty.


## Why?

I was looking around the code for this bit of functionality and noticed that there was an open issue to add some logging. (Closes #180)

The issue was opened a while ago, so I don't know if it's still something you want to add. But because it was a small change I thought I might as well just submit a PR. Feel free to reject it if the issue isn't relevant anymore!


## Testing

This PR shouldn't affect functionality, and it doesn't seem like logging messages are something that should have unit tests attached to it. But if it's desirable to add a test here then I'm happy to do so.

On this topic, I was wondering if you have any tips on how to run a locally modified version of lume? I had a quick attempt but I was hitting some issues; I'm fairly new to Deno so I imagine that I'm missing something about how dependencies are resolved..
PR_kwDOEYAvFc47_fXH.Author: bryophyta

Comments.Body:
Hey, thanks for this!

It looks good to me. Just one thing: there's another place to place this warning. Just before executing the preprocessors:
https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/core/site.ts#L520-L523

>  I was wondering if you have any tips on how to run a locally modified version of lume?

You can install the local cloned repository by executing `deno task install`, a task defined here: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/deno.json#L5
Once installed, the `lume` command points to your local repository.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Thanks for the tips! For some reason I'm having trouble with installing lume (I usually just run `ci.ts` using Deno). The install works, but then my shell can't find `lume` in the path. However, it seems quite likely that this is due to some config issue on my end. When I get the chance I'll try to replicate this more carefully and write up an issue if it's still a problem.

I've updated the branch with a warning at the `site.ts` skip step, as you suggested. Hope it all looks alright. When I was testing it locally I spotted something that I think is a small bug in the `url` logic, but I'll open this as a separate issue.
Comments.Author: bryophyta
oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> The install works, but then my shell can't find lume in the path

It happens in some environments, that the PATH is not updated automatically on install Deno. This script may help (from the [Deno manual](https://deno.land/manual/tools/script_installer#script-installer)):
```
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.deno/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
```
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Thank you!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> The reason `source file is empty` doesn't look correct to me. I think `output content is empty` is more accurated, because the source file might don't be empty, but the generated output does.

Sorry for the late reply! This makes a lot of sense to me, I've opened #224 to fix it.

---

Thanks for this! I'll look into that 👍

PR_kwDOEYAvFc5uean6.Title: feat(metas): add `other` tags field
PR_kwDOEYAvFc5uean6.Body:
<!--Please read the [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md)-->

## Description

<!-- Write a brief description of the changes introduced by this PR -->

This PR adds the possibilty to add custom meta tags via the `metas` plugin.  It is possible via the `other` attribute in the metas field.

An code example:
```yaml
metas:
  title: "My great blog post"
  other:
    "twitter:label1": Reading time
    "twitter:data1": 1 minute
```

Which results in:
```html
<meta name="twitter:label1" content="Reading time">
<meta name="twitter:data1" content="1 minute">
```

The background of this PR was adding `twitter:label1` and `twitter:data1` meta tags to use the preview features that are now available to build previews with. It uses the `twitter` prefix but is also available on e.g. Slack.

Examples:

### X
[![levelsio on X](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/C4DRmwSUEAA4ItJ?format=jpg&name=medium)](https://twitter.com/levelsio/status/828892835172671489?lang=en)

See `Nomad Score` and `Nomad Cost` here.

### Slack
[![Add support for twitter label meta](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1614415/222043808-e486f5e5-b0f1-40d6-bd3c-3b5599ae0b38.png)](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/46603)

From an implementation point of view I took the way that NextJS does it to make them able to add them via the `other` attribute in the metadata fields. See [this discussion](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/46603)

I'm open for feedback to get this feature landed ☺️ 

## Related Issues

<!--
  Link to the issue that is fixed by this PR (if there is one)
  e.g. Fixes #1234, Addresses #1234, Related to #1234, etc.
-->

none - yet

### Check List

- [x] Have you read the
      [CODE OF CONDUCT](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md)
- [x] Have you read the document
      [CONTRIBUTING](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md)
  - [x] One pull request per feature. If you want to do more than one thing,
        send multiple pull request.
  - [x] Write tests.
  - [x] Run deno `fmt` to fix the code format before commit.
  - [x] Document any change in the `CHANGELOG.md`.
PR_kwDOEYAvFc5uean6.Author: niklasmtj

Comments.Body: After having another look on the `getDataValue` function I think we have to extend it to be able to work with arrays. Right now I'm not supporting the possibilty to use something like `=others`. But I was not 100% how the best way to implement would look like.
Comments.Author: niklasmtj

Comments.Body:
Thank! Your implementation looks great! The only problem is the `metas` object has one more level of depth that will cause merging issues.

For example, if in the root `_data.yml` file you have some defaults:

```yml
metas:
  other:
    "twitter:label1": Reading time
```
And you want to customize the value in a nested _data file:

```yml
metas:
  other:
    "twitter:data1": 1 minute
```

The complete `other` object will be overrided, losing the `twitter:label1` key, because Lume only merges [the first level of the object](https://lume.land/docs/core/merged-keys/#object-mode-merging).

I can think of different solutions:

1. Allow extra keys in the root of the `metas` object. Any key different to the standard keys will be added as is.

```yml
metas:
  title: "My great blog post"
  "twitter:label1": Reading time
  "twitter:data1": 1 minute
```

2. Or create a new mode to merge objects recursively. For example `recursiveObject` and assign this mode by default to the `metas` key.

I guess the solution 1 is the most simple to implement, but I would like to hear your opinion.
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Oh thanks for the feedback. This totally makes sense! I forgot about the merging "only" on the first level. 

I think extending the `MetaData` with something like 

```ts
interface MetaData {
...
   [key: string]: string
}
```

To get it to accept dynamic keys in the type. Based on [the stack overflow answer](https://stackoverflow.com/a/39257653). And then maybe destructure the incoming object to get the non fixed defined keys. 

I have to try it in the next few days that might be a good idea. Sounds like a first plan to start with. Thanks!
Comments.Author: niklasmtj

Comments.Body:
@niklasmtj Hey there! Have you been able to make any progress in this? No problem if you need more time, just want to know it because I want to release a new Lume version soon and I'd like to include this feature. Or let me know if you are not going to work on this (because reasons) so I can continue your work.
Thanks!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
> @niklasmtj Hey there! Have you been able to make any progress in this? No problem if you need more time, just want to know it because I want to release a new Lume version soon and I'd like to include this feature. Or let me know if you are not going to work on this (because reasons) so I can continue your work. Thanks!

Hey sorry, had a busy week. We can also collaborate on this! So if you have an idea on how to implement it easily, go forward :)
Comments.Author: niklasmtj

Comments.Body:
@niklasmtj I cannot commit to your PR (obviously) so created this new branch and PR #608 with your commits and additional changes to make the `metas` object flat.
So I'm closing this. Thanks so much!
Comments.Author: oscarotero

MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0NTY5MTMzMzM2.Title: Add slug to page data
MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0NTY5MTMzMzM2.Body:
Add the page's "slug" to the page data.

The slug is intended to be the last segment in the url of the page.
MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0NTY5MTMzMzM2.Author: JordanShurmer

Comments.Body: Why do you need it? (A easier way to get it is with `basename(page.data.url)`).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
I've used `slug`  in other frameworks (e.g. 11ty) for various things.

One use I still have is in building out a section's index page. I auto-tag all the pages in directory with the slug of the directory. Then the index-page.njk template can do `search.page(slug)`
Comments.Author: JordanShurmer

Comments.Body: I used `page.data.url` at first - seemed like it was always `index` due to the **prettyUrls** logic.
Comments.Author: JordanShurmer

Comments.Body:
Note that I've improved the `search` functionality in the last version. Take a look: https://lumeland.github.io/creating-pages/searching/ and now it can search not only by tags but any other field.

To get all pages in a directory, you can do this:

- Add a `_data.yml` file in the directory with any field name that you like to use to select, for example `section: folder-name`
- Then you can search all pages with `search.pages("section=folder-name")`

Another way:

- Simply search pages whose urls starts with something: `search.pages("url^=/folder-name/")`
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body:
Ok. I'll take one of those approaches for this.

Feel free to close this PR :)
Comments.Author: JordanShurmer

Comments.Body: Ok, I close this. Anyway, I will think in a way to create permalinks dinamically (for example, with the title slugged)
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Extensions on Nunjucks can't be passed by regular options, they need to be added by `addExtension`, so we add a new `extensions` option and register them.

PR_kwDOEYAvFc6hk_aH.Title: fix date handling in sheets plugin
PR_kwDOEYAvFc6hk_aH.Body:
## Description

I noticed an issue with handling of dates in CSV files in the sheets plugin. If the `cellDates` option was set, and a date was provided (with no timezone information) the date was off by one under daylight savings (may be because I'm in GMT which is close to UTC). The internal representation seems to be UTC, and setting `UTC: true` on the `sheet_to_json` function fixes the issue. I don't think this should cause issues with other formats.

MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0Njc2NjY2NTgy.Title: Make includes directory configurable
MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0Njc2NjY2NTgy.Body:
This implements the possibility of configuring the includes directory proposed in #111:

- <del>Adds a new `--includes` CLI option.</del>
- Adds `site.options.includes` and a `includes` option for `eta`, `nunjucks` and `pug`.
- Adds a `options` option for `pug`.
- Adds `site.includes()`, similar to `site.src()`.
- Replaces the hard coded include paths with `site.includes()`.

@oscarotero, what do you think? Do we need this?

We should also decide what should happen if the specified includes directory doesn’t start with `_` (or `.`). Should we throw an error or just ignore the directory (like `site.ignore(site.includes())`)?
MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0Njc2NjY2NTgy.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
Nice approach. One thing that I have thought about was including the possibility to configure different includes. For example, it may be more practical for some users if the css files are in `/_css` instead `_includes/css`, and the same for layouts, etc. This requires to have this option at the plugin level, not site level. For instance:

```js
site.use(postcss({ includes: "_css" });
```

In fact, it's something already [implemented for postcss](https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/plugins/postcss.js#L29), but not other plugins like nunjucks, eta or pug.

I think this is a more flexible solution. The plugins can have the `_includes` folder by default (and maybe this folder can be configured in the site, using your changes here), but with the possibility to change them.

To me, the `--includes` argument in CLI is a bit excesive, I can't think of a use case for that.

About the directories that doesn't start with `_` or `.`, good point.
- I think `site.ignore(site.includes())` is an easy and clean solution. 
- But, in the other hand, the user may not want to ignore this folder for any reason:
  - Because the includes files is in a subdirectory
  - Because she want to reuse a page as a template (crazy idea)
  - Because mixes pages and layouts in the same folder, and use `_` to ignore the layouts. For example:
    ```
    pages/
      page-1.md
      page-2.njk
      _layout.njk
    ```
So, maybe the best approach is doing nothing? Just include in the documentation a text indicating that the includes folder/files should be ignored someway.


What do you think?
Comments.Author: oscarotero
valtlai

Comments.Body:
Probably, I'd create the nunjucks instance out of the NunjucksEngine constructor. I like the idea of having the engines as much light as possible, so they can be reused with a custom instance if you want (for example, creating your different nunjucks plugin but reusing the same engine).

This is something that I did with markdown engine: https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/master/engines/markdown.js (the markdown-it instance is created in the plugin file and passed to the engine in the constructor).
Comments.Author: oscarotero

Comments.Body: Okay, I’ll try that later, but feel free to push commit(s) here if you like.
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body:
Thanks for those commits!

Added a plugin-level `includes` option for the `nunjucks` and `eta` plugins, but not really for `pug`, because the include path is set in the engine instead of the plugin.
Comments.Author: valtlai

Comments.Body: Nice! I just commited a change to configure the pug engine, including the basedir (includes) from the plugin.
Comments.Author: oscarotero
